## Thread: TinyCobol and OpenCobol — 17 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-c4defbcfb6)._

#### ↳ Re: TinyCobol and OpenCobol

- **From:** Billy O'Connor <billyoc@linuxfromscratch.org>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T03:53:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81k7hpiw9y.fsf@dps10.oconnoronline.net>`
- **References:** `<1041367537@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`

```
"Vince Coen" <Vince_Coen@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.org> writes:

> Hello All!
> 
> Has anyone installed these products on Mandrake v9.0?
> 
> If so, How?

tinyCOBOL
Prerequistes are db-4.0.14.

http://umn.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/tiny-cobol/tinycobol-0.59.tar.gz

You'll need the gcc rpms installed, gcc-cpp-3.2-1mdk, gcc-3.2-1mdk and
libgcc1-3.2-1mdk.  Note that there may be glibc-devel dependencies
here, the urpmi utility will detail them if so.

Extract the tarball, and run:
./configure --prefix=/usr &&
make &&
make install


OpenCOBOL
Prerequisites are gmp(The GNU Multi-precision library)
http://twtelecom.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/open-cobol/open-cobol-0.10.tar.gz
building is the same as tinyCOBOL.

Enjoy!
```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
> A. Top posting.
>
> Q. What's the biggest irritant in newsgoups?

I'd say excess quoted material. Top-posting is just a special case. If 
the entire previous text is present, it's an irritant, top or bottom. 
If anything, bottom-posting without trimming the quote is worse than 
top-posting. Heresy, I know.

Edward
```

- **References:** `<1041367537@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn> <QdrR9.549830$WL3.146824@rwcrnsc54> <81fzs9n826.fsf@dps10.oconnoronline.net> <av5pgk$lrj$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <v56dnZ1S7K6jbYujXTWckA@giganews.com>`

On  4-Jan-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:


> Q. What's the biggest irritant in newsgoups?
>
> A. Top posting.


Posting according to some old standard instead of reading and editing the post
to facilitate communication.  Sometimes top posting communicates best and most
quickly, sometimes bottom posting, sometimes mid-posting.

If you have a reason to make a long post - PLEASE top post so that people don't
have to page down to find out what new was added.  Otherwise, lots of people
will just skip over your post.

If you are going to add a one-line reply to a one-line reply - edit the messages
to make it obvious to which you are replying to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T04:57:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net...
> It looks to me as if you recognize a reason NOT to use periods, but want
to keep
> using them anyway.  I'm part of the "don't code 'em if you don't need 'em"
camp,
> and since you never need 'em except at the end of a procedure (paragraph
or
> section), I recommend not using periods anywhere except there.
>
> I seriously doubt if there is a COBOL programmer anywhere who hasn't seen
some
> of the following:
> 1.  A period that caused an logic error.
> 2.  A missing period that caused a logic error.
> 3.  A set of imperative statements which all ended with periods that had
to be
> made subordinate to a conditional (IF, EVALUATE, PERFORM ... UNTIL, etc.)
> statement, meaning that all of the periods had to be removed.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> providing the scope terminators.  In addition, they gave us more freedom
to code
> what the program requires, without artifical solutions such as a PERFORM
that
> moves code out of line, or writing the same statement twice, etc.
>
> Not to take full advantage of the improved language is just like throwing
small
> change on the ground, because it is too much trouble to carry it around.
> Eventually, you've cost yourself some real money.
>

    I do use scope terminators.  You can get into trouble either way,
whether you use one period only per paragraph or if you jam one in
anywhere you can make one fit.

    My point was that I do not understand why I should consider
removing all periods but one as an end in itself. I have personally
experienced trouble with go to's and perform thru's.  Debugging problems
here can be especially hard when the go to in question is used only after an
error.  But I can see pro's and con's for both one period and many period
styles.

    Simple example - If's nested six deep, but only five END-IF'S.  If you
put a period after the fifth end-if, there is no error.  Without the period,
all statements up to the end of the paragraph are erroneously part of the
if.

    Similar examples exist for the opposite problem.

    Oddly enough, there can be an advantage to using both scope terminators
and periods.  Extra periods can result in compiler warnings.  The compiler
is
much better at spotting that sort of thing than any person.

    Maybe the best bet would be a compromise.  Cut back on routine
periods, but continue to use them after the final END in a sentence.




> Russell Styles wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > On 30-Dec-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:
> > >
> > > > None of the reasons listed above are sufficient (IMHO) to do a
"major"
> > > > rewrite of an existing/functioning program or application.  On the
other
> > > > hand, I *do* view them as sufficient reasons to ENCOURAGE (not
require)
> > a
> > > > change to a "single period per paragraph style of coding with NO
"next
> > > > sentences" included in new programs and new "major" insertions of
> > code/logic
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

you

SENTENCE

contrary.
> > >

that

> > > I remember trying to decide whether an impact printer had a comma or a
> > period -
> > > and whether the period was in column 72 or 73 - but now we can use
END-IF
> > and it

and

> > > NEXT-SENTENCE, I don't see that this makes a significant difference.
If

you

with

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T10:30:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA3873CC048C82301849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net> <ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03>`

```
On Tue, 31 Dec 2002 23:57:46 -0500, Russell Styles wrote
> My point was that I do not understand why I should consider
> removing all periods but one as an end in itself.

I don't see it as an end in itself. It's part of a package of teaching 
programmers to use scope terminators consistently rather than depending 
on periods to end scope.

>     Simple example - If's nested six deep, but only five END-IF'S.  If you
> put a period after the fifth end-if, there is no error.

I strongly disagree with your conclusion. "There is no error" ONLY if 
the missing END-IF is the LAST one. But if I omit the last END-IF, it 
will stand out because of the prominent gap in the code, and it's very 
likely that I'll catch it by eye, and so adding the period didn't buy 
me anything. (Of course, I have all the code indented properly. I also 
leave a blank line after an outer level scope terminator, making its 
absence even more prominent.)

OTOH if I omit an inner END-IF, then it's far less obvious to the eye. 
There's generally code following the missing END-IF at the same 
indentation level, and code farther down to the left. I'm a lot less 
likely to see the missing END-IF. This is not just theoretical; I know 
this from experience.

And if I omit an inner END-IF and then later close it with a period, 
then I have almost certainly introduced an error. The period didn't fix 
the error, but rather helped to hide it (by limiting the scope of the 
bug and thus making it less likely to be detected on any given test).

Now again, I agree that omitting the period doesn't solve this problem. 
Only using a tool which requires matching scope terminators for all 
conditionals solves it. But programmers who are accustomed to using 
scope terminators consistently are much more likely to want, use, ask 
for, insist on, a tool to do this. And are also more likely to catch a 
missing END-IF by eye. Abjuring excess periods is just a part of the 
whole package.

The more I write about this, the more I want all my programs 
automatically checked every time I compile ...

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T11:46:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301011146.4f3fb55d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net> <ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03> <01HW.BA3873CC048C82301849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote

> And if I omit an inner END-IF and then later close it with a period, 
> then I have almost certainly introduced an error. The period didn't fix 
> the error, but rather helped to hide it (by limiting the scope of the 
> bug and thus making it less likely to be detected on any given test).

Actually missing END-IFs don't need to be a bug, it depends on whether
ELSE is used.

      IF ( a )
          IF ( b )
              do x
          ELSE
              do y
      ELSE
          do z
      END-IF

There is no error, all scopes are correctly terminated, a full stop
makes no difference.  This is why compilers cannot warn about
'missing' END-IFs.
```

Richard wrote:
> Actually missing END-IFs don't need to be a bug, it depends on whether
> ELSE is used.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'missing' END-IFs.

Perhaps, but it certainly is clearer as follows:

        IF ( a )
            IF ( b )
                do x
            ELSE
                do y
----->     end-if
        ELSE
            do z
        END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T21:14:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av0apu$rsd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net> <ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03> <01HW.BA3873CC048C82301849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0301011146.4f3fb55d@posting.google.com> <3E134ED0.4040904@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert,
  Although I agree with you that adding the "optional" END-IF *might* make
it clearer to some/most maintenance programmers, it is also true that if
either
   "do x"
     or
   "do y"

were replaced by "Next Sentence" - adding the End-if would change conforming
(and portable) source code to non-conforming (and potentially non-portable)
source code.  If the logic actually DID want to "jump" to a period, this
would be a problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T23:56:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA39309304B8DF921849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net> <ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03> <01HW.BA3873CC048C82301849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0301011146.4f3fb55d@posting.google.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Jan 2003 14:46:48 -0500, Richard wrote
> Actually missing END-IFs don't need to be a bug, it depends on whether
> ELSE is used.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>       END-IF

After "do y", insert

     END-IF
     do q

Now dropping the END-IF introduces an error. I agree that a missing 
END-IF won't *always* introduce a bug, but it will introduce a bug far 
too often for my comfort.

> There is no error, all scopes are correctly terminated, a full stop
> makes no difference.  This is why compilers cannot warn about
> 'missing' END-IFs.

Compilers cannot warn about missing END-IF in general because COBOL85 
never requires you to use END-IF. This feature can only be an option 
invoked by a user who wants to program in a subset of COBOL85 that is 
restricted for safety. But if the user has requested to be warned when 
exceeding this subset, then the compiler certainly can warn for every 
END-IF that is missing according to the subset definition.

Safety-restricted subsets have a long history, and can be valuable 
tools.

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T06:08:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wHQQ9.593813$QZ.86145@sccrnsc02>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T00:54:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA3A8FBA050B67D71849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03> <3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net> <ezuQ9.382191$pN3.43096@sccrnsc03> <01HW.BA3873CC048C82301849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uIQQ9.407988$GR5.130699@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <av1m9l$bc8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Thu, 2 Jan 2003 10:36:07 -0500, Howard Brazee wrote
> Would you want it for all commands that can have an END?  Or would 
> it be better to only flag missing END IF's? 

It should flag the missing scope terminator for all *conditionals*.

I place the scope terminators in four categories:

1) PERFORM

2) primary conditionals: IF, EVALUATE, SEARCH

3a) statements with secondary conditional(s), for example READ AT END, 
STRING ON OVERFLOW, etc.

3b) statements which can have secondary conditionals but as written do 
not -- for example, READ with neither AT END nor NOT AT END.

PERFORM (1) is not an issue, as there is never an optional END-PERFORM.

Primary conditionals (2) must always include the scope terminator in my 
safer subset.

Statements with secondary conditionals (3a) must always include the 
scope terminator in my safer subset.

Statements without conditionals (including 3b) should not include the 
scope terminator. However, they may include the scope terminator, due 
to the odd cases which require either a scope terminator or some other 
syntactic de-fuzz to avoid ambiguities. (I'm tempted to say never 
include the scope terminator, and use a trivial PERFORM/END-PERFORM to 
disambiguate when necessary. But I haven't studied this cranny 
carefully enough to state that this is always a viable alternative.)

The distinction between 3a and 3b allows us to make maximum use of 
scope terminators without absurdities like including END-ADD for every 
simple ADD, a practice which would make most programmer revolt.

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T05:24:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FYuQ9.186182$qF3.12212@sccrnsc04>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <austek$i23$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:austek$i23$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> > And compilers that think so as well ... I only learned a couple of days
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is only an extension when used in conjunction with an END-IF.   Without
an
> END-IF, NEXT SENTENCE has a specific meaning which says go to the next
period.
> I don't like changing its meaning when used with an END-IF.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The skip-to-next-period option is, to me, a recipe for hard-to-read
code.
>
> Agreed.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Me too.  Periods sometimes allow the compiler to notice when you have
screwed up
> the number of terminators.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> terminators and eschew NEXT SENTENCE.

    Not if you leave out an END.  The compiler spots extra ENDs easily,
but does not help with missing ENDs. Every statement after the missing
END up to the next period becomes part of the IF.


>
> > I've taught a few classes, and given a few talks, on the topic "COBOL85
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Periods caused problems when they were the only terminators for
conditions.
> Ask the audience whether those conditions were with condition terminators.
I
> bet none of them were.
>
> The "solution" of eliminating the periods misses the point.   The true
solution
> is to add END terminators.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>

> On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 12:14:52 -0500, William M. Klein wrote

*is*
> > the same thing as a "continue" with an End-IF (or End-Search).
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> delimiters for conditionals and for incorrect indentation.

    If we could get the compiler to generate a syntax error whenever
we leave out a scope delimiter, than I would agree that one period per
paragraph would be a genuine good thing.

    Some way to enforce indention would be nice as well.  Perhaps
an editor enhancement, or a utility to do this for us.  I know of one
utility (pretty print)  that is supposed to do this, but whenever it finds
some
source code that it does not recognize (The ON verb for example),
it deletes that offending code.

Why can't it just leave it alone.


>
> On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 14:58:51 -0500, Howard Brazee wrote
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs)
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T17:37:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ca5032.0301011737.6078a3f9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Some comments on WHY (personally) I would suggest a style with minimal
> periods and Next Sentence phrases:

What about a future COBOL standard that places the period in the
archaic category and requires the use of END-whatever?  I suspect that
some programs would need some major rewriting, other programs would
get by with a search and delete.

Just a thought ... or a hallucination ...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T21:15:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av0asj$dc7$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <74ca5032.0301011737.6078a3f9@posting.google.com>`

```
Discussed some time last year, the requirement of a period (or a *new*
construct such as "END-PROCEDURE") is required to tell where each
paragraph/section ends and where the next one begins (once you get rid of
the A-/B-margin restrictions - as they have been removed in the 2002
Standard)
```

## Thread: Looking for cobol assistance — 11 msgs

### Looking for cobol assistance

- **From:** "\\(www.teknosoluzioni.it\\)" <tatife@tin.it>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T10:13:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qbzQ9.55855$R45.2578585@news2.tin.it>`

```
Dear friend,

I am a person of an Italian small company.

I use from about 7-8 years one cobol 45 program that run very right, for the
work of my company;
the program was made only for me by a good boy that now is not here.

My problem is this: all the number price of the program was made without
"dot" (0000000) because with the old money not necessary
(example 100000 lire) ; now there is the ? Euro and the same example is
51,16 Euro.
For this I use again the old money because my program is very important for
me.

I wanted a good person that can modify my program for the use of the "
00000,00"

or , pheraps no possible, that is able to make a program that change all my
video result money in the same result   : 1936,27  (conversion with Euro) .

This my necessity is very very  important for me.

I hope for your answer.

Many thanks for time and consideration.

 Best regards
Celestino Costa

Via Martiri della Libert� 71 Massa Lombarda 48024 Ra Italy
www.costacelestino.it
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for cobol assistance

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T13:27:46+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v4k51v0nl6641qdj22p1v6do1ffk2tmp7k@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qbzQ9.55855$R45.2578585@news2.tin.it>`

```
On Wed, 01 Jan 2003 10:13:42 GMT "\(www.teknosoluzioni.it\)" <tatife@tin.it>
wrote:

:>I am a person of an Italian small company.

:>I use from about 7-8 years one cobol 45 program that run very right, for the
:>work of my company;
:>the program was made only for me by a good boy that now is not here.

:>My problem is this: all the number price of the program was made without
:>"dot" (0000000) because with the old money not necessary
:>(example 100000 lire) ; now there is the ? Euro and the same example is
:>51,16 Euro.
:>For this I use again the old money because my program is very important for
:>me.

:>I wanted a good person that can modify my program for the use of the "
:>00000,00"

Change the PICTURE to 999999,99

Note that this will take one more print column (though with the use of the
Euro you may have less digits).

:>or , pheraps no possible, that is able to make a program that change all my
:>video result money in the same result   : 1936,27  (conversion with Euro) .

How would you know the fields? If they have a PICTURE with $ (or whatever the
Italian equivalent is) you could write a program or script to change them.
```

```
Thane,

I appreciate your comments.  Too often people only see the 
new technology as the "be all to end all" for a project and 
they forget that other experiences can contribute to the 
success of a system.  New technology is not hard to learn, 
but "having been there and done that" takes years.  Not 
everyone can think logically and critically right out of 
school, and that can be more important to the success of a 
project than knowing .asp, etc.

Robert

## Thread: Strange Cobol-behaviour — 2 this month / 147 total

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-a10085c4b3)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T13:20:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v16cr26ibu3t9c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212301138.5d8271ed@posting.google.com> <v11skdri4mre18@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212312116.3a31141c@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212312116.3a31141c@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> also uses setting record area to high-values and tests on this.

I started programming during the transition to structured
programming (1976), after a few years repairing computer
hardware. (I actually debugged a program from a core dump
in 1974. The programmer used an alphanumeric key field
as a disk address. The hardware return error status, so I
got the 3am call.)

The local college was not yet teaching SP, no one, in the
organization (where I worked as a third shift operator), was
using SP and the existing programs had both GO TO EOJ
and MOVE HIGH-VALUES. As a third shift operator, I was
not writing applications, though I did write some utilities and
and did some systems programming, like: patch the IBM
utility, POWER, to fix an error with a channel command;
transferred files from a System/3 disk pack to tape on a S/360
(my hardware experience was invaluable, none of the
programmers knew it was even possible); moved a major
application from one DOS configuration to another, to keep
from rebooting the S/360; and cut hours off a tape-to-tape
conversion. Then, I got my first junior programmer assignment!
Ahh, to be young and in love ... with computing. But, I digress.

During 1976 and 1977, I was reading about SP and, in
November 1977, I transferred to a sister company where
I began as an embedded systems programmer, programming
a Motorola 6800 in assembler. It was here that I first applied
SP using branch and jump instructions. In 1982, I used DRI's
Pascal/MT+ and Microsoft's assembler, on CP/M, to build an
embedded program for an S-100, Z-80 box that served as
a convertor for 2780 emulation to "my" terminals.

In November 1983, I got my first IBM PC. In 1984, I bought
a number of languages, including Micro Focus Personal
COBOL V1.0 for my own use. The company started with
RM/COBOL, then switched to Micro Focus Professional
COBOL V1.1, due to limitation in RM. Total data could not
exceed 58K, as I recall. All COBOL development was done
by another group within the company. In 1988, that group,
actually a separate company, walked out, in a dispute over
profit sharing, six programmers were brought in to save the
company. I also worked with them. Within two years, they
were all gone and I inherited the system, alone. 58K source
with over 7K GO TO statements. "Assembler in COBOL."

Over the years, in this newsgroup, I have mentioned most
of the above. My purpose in restating it is to show that my
experience has been atypical for a COBOL programmer.

You are mostly right about what the literature shows (more later).
I've seen similar examples. I am influenced by the literature; but,
I almost never make my decisions based soley upon what others
say or do. If I like something, I use it; even it isn't pretty or accepted.

> High-values to record areas is the most useful mechanism in such
> things as merging and selecting part of the file, in many cases this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> EoF Flag setting and testing.

I have seen this; but, in the rare cases where I have done
merging, I used MERGE with RETURN AT END EXIT PERFORM;
or SORT with INPUT PROCEDURE: READ file-1 AT END
EXIT PERFORM, READ file-2 AT END EXIT PERFORM, and
OUTPUT PROCEDURE with RETURN AT END EXIT PERFORM.

For selecting part of a file, it has been to select detail for a
single partial key, placing the detail into a table: after a START
on a partial-detail-key & LOW-VALUES (or ZEROES),

PERFORM UNTIL partial-detail-key > comparable-key
    READ detail-file NEXT
     AT END EXIT PERFORM
    END-READ
    *> insert record into table
END-PERFORM
.
There is one batch process in the system that matches detail
transactions with a master and does use HIGH-VALUES.
I never changed that part of the system. Come to think of it,
I have never written a sequential master file update; but, I could
use "Hour 18" as a reference.

Incidentally, the example sequential update program in
Stern & Stern, "COBOL Programming," 1970, does not use
HIGH-VALUES for end-of-file. Stern & Stern, "Structured
COBOL Programming," 8th Edition, 1997, does.

Hmm! There is something about the 70's version I like. <g>
```

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **Message-ID:** `<v16cr498umuh9f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212312129.72475c14@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> TO, that is what explicit means.

Ok, the design of the structure is intended to use GO TO!

> You probably see all state machines as if they were a GO TO ...
> DEPENDING ON .. regardless of how they are actually constructed, it is
> just what you think a state machine _is_.

No, it has nothing to do with GO TO ... DEPENDING ON. The
implementations I have seen were used to control computers.
You might have known them as ROS, ROM, or Control Store.

> > Essentially, the use of PERFORM removes
> > the clues that are needed to keep the clueless
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> imagination.

I have a vivid imagination that, fortunately, stops short of paranoia.
Hmm! Why are you attacking me? <g>

> Certainly a mix of PERFORM and GO TO may well a cause of
> disaster, but a mix of PERFORM and the GO TO structure that you
> imagine around, say, an in-line perform  and evaluate, only exists in
> your mind.

Thank you! Every innovation in science and technology only
exists in one mind, initially. <g>
```

Stan, et. al.

<unabashed commercialism>

I noted that FormPrint has not been mentioned as a possible
alternative.

FormPrint supports Windows printing using NetExpress and allows for
Network printing and printing across the Internet (when combined with
the Flexus Thin Client product.)

FormPrint also has a great print preview feature as well.

For more information, please visit http://www.flexus.com or send a
private e-mail message to me.

<a id="t-8f7663699e"></a>
## Thread: VSAM Programming in COBOL vs Assembler on IBM Mainframe — 4 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-8f7663699e)._

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Programming in COBOL vs Assembler on IBM Mainframe

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T20:29:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%cIQ9.23910$an1.968162@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3E120E08.45253F22@attglobal.net>`

```
Depends upon the hardware a good bit; if you are running on anything like modern hardware, the efficiency between COBOL and
Assembler is a lot less important than if you are running on a 10+ year old machine.

Given that, if you know your business processes very well, you can always write a much more efficient program in Assembler than in
COBOL, and it will be far faster to use VSAM than a database. The trade off is that the system will be more costly to maintain in
terms of human time, and less flexible than a system written in COBOL, and far less flexible than a system written using a database.

To wit, I know of (and work with) a 30 year old system written in assembler that does its job far faster - on a 20 year old Wang VS
machine - than comparable systems perform on a modern machine which is awash with spare MIPS. It runs a life insurance daily cycle
in minutes, and virtually all queries into the system are sub second. That means if you call the company, they will have your policy
on screen as fast as they can type in your policy number, with current loan values calculated and all that kind of good stuff. By
comparison, other companies often take minutes just to get that information on screen, and then they cannot tell you the current
values and balances on the policy. (*sigh*)

The tradeoff of course, is that the system is much harder to expand or modify; but that isn't a problem with the current system
because it does what it was designed to do and new capabilities are generally not required. It is an example of a very well designed
application for a mainframe system.

Basically, you should not even be worrying about the implementation details of your project until you have all the business details
worked out in fine detail. At that point, the implementation details often take care of themselves. (Yes, that sounds like a brush
off, but it is true. If the details turn out to be basically batch oriented, then a VSAM solution is almost a no brainer; if
primarily interactive, then a DB/2 solution might be a better choice. If it has to be implemented under UNIX or on a Mainframe or
within a certain period of time, all those factors play a big part.)

What you might want to do is find a good software engineer in your area and pick his or her brains for a couple weeks. Software
engineers (like yours implemented) are usually very good at doing exactly that kind of activity, both by training and by
temperament.

-Paul


"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote in message news:3E120E08.45253F22@attglobal.net...
> I'm not a big time user of COBOL direct file processing, but from a
> study I made several years (and several COBOL compilers) ago, I have
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Programming in COBOL vs Assembler on IBM Mainframe

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T18:34:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E137B1F.CE1C0985@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3E120E08.45253F22@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell wrote:
> 
> I'm not a big time user of COBOL direct file processing, but from a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> occurred in modern IBM COBOL implementations.
<snip>
> Any info you could provide would be appreciated!

I've just been involved in migrating a shop from VSE to z/OS
1.1. They had their system tuned for the way VSE implemented
VSAM and so they ran quite fast (in fact, they incurred
increased elapsed times for some batch jobs).

What we did to solve this was to use BLSR (you code this in
the JCL). We also installed a ISV product and gave it tuning
parameters. The end result was a lowered number of I/Os and
a certain amount of buffering that the COBOL programs would
not otherwise do.

Note that with variable length records and KSDS you will be
seeing lots of CA/CI splits unless you have built your files
with plenty of free space. Even then, you will have to do
re-orgs based on how often updates are done where records
expand in size -- if you need good response time.
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM Programming in COBOL vs Assembler on IBM Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-01T21:35:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av0c27$c2t$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E120E08.45253F22@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin,
  Have you read the information in
    "1.10.7 Improving VSAM performance"
at

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.10.7

It certainly does give SOME guidance on "tuning" VSAM applications in COBOL.
As indicated, some of the "defaults" (like BUFNI) are *not* ideal for
performance with many applications.  From what I have heard, it is ALWAYS
possible to create some sort of Assembler "tuned" application that performs
better than a "simple" COBOL application.  On the other hand, current
optimization and hardware/software improvements makes me think that you
would find "sufficient" VSAM performance with "good tuning".

P.S.  I also suggest that you look at informational APAR II13288 - which
addresses a change in CI-size introduced with z/OS V1R3.  This may change
performance for MANY existing and new applications (COBOL or otherwise)
unless appropriate action is taken.
```

#### ↳ Amazing! Three answers on New Years Day!

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T11:44:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E14969F.833D0B26@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3E120E08.45253F22@attglobal.net>`

```
I guess we really don't have lives, do we?!

Computer nerds unite!

Seriously, thanks to all of you for your responses.  I have been reading an
IBM Redbook titled "VSAM Demystified", and I just got curious about the way
VSAM file processing is implemented in the real world.  My case, as I said,
was just an investigation of alternatives, and we went with sequential file
processing, with no regrets, to this day (after 15+ years).
Colin
```

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T08:58:52+00:00

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-8a769297a4"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T06:19:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm trying to create a box frame for a DOS screen by painting the
appropriate line drawing graphics characters to the screen. I've used
the library routine
CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS with the relevant parameters (screen-position,
character-buffer and string-length) but I'm not getting the desired
result.
								
For example trying to paint a horizontal line near the bottom of the
screen I'm using this:

working-storage section.
01 screen-position.
   02 row-num      pic x comp-x value 21.
   02 col-num      pic x comp-x value 00.
01 char-buffer     pic x value x"c4".
01 string-len      pic x value 80.

procedure division.

call "CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS" using
      screen-position
      char-buffer
      string-len.

All I get is a single horizontal char at col 00 and a letter "P" at
about col 10.

I'd appreciate any guidance on what I might be doing wrong.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T10:12:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com>`

```
Try the changes indicated.

Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com...
[...]
> For example trying to paint a horizontal line near the bottom of the
> screen I'm using this:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01 char-buffer     pic x value x"c4".

01 char-buffer    pic x(80) value all x"c4".

> 01 string-len      pic x value 80.

01 string-len    pic x(2) comp-x value 80.

[...]
> All I get is a single horizontal char at col 00 and a letter "P" at
> about col 10.

The letter "P" has a decimal value of 80 in the ASCII character
set.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T01:42:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com>...
> Try the changes indicated.
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> set.

Rick: thanks - I tried what you suggested but the result was the same
as before.

I actually had  '01 string-len    pic x(2) comp-x value 80' in my code
- I mistyped it on the NG posting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T09:02:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v1b5e0pn7btp4e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com>`

```

Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com>...
> > Try the changes indicated.
> >
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> - I mistyped it on the NG posting.

The result can only be the same, if the storage layout is the same;
that is, if you did not change 'char-buffer', as indicated.

The library routine 'call' is writing 80 characters beginning at the
address of 'char-buffer'. After adjustment for the 'pic x(2) comp-x'
for 'string-len', the storage layout, you provided, has one horizontal
character (x"c4") at 'char-buffer', seven spaces (or low-values),
one low-value (from 'string-len'), one "P" (value 80 from 'string-len'),
six spaces (or low-values), and 64 undefined character positions
of spaces (or low-values). This layout is what you saw.

Changing 'char-buffer' to 'pic x(80) value all x"c4"' gives a storage
layout of 80 consecutive character positions, all with the value x"c4"
(a horizontal line). This layout is what you said you want to see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T00:02:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301040002.760edb3a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com> <v1b5e0pn7btp4e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick: thanks for the explanation. I guess I was confused about the
definition of 'character-buffer' and 'string-length'. To my way of
thinking I was trying to write a single character (x"c4")80 times,
hence my original code.

The way it's re-defined, it appears we have 80 x"c4" in
'character-buffer' which we are writing 80 times ('string-length').
Still confused about the definitions I guess!

Thanks


"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<v1b5e0pn7btp4e@corp.supernews.com>...
> Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
> news:fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com...
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> (a horizontal line). This layout is what you said you want to see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T09:27:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v1dr9aj4o3t10a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com> <v1b5e0pn7btp4e@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301040002.760edb3a@posting.google.com>`

```

Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:fb10cc3a.0301040002.760edb3a@posting.google.com...
> Rick: thanks for the explanation. I guess I was confused about the
> definition of 'character-buffer' and 'string-length'. To my way of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

This type of problem occurs when meaningful names are not
used. The combination 'character-buffer' and 'string-length'
may be confusing; but 'character-buffer' and 'buffer-length'
would be more clear. Also, the use of 'string' in the sense of
the C programming language may be confusing to those
not familiar with C. The stated purpose of the library routine
is: "Writes a string of characters to the screen." This might
be clearer if stated as, "Writes a buffer ..."

Look at the routine CBL_WRITE_SCR_N_CHAR which writes
a single character to successive positions on the screen. This
routine does what you wanted, in the way you expected. Even
here, the use of "N" in the name of the routine was, likely, derived
from the use of 'n' within some string routines in C. Also, the
routine is described as writing "to a string of positions" which
is less clear than "successive positions".

In COBOL 'string' is a verb, using it as a noun may be confusing
to some.


> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <v18l473i312oe6@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301030142.1c0c2171@posting.google.com> <v1b5e0pn7btp4e@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301040002.760edb3a@posting.google.com> <v1dr9aj4o3t10a@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301060020.230323dc@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is a program that reflects what you posted and the
changes I suggested.
-------
       program-id. drawline.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 screen-position.
         02 row-num pic x comp-x value 21.
         02 col-num pic x comp-x value 0.
       01 char-buffer pic x(80) value all x"c4".
       01 string-len pic x(2) comp-x value 80.
       procedure division.
       display-horizontal-line.
           call "CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS"
             using screen-position
               char-buffer
               string-len
           stop run.
-------
This program displays a horizontal line near the bottom
of the screen (line 22 on the screen).

What you describe as "'animate' ... all I see is ..." would
happen if 'char-buffer' is described in the linkage section
of a called program and the changes suggested were not
made in the calling program. This is because value clauses
are ignored in the linkage section.  Your original post
showed "working-storage"; not "linkage".

'string-len'),

> > > >

storage
> > > > layout of 80 consecutive character positions, all with the value
x"c4"
> > > > (a horizontal line). This layout is what you said you want to see.

Rick: I have a 'main' program which calls a screen management program
(containing primarily a screen section). This screen management
program calls a 'graphics painting' program (without any parameters).
At the moment, just to test the concept, I'm just trying to draw the
horizontal line, with the results I've posted. If I compile and run
the 'graphics painting' program as a standalone - as opposed to
'called' program - everything works fine i.e I get the horizontal line
as required. It just does not work as a 'called' program.

Here's the code in the 'graphics painting' program:

 *--- Paint Graphics Characters on Screen.    2nd January 2003
       program-id. GraphicsPaint.

       working-storage section.
       01 screen-position.
          05 row-num                    pic x comp-x    value 22.
          05 col-num                    pic x comp-x    value 00.
       01 char-buffer                   pic x(80)       value all
x"C4".
       01 string-len                    pic x(2) comp-x value 80.
      *01 status-code                   pic x(2) comp-5.
       procedure division.

       call "CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS" using
             screen-position
             char-buffer
             string-len.
      *    returning status-code.  returns 00000 i.e. success
        exit program.

I tried three combinations: separate source files, nested sources,
and multiple programs in the same source file.

I was able to generate something close to what you are reporting
with multiple programs in the same source file. I added a program,
"GraphicsFail" as the last program. It used the original code, as
I understood it. The program sequence, in the source file, was:
drawline, GraphicsPaint, GraphicsFail. The call to GraphicsPaint,
from drawline, exectued the code from GraphicsFail. This occurred
because the first eight characters of the program-name were not
unique. Does this look familiar?

Incidentally, what version of MicroFocus are you using?

```
I don't know how VisOC resolves the difference between
'program-id. GraphicsPaint.' and the source file name
'grfpaint'; that is, do you call "grfpaint" or "GraphicsPaint"?
And, why would you not make them the same? I don't know
if this is a problem; but it does have the potential to be one.

I did not recognize, earlier, that, when Animator does "funny
things", it means that either the source code is out-of-sync
with the IDY or the INT is out-of-sync with the IDY. When you
mentioned that viewing 'char-buffer' showed a horizontal line,
spaces, "P", etc, and the source was 'pic x(80) all x"c4"'; this
meant that the INT was out-of-sync. I wrongly questioned
whether it was a linkage data-item. That is, when Animator
cross-referenced the INT location for 'char-buffer' it then
displayed 80 characters as defined in the updated source
rather than the single character used in the old INT; thus it
displayed 80 characters of the old INT's working-storage.
This was the horizontal line, spaces, "P", etc.

I am confident the old INT is out there, I just don't know where.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T19:26:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1DCD05.A9F5AC68@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <fb10cc3a.0301040002.760edb3a@posting.google.com> <v1dr9aj4o3t10a@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301060020.230323dc@posting.google.com> <v1j3lea5cngb11@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301070005.3ba0f8c6@posting.google.com> <v1lrdg9eesva7a@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301072331.3b1b5e0a@posting.google.com> <v1o8c6s3tokacc@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301082358.51a68e1d@posting.google.com> <v1qg155r404va1@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301090644.2d6e7073@posting.google.com> <v1r7cm1duq0e4@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:

> I don't know how VisOC resolves the difference between
> 'program-id. GraphicsPaint.' and the source file name
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am confident the old INT is out there, I just don't know where.

Paul,

Assuming Rick is on the right track about the 'spurious' INT file - try the
following -
completely EXIT from the VISOC IDE. Use the Windows Explorer to view the
project and go to sub-directory(folder) \DEBUG - delete BOTH the INT and IDY
files for the project. (You can't delete using Explorer while VISOC is
'active'). Now go back into VISOC and do a complete Rebuild of the project.

Line1 below gives you 'double-line' characters, top-left,  horizontal, a
vertical break half-way, horizontal again and then top-right.

01 Line1.
   05 pic x(01) value x"c9".           *> Alt 201
   05 pic x(38) value all x"cd".       *> Alt 205
   05 pic x(01) value x"cb".           *> Alt 203
   05 pic x(38) value all x"cd".       *> Alt 205
   05 pic x(01) value x"bb".           *> Alt 187

01 Line2 pic x(80) value all x"c4".    *> Alt 196  - your 80 char horizontal
line

Procedure Division.
 display Line1
 display " "
 display Line2
 EXIT PROGRAM.

In a DOS text environment you were able to put Numlock ON, hold down the Alt
Key while entering a three digit Decimal value for 'characters'. Wont work with
VISOC which is a Windows editor/compiler - the 'characters' get shifted. - so
you have to go with the Hex values.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

resolved with a physical file name rather than the program name in the
program-id of the called program.

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301092342.73f32220@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <fb10cc3a.0301060020.230323dc@posting.google.com> <v1j3lea5cngb11@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301070005.3ba0f8c6@posting.google.com> <v1lrdg9eesva7a@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301072331.3b1b5e0a@posting.google.com> <v1o8c6s3tokacc@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301082358.51a68e1d@posting.google.com> <v1qg155r404va1@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301090644.2d6e7073@posting.google.com> <v1r7cm1duq0e4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick: finally got the solution. I deleted all .INT and .IDY files and
did a complete rebuild. When I ran the program again, on the call to
'grfpaint' I received an error 173 'Program not in drive/directory:
grfpaint'. So, with this rather large clue and your comments guiding
me, I changed the program-id in grfpaint.cbl to 'grfpaint' and, hey
presto, it works!

I assumed, obviously incorrectly, that a call used the physical file
name to resolve the call rather than the propgram-id in the called
program. So I've learnt something rather basic.

Many thanks for your persistence.


"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<v1r7cm1duq0e4@corp.supernews.com>...
> I don't know how VisOC resolves the difference between
> 'program-id. GraphicsPaint.' and the source file name
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > > as required. It just does not work as a 'called' program.
```

Attached is a copy file containing the line drawing characters.

Note that the literal names are defined for single or double lines
along with the compass positions :

    sng-nw = single line, north west corner.
    sng-ns = single line, running north, south (vertical)
    dbl-we = double line, running west, east (horizontal)
    dbl-nt = double line, north T conector

And here is the CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS from the help file:

Routine:      CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS

Function:     Writes a string of characters to the screen.

Syntax:       call "CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS" using     screen-position
                                                   character-buffer
                                                   string-length

Parameters:   screen-position        Group item defined as:
                row-number             PIC X COMP-X.
                column-number          PIC X COMP-X.
              character-buffer       PIC X(n).
              string-length          PIC X(2) COMP-X.

Notice that you have the incorrect definition of string-length.
Hope this is of some use.

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T14:02:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E198EE0.3080300@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com>`

```
Why not try the following:

01 line-stuff.
    02  line-table pic x occurs 80 times.
    02  line-80 redefines line-table pic x(80).

perform varying line-sub from 1 by 1 until line-sub > 80
    move 'c4' to line-table(line-sub)
end-perform

display line-table (or whatever you are using to display 
the "line" on the screen.)

I know this is brute force but it might give you a line and 
if it does, well then...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T14:46:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301061446.6da8d0f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <3E198EE0.3080300@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote 

> Why not try the following:
> 
> 01 line-stuff.
>     02  line-table pic x occurs 80 times.
>     02  line-80 redefines line-table pic x(80).

You are redefining a single character field with an 80 character field.  
There happens to be 80 'line-table's, but the redefine is of one of them. 
In fact the REDEFINE has a rule that states you cannot redefine a field 
that has an OCCURS clause.
 
> perform varying line-sub from 1 by 1 until line-sub > 80
>     move 'c4' to line-table(line-sub)
> end-perform

Don't you trust 'VALUE ALL' or 'MOVE ALL' ?

Note also that 'c4' is not the same as x'c4'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - writing graphics characters to DOS screen

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T15:50:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1AF9C6.9000606@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301020619.7c8d451f@posting.google.com> <3E198EE0.3080300@nycap.rr.com> <217e491a.0301061446.6da8d0f0@posting.google.com>`

```
You are right, it was a quick thought.  The 80 character 
field is what should be redefined.

It is not that I don't trust "VALUE ALL" or "MOVE ALL" it 
seemed from a previous post that that didn't work and I was 
suggesting a quick and dirty way to get a line.

I know there is a difference in x'c4' and 'c4', again it was 
a quick post - sort of brainstorming.  Probably I should 
have said MOVE '-' since I was suggesting a brute force 
solution.

Richard wrote:
> Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote 
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Note also that 'c4' is not the same as x'c4'.
```

<a id="t-2197deb258"></a>
## Thread: Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages) — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-02T10:04:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av1no7$6u4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Any *conforming* compiler ('85 or 2002 Standard) *must* already issue a
message for missing scope terminators except in one case.  (This message
needs to be either an error message or an "extension" flagging message -
depending on whether this compiler has certain extensions.)

The code:

 Read ABC
   At End
      If X = "Y"
          Display "this"
      Else
         Display "that"
   Not at End
      Display "something else"
End-Read

is NOT conforming source code and a compiler error must be issued (by
conforming compilers - when extension flagging is specified)

Similarly,

   Call  "ABC"
      On Exception
         Add 1 to Num-Field
               On Size Error
                     Display  "Too Big"
      .

needs to get a compiler message.

    ***

The *only* time that a conditional statement without a scope terminator may
be NESTED within another statement is in either side of an
    IF ... THEN
    ELSE
construct.

Therefore, what you are asking (in another thread) for is not flagging of
missing END statements but unmatched IF-THEN-ELSE-END-IF phrase.

Rather than such a (required to be informational - because the logic MIGHT
be correct) message, IBM's compiler shows "nesting" levels in their compiler
listings.  To me this seems like the "best of both worlds".  It doesn't
imply that something is an error (when it may or may not be) but DOES give
the programmer a "visible" clue in the listing of what IF's are matched with
what END-IF's.

For those of you not familiar with how this looks, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.5.3.2

and notice the first column of single digits and how it is EASY to tell
which END-IF goes with which IF
```

#### ↳ Re: Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T22:48:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA3BC3C20553CDCE1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<av1no7$6u4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Thu, 2 Jan 2003 11:04:55 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> Any *conforming* compiler ('85 or 2002 Standard) *must* already issue a
> message for missing scope terminators except in one case. 

Bill,

We're using the term "missing" in two different senses. You're using it 
in this posting as "missing resulting in non-corformance with the ANSI 
standard". Others are using it to mean "missing according to the 
programming style I prefer". More specifically, I'm using it to mean 
"missing resulting in non-corformance with the 'safer subset' I 
described in another posting".

Both of course are valid usages, and the point I'm making is that we 
all need to specify the context.

>    Call  "ABC"
>       On Exception
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> needs to get a compiler message.

I don't understand this one. In fact I don't understand the other one 
either (that I already clipped and am too lazy to restore).

Section 4.4.3 of the 1985 standard, "Scope of Statements", second and 
third paragraphs, says "When statements are nested within aother 
statements, a separator period which terminates the sentence also 
implicitly terminates all nested statements. Whenever any statement is 
contained within another statement, the next phrase of the containing 
statement following the contained statement terminates the scope of any 
unterminated contained statement".

Where do these get to be non-conforming code?

> Therefore, what you are asking (in another thread) for is not flagging of
> missing END statements but unmatched IF-THEN-ELSE-END-IF phrase.

As explained above, what I (at least) am requesting is flagging of 
constructs that are not in a defined safer subset. This has nothing to 
do with the COBOL standard except that it's a subset.

> Rather than such a (required to be informational - because the logic MIGHT
> be correct) message,

Again (same explanation), because it's a defined subset that I'm 
concerned with, there are certain programs which are 
standard-conforming but which I nonetheless want flagged as incorrect.

> IBM's compiler shows "nesting" levels in their compiler
> listings.  To me this seems like the "best of both worlds".

The huge problem with this is that you have to look at a listing!!!

My goodness, I haven't looked at a compiler output listing more than 
once or twice in the last many thousands of lines of COBOL I've 
written. I went through a half-million-line conversion in 2001 and 
looked at a compiler output listing maybe once or twice, and those 
times I was looking for a very specific piece of information and not 
scanning the listing.

Even if I looked at listings, I would have to look through and verify 
every single case of nesting. I want mismatches pushed to me; I don't 
want to have to pull them from the listing.

Edward
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T22:53:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av5pcs$4tu$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<av1no7$6u4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA3BC3C20553CDCE1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
I won't go into the LONG and DIFFICULT work and study that the Standards
committees did concerning the text,

"When statements are nested within other statements, a separator period
which terminates the sentence also implicitly terminates all nested
statements. Whenever any statement is contained within another statement,
the next phrase of the containing statement following the contained
statement terminates the scope of any unterminated contained statement".

However, suffice it to say that this was "studied" in depth and there were
corrections made to that section to make it clear that the ONLY time a
conditional statement may be nested within another statement is within an
IF/ELSE structure.  Therefore, the only conditional statement that can be
terminated by a period is an IF/ELSE *of* if it is the "outside" statement
before a period.

This means that

  Call "ABC"
     On Exception
           Display "the displays statement is imperative"
     .

Is conforming - while

  Call  "ABC"
     On Exception
             Add 1 to NumField
                On Size Error
                       Display  "You can't nest a conditional ADD statement
within a CALL ON EXCEPTION"
         .

is NOT conforming (and requires a compiler error - or an extension flag)

Similarly,

If A = B
    Display "This is imperative"
Else
   Add 1 to NumField
     On Size Error
          Display "nesting a conditional within an ELSE is valid"
      .

is conforming while

If A = B
    Display "This is imperative"
Else
   Add 1 to NumField
     On Size Error
          Call "ABC"
             On Exception
                  Display "You ma;y NOT nest a conditional within the ADD ON
SIZE ERROR"
    .

is NOT conforming (and requires a compiler message - error or extension
flagging) as there is a conditional nested within the ADD statement.

    ***

This is why I say that OTHER than with nested IF statements, there is never
a time that a conforming compiler won't issue a message when a scope
terminator is missing.

P.S.  If anyone tries this with any compiler - with extension flagging
turned on - and doesn't get the messages where I say they will, I would be
interested in which compiler (which claims to conform to the '85 Standard)
they are using.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T10:36:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA3C69BE057AD09C1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<av1no7$6u4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA3BC3C20553CDCE1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <av5pcs$4tu$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Fri, 3 Jan 2003 23:53:46 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> However, suffice it to say that this was "studied" in depth and there were
> corrections made to that section

I was hoping for a pointer to published information ...

I don't have the 1993 update -- at the time I purchased the document, 
ANSI did not include the supplements, which each cost as much as the 
primary document. They now say the document includes the supplements; 
I'm gonna get on their case and see if they will provide me with the 
compelte document.

However, in the light of morning I see the relevant elements. In most 
statements which allow embedded statements, the embedded statement must 
be an "imperative-statement", which means a basically non-conditional 
statement or a conditional statement with explicit scope termination. 
And that's what makes your examples non-conforming.

I repeatedly forget about this "imperative-statement" limitation, even 
though it's been around since forever -- I think the distinction was in 
COBOL74, and maybe even before -- and I've been bitten by it before.

BTW, the compiler I'm using (Unisys NX COBOL85) does not flag these as 
extensions ... it flags them as errors and does not allow the extension 
at all. Of which I approve. This extension I don't need.

Edward
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Missing scope terminators (and compiler messages)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T14:49:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avc51o$gll$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<av1no7$6u4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA3BC3C20553CDCE1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

On  3-Jan-2003, Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote:

> > IBM's compiler shows "nesting" levels in their compiler
> > listings.  To me this seems like the "best of both worlds".
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> scanning the listing.

I look at them quite often - but usually with my terminal set to 80 columns, so
it isn't easy to see these nesting levels.   Not that it makes much difference,
when I pull up a program to work on, I look at the source code.  It is better to
have standards based upon KISS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard <pierra@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T19:40:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E163BA5.3000902@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <q3idnUprL-QIa4mjXTWcpA@comcast.com>`

```
The operative word is "CAN" not "will".  Nested if's can be a real pain 
if not done properly.  (I know, we all do them properly).  A convienent 
go to exit command can (not necessarily will) be signficiantly easier to 
read and follow than a highly complex if statement.

<a id="t-4338d66e23"></a>
## Thread: Cobol LE — 2 msgs

### Cobol LE

- **From:** jp <jpbeaud@gmx.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T00:38:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E161F02.C7C0BFCC@gmx.fr>`

```
I would like to knoiw if somebody knows the new features of Cobol LE.

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol LE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-03T18:05:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<av58gc$r6b$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E161F02.C7C0BFCC@gmx.fr>`

```
Easy answer, there  is not now, nor has there ever been any such  product as
   "Cobol LE"

There are a number of IBM COBOL compilers that require the LE run-time
library (and don't have their own run-time library).  This is in addition to
the currently unsupported OS/VS COBOL, DOS/VS COBOL, and VS COBOL II
products that work with LE, but didn't require LE.  (They *do* require LE to
have IBM "run-time" support today).

The names of the products (on MVS, OS/390, and z/OS) that require LE are:

  IBM SAA AD/Cycle COBOL/370
  IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
  IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
  IBM Enterprise COBOL

(All but the last one of these also work with LE under VM.  VSE has another
set of LE-enabled compilers)

Having said this, the best place to learn about the *current* IBM z/OS COBOL
compiler, is in the Migration Guide at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3MG10/CCONTENT
S

If you don't know about LE (which is NOT COBOL specific), you should also
read the latest migration guide for it at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA6130/CCONTENT
S

If you have specific question (after looking at these manuals) let us know
and I am certain that we can provide more information.
```

---

##### ↳ ↳ Re: capturing CICS screens

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T16:32:22+00:00

Also, there used to be a 3270 bridge product for CICS that allowed you to use one remote CICS client to service a web server, with
automatic translation of the 3270 data stream to HTML. Worked pretty well actually. Check out the latest version of the CICS client
and I a descendant of it is still included.

Takes about an afternoon of work to do that these days.

On the other hand, you also know that, depending upon the version of CICS you are running, you can do things like exec cics read
form and so on, right? Which means you put an HTML person on designing the screens to look good in HTML, then just power then from
CICS. It is a wee bit more complex than that, but not all that much.

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T13:01:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301041301.589e1abd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212291607.1d4a2a03@posting.google.com> <av60sm0245n@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote

> > Maybe, but it was designed for Pascal.
> 
> The hook has been set -- can you elaborate?

The Windosw API was originally developed using the Pascal calling 
convention rather than the C calling convention. The main difference is 
whether the caller or the called routine does stack tidying.

MS first worked on putting applications onto the Macintosh, this had been 
developed for Pascal.  At the time that MS started writing Windows 1 it had 
Microsoft Pascal, but no C, so both these led MS to make the API as Pascal.

Later, MS bought in a version of Lattice C which they called MSC 4.0.

I have "C Programmers Guide to MS Windows 2.0" 1988:

"The following are required features of a Windows C compiler:
  
  * Must support the NEAR and FAR keywords.

  * Must support the use of the PASCAL parameter passing convention:

     void PASCAL MenuProc( HWND, WORD );

  This keyword indicates the arguments to a called routine are passed
  in reverse sequence to the function, and the function should "pop"
  the stack.
  This is the revesrse of the normal C sequence, where the arguments
  are passed in the same sequence and the caller pops the stack."
```

difference is

Ah -- thanks.  Perhaps it's MS doing historical revision, but
that call convention (in MS/Borland products) is now referred
to as stdcall convention.  Thanks for the info.

#### ↳ Re: File-Control and File Section for MS-COBOL under CP/M

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T10:01:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0301041001.4d67b543@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E169BC7.201CBAB7@kirkvine.freeserve.co.uk>`

```
J R I <jri@kirkvine.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message news:<3E169BC7.201CBAB7@kirkvine.freeserve.co.uk>...
> I would be grateful if someone has some sample code which shows
> a declaration of the SELECT .... ASSIGN.....for MS-COBOL
> under CP/M..presume the declaration is OS specific but I don't
> have any docs to check and I'm stuck with declaring the CP/M file-name.
> Thank you

It's the same as their MS-DOS versions. Select associates an internal
file name with a device such as DISK or PRINTER. The external file
name is set with the VALUE OF FILE-ID clause.

for example:

    SELECT OUT-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK
    [ORGANIZATION IS...].

    FD OUT-FILE
        LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
        VALUE OF FILE-ID IS "OUT.DAT".

to compile foo.cob under CP/M:

COBOL =foo

to link, load and go under CP/M:

L80 foo/g

Under MS-DOS the commands are different and depend on the version.
```

#### ↳ Re: File-Control and File Section for MS-COBOL under CP/M

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-04T12:57:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301041257.25e6ac67@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E169BC7.201CBAB7@kirkvine.freeserve.co.uk>`

```
J R I <jri@kirkvine.freeserve.co.uk> wrote

> I would be grateful if someone has some sample code which shows
> a declaration of the SELECT .... ASSIGN.....for MS-COBOL
> under CP/M..presume the declaration is OS specific but I don't
> have any docs to check and I'm stuck with declaring the CP/M file-name.
> Thank you

        SELECT [OPTIONAL] myfile
            ASSIGN TO DISK
            ORGANIZATION etc ..

       
        FD  myfile
            VALUE OF FILE-ID IS {data-name | literal}
            .

The CP/M name must only be a 8.3 name and type optionally preceeded by
a drive letter and ':'.

I think that to create a new file that is OPEN I-O you need to specify
OPTIONAL otherwise it will report that the file doesn't exist. 
Obviously creating a file with OPEN OUTPUT will work without OPTIONAL.
```

### File locking and Windows 2000

- **From:** "Manny Venkatesan" <swiftaccounts@yahoo.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T13:58:43
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e198afe$0$228$cc9e4d1f@news.dial.pipex.com>`

```
I have a 16 bit package written and compiled using MF Cobol 3.4. This seems
to run well on NT or Novell servers.

Problems arise when using Windows 2000 servers and Win 98 clients. The PC's
using the application will experience a frozen DOS session.The application
will not run unless all PC's that were using the app are rebooted. This does
not happen if the client PC's are running Win 2000 or XP. The programs and
data reside on the server.

Could the problem have anything to do with incompatibilities with SMB
signing? Would MF File Share or netexpress cure this?

Thanks

Manny


---

```
