<a id="t-4ff1761b87"></a>
## Thread: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING — 10 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-4ff1761b87)._

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T14:58:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
As I read the standard, the first "initialize" could be disallowed for the
very reason you cite -- VI.6.17.3, THE INITIALIZE STATEMENT, SR4, which
disallows "initialize" against a data item that either has, or has
subordinate to it, OCCURS ... DEPENDING ON (hereinafter ODO).

However, coding the data description as
 1  the-table.
   2  filler occurs 0 to 30 depending on the-sub.
      3  the-item pic x.

avoids this ANSI-85 restriction against "initialize the-item of the-table
(the-sub)".

The fact that the circumvention of this restriction is so trivial and so
arbitrary leads me to suspect that the restriction may be unnecessary.
Since this restriction has been explicitly removed from the international
standard now in effect (ISO/IEC 1989:2002), I suspect the authors of that
new standard agreed.  (By the way, the new standard also allows "initialize
the-table").  Because the new standard allows it, and because I can't think
of a way in which the execution-time behavior would differ from the above
substitution in the data declaration, I can't seem to get terribly excited
about a compiler's failure to issue a syntax error on this subject.

However, I would not expect "initialize the-item." (without subscripts) to
be accepted as standard ANSI syntax.

I think you've misunderstood the intent of the standard as to the
optionality of subscripts when you paraphrase IV.4.3.8.4, Identifier, as "an
identifier includes a leading data-name, optional qualification, optional
subscript(s), and optional reference modification. This can involve numerous
data-names. ... ".  What it actually *says* is "An identifier is a term used
to reflect a data name that, if not unique in a program, must be followed by
a syntactically correct combination of qualifiers, subscripts, or reference
modifiers necessary for uniqueness of reference."

Unless the context in which an identifier referencing a table entry is used
explicitly allows the reference to such a table entry without explicit
subscripts, they are required.  And since "initialize" doesn't appear in the
list of such exceptions in IV.4.3.8.2, Subscripting, I would surmise that
when a table element is the target of "initialize" that table element must
be subscripted.

By the way, I can't reproduce your assertion that MCP/AS COBOL85 rejects the
first "initialize".   I get the same results as I get with my source-level
change, and that appears to match what Jerry Mouse reported for Fujitsu 6
for the first "initialize".  But I'm pretty sure your second statement, an
unsubscripted "initialize the-item",  would have to be an extension (flagged
or not) to ANSI COBOL, as I see it, even applying the 2002 standard.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Edward Reid" <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com...
> The question: does the DEPENDING restriction on INITIALIZE apply only
> to unsubscripted references or to all references? (In ANSI85 COBOL.)
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** edwardreid@spamcop.net (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T20:57:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
> By the way, I can't reproduce your assertion that MCP/AS COBOL85 rejects the
> first "initialize".   I get the same results as I get with my source-level
> change,

Chuck,

Thanks for the long and informative response.

Where I am tonight, I don't have access to the latest released COBOL85
compiler, and of course I don't have access to all the versions that
you do. All I can say for now is as follows:

l;c
000100 identification division.
000200 program-id. init-sub.
000300 data division.
000400 working-storage section.
000600 1 the-sub pic 99.
000700 1 the-table.
000800   2 the-item pic x occurs 0 to 30 depending on the-sub.
000900 procedure division.
001000 begin-prog.
001100     initialize the-item of the-table (the-sub)
001200     .
#
#COMPILING 6320
001200     .
           1
 1) ERROR:  963 : INVALID INITIALIZE IDENTIFIER
#SNTX
#ET=0.6 PT=0.5 IO=0.0
file *system/cobol85 on disk {sh releaseid}
 *SYSTEM/COBOL85 ON DISK {SH RELEASEID}

File Name                ReleaseID
--------------------------+------------------------

*SYSTEM/COBOL85            IC COBOL85-047.1A.42 [47
*SYSTEM/COBOL85/INITIALCCI

2 FILES FOUND
#
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T00:08:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avdqtr$9f4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com>`

```
You seem to have missed the main point of Chuck's note.  In the '85 Standard
(but *not* the 2002 Standard) it is "illegal" to do an INITIALIZE against a
data-item defined with an OCCURS ... DEPENDING ON clause.  In your example
listed below you try and do an INITIALIZE against the field

  "the-item"

which is defined with an OCCURS DEPENDING clause.  The correction (according
to what Chuck indicated) is to change your sample code to:

 000100 identification division.
 000200 program-id. init-sub.
 000300 data division.
 000400 working-storage section.
 000600 1 the-sub pic 99.
 000700 1 the-table.
 000800   2 the-item occurs 0 to 30 depending on the-sub.
             *  the next line is new
                  3 Item-with-no-ODO-clause  Pic X.
 000900 procedure division.
 001000 begin-prog.
             * you probably want to set the-sub to some value here to vaoid
bad run-time results
 001100     initialize the-item of Item-with-no-ODO-clause  (the-sub)
 001200     .

Although this "functionally" does what your sample did, it does it in a way
that conforms to the '85 Standard rule.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** edwardreid@spamcop.net (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T11:23:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa1295b3.0301071123.7258406d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com> <avdqtr$9f4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> You seem to have missed the main point of Chuck's note.  In the '85 Standard
> (but *not* the 2002 Standard) it is "illegal" to do an INITIALIZE against a
> data-item defined with an OCCURS ... DEPENDING ON clause.  In your example
> listed below you try and do an INITIALIZE against the field

Thanks, Bill.

I was only responding to a small part of Chuck's posting. He noted the
he didn't get a syntax error from the compiler where I said I did. I
wasn't responding to the rest of his posting, except where I thanked
him for the information. I understand what you are saying.

Chuck has probably also seen the TRs I've filed, so there's a
background that isn't completely visible in either of our postings.
Some of his explanation was also relevant to a different issue I had
raised, regarding whether you can INITIALIZE an item which is
*subsidiary* to an OCCURS (not ODO). It appears that this case is
widely implemented but, contrary to my previous belief (and probably
contrary to the belief of many who discuss it at various places on the
web), is an extension to the 1985 standard.

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T12:21:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avfct0$rjs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com> <avdqtr$9f4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aa1295b3.0301071123.7258406d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Edward Reid" <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:aa1295b3.0301071123.7258406d@posting.google.com...

> Some of his explanation was also relevant to a different issue I had
> raised, regarding whether you can INITIALIZE an item which is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> web), is an extension to the 1985 standard.

No, I don't think that's true.  You should be able to INITIALIZE an item
that's *subsidiary* to an item with an OCCURS clause whether or not it's an
ODO OCCURS (my suggested revision does exactly that, in fact, in what both
Bill and I have stated is a standards-conforming manner).  But if the target
of INITIALIZE is subsidiary to one with an OCCURS, that target's going to
need subscripts.

According to the standard, you can also INITIALIZE a GROUP that contains an
item that has OCCURS; it is however illegal to INITIALIZE a GROUP that
contains an item that has ODO.  COBOL2002 lifts this latter restriction.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** edwardreid@spamcop.net (Edward Reid)
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T10:14:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aa1295b3.0301081014.456d6002@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com> <avdqtr$9f4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aa1295b3.0301071123.7258406d@posting.google.com> <avfct0$rjs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> But if the target
> of INITIALIZE is subsidiary to one with an OCCURS, that target's going to
> need subscripts.

This is what I'm saying is implemented (as an extension, though many
people don't realize that) on many compilers. I base this observation
not on personal experience with other compilers, but on searching the
web and the newsgroup for information on this issue, and finding
various comments indicating that the feature is available.
Unfortunately I did not compile a bibliography ;-).

As with some of the other things we've discussed, this is certainly
implementable and doesn't appear to have any ambiguities, at least at
first glance. The meaning, of course, would be to initialize all
occurrences.

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T15:28:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avi55g$gkv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com> <avdqtr$9f4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aa1295b3.0301071123.7258406d@posting.google.com> <avfct0$rjs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301081014.456d6002@posting.google.com>`

```
Edward,
   Can you point me to some rule in the '85 Standard that says doing an

initialize of data-item that does NOT have an ODO and does not have an ODO
subordinate to it - and is referenced with s subscript

is an extension?

It is an extension to INITIALIZE an item that is defined with an ODO - but
not one that is SUB-ordinate to it.
```

against a

example

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T08:56:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avf0s4$ins$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <avd1n5$28v2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com>`

```

"Edward Reid" <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:aa1295b3.0301062057.42ced9d2@posting.google.com...

> Where I am tonight, I don't have access to the latest released COBOL85
> compiler, and of course I don't have access to all the versions that
> you do. All I can say for now is as follows:  ...

Well, I just tried it with your new version, and you're right, it won't
compile.  Then I tried it again with a reconstruction of the one I used
yesterday (without my change), and it won't compile either.  I don't know
what was different; I can't see any change to the program, and every
compiler I tried from base release SSR 46.1 onward worked just fine
yesterday.  There's some minor difference between my test program of
yesterday and that of today that I don't see.

In any event, the syntax error you are receiving on the subscripted
initialize is consistent with the rules, and you don't get it if you move
the OCCURS up one level to a FILLER item that contains the-item, the
recommended approach.  And subscripts are indeed required when a table
element is the target of INITIALIZE.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

## Thread: calling c program from cics — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### calling c program from cics

- **From:** raj1136@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T20:12:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3018c431.0301062012.1ee2f4f1@posting.google.com>`

```
How can i call c program on tso/ispf from cics program?
```

#### ↳ Re: calling c program from cics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-06T23:58:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avdq9m$nog$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3018c431.0301062012.1ee2f4f1@posting.google.com>`

```
There may be a language problem, but I don't understand your question.

You can use EXEC CICS LINK (or the COBOL CALL statement) to go between COBOL
and C under CICS.

However, I don't understand your reference to
  "c  program on tso/ispf"

Are you trying to run a program under ISPF instead of under CICS?  Are you
trying to find a CICS emulator under ISPF (similar to BTS for IMS)? Are you
interested in batch?   What environment are your calling and called really
programs in?

In general, most of your questions about ILC (inter-language communications)
between COBOL and C should be answered at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea4110/4.0

which is,
 "Chapter 4. Communicating between C and COBOL"

in the current LE ILC manual.
```

- **References:** `<3018c431.0301062012.1ee2f4f1@posting.google.com>`

```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e1ada4d$1_2@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Statistical function normal distribution is missing in cobol. Where can
I find a solution to this problem. Environment is IBM Enterprise COBOL
for z/OS and OS/390 3.1.0
```

#### ↳ Re: Statistic function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T16:33:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avfkk9$2vv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The following is the information that I received from my "usually reliable
source" for COBOL's interactions with statistical stuff.  If you have a
better explanation, we may be able to help you better.

"Bill -

I don't understand the question.

Not sure what the function would do. Is the person looking for random
numbers to be generated in a normal distribution?

Standard deviation is often expressed as percentages related to the Normal
Distribution. For example, (I am sure I have the numbers wrong) 67% of a
Normal Distribution is within one standard deviation, 88% within two, 97%
within three,..., 99.9999% within six.

If there is a mathematical relationship, and that is what you want, I don't
know it, but I have some people here who might be able to help."
```

```

#### ↳ Re: Statistic function

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T13:47:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C792A5C7BCC29281.D9DAD22C5753CC1F.B0313BE06FF1FD98@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3e1ada4d$1_2@news.onlynews.com>`

```
On 7 Jan 2003 08:46:53 -0500, lindfors <member@mainframeforum.com>
enlightened us:

>Statistical function normal distribution is missing in cobol. Where can
>I find a solution to this problem. Environment is IBM Enterprise COBOL
>for z/OS and OS/390 3.1.0

Calculating a Normal Distribution is a process done with Calculus
which doesn't lend itself well to Cobol.  SPSS or Fortran are much
better at handling this kind of calculation.  I could give you the
formula but calculus formulas do not lend themselves to a PC keyboard,
i.e., I couldn't type many of the symbols.  However, an example can be
seen at:

http://www.formestrie.qc.ca/met5240/plaisent/13_normal_distribution.html

Good luck.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

My wife dresses to kill.  She cooks the same way.
                               -- Henny Youngman
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-72ad67550b"></a>
## Thread: Calling a C program from COBOL. — 3 msgs

### Calling a C program from COBOL.

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T07:42:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0301070742.6a92580a@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to write a COBOL program that would call an existing C program.

This is on a mainframe (OS/390 with LE).

The C program has a main() function and expects one argument.  For some
reason, the name of the C program is 'CALL'.  It does not return a result.

***************************************
void main(int  argc,char *argv[])
***************************************

My COBOL program (in relevant part), is coded like this:

********************************************************************
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.    CSQUTILD.

...

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

...

 01  PGM-CALL             PIC X(08) VALUE 'CALL    '.
 01  PARM-EMM             PIC X(03) VALUE 'EMM'.

...

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARM-INPUT.

...

CALL PGM-CALL USING BY CONTENT PARM-EMM.

...


**********************************************************************


The following complile options were used:

'DYNAM,OPT,RENT,DATA(24),MAP,XREF',
'NOCMPR2,NUMPROC(NOPFD),NOLIST',

***********************************************************************

The following link-edit options were used:

'LIST,LET,XREF,REUS,AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY),RENT,RES',

The link-edit SYSIN card looked like this :

//SYSIN    DD *
  INCLUDE SYSLIB(EDCSTART)
  INCLUDE SYSLIB(CEEROOTB)
  INCLUDE SYSLIB(@@CBL2C)
  INCLUDE SYSLMOD(CALL)
  ENTRY CSQUTILD
  NAME CALL(R)
//*
***********************************************************************

The compile and link-edit went successfully, but when the program is run,
it abends
with U4038 with the following message:

IGZ0015S A recursive call was attempted to a program that was already
active.
         The program name is CSQUTILD.
         From compile unit CSQUTILD at entry point CSQUTILD at statement
116 at
         compile unit offset +00000716 at address 3B600B36.
<> LEAID ENTERED (LEVEL 09/02/1999 AT 08.41)
<> LEAID ABENDAID DD ALLOCATED BY CWBMAKDD DYNALLOC RC =00000
o

Offset 716 is the above CALL statement.

******************************************************************

The ABENDAID CALL TRACE summary looks like this:

                         **********************
                         * Call Trace Summary *
                         **********************

******Calling*****     ****Return****           ******Called******
Load-Mod  Program      Type     Value           Program   Load-Mod

*SYSTEM                                Calls    CSQUTILD  CSQUTILD

CSQUTILD  CSQUTILD                     Calls    CEEBTOR   CEEBINIT

CEEBINIT  CEEBTOR                      Calls    CSQUTILD  CSQUTILD   *

CSQUTILD  CSQUTILD     DISP  00000718  Calls    IGZCFCC   IGZCFCC

                      Program Causing Error  *

********************************************************************

I am not able to figure why CSQUTILD is being called again when I call
'CALL    '.

What should I change (way the CALL statement is coded, compile/link-edit
options) to make this work?

As another poster was advised, I did read the ILC guide and that is where I 
thought I got the link-edit statements.  Obviously, I am doing something 
wrong.  And I don't understand compile and link-edit options very well.

Any help is appreciated.

Thanks in advance,

- Manoj.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a C program from COBOL.

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T13:52:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301071352.4d799d9c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0301070742.6a92580a@posting.google.com>`

```
manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj) wrote in message news:<ec0f6848.0301070742.6a92580a@posting.google.com>...
> //SYSIN    DD *
>   INCLUDE SYSLIB(EDCSTART)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> /*

Is this the LKED parms for the Cobol compile?  You are mixing the C
module name "CALL" with the entry name of your Cobol program,
"CSQUTILD".

I would suggest just using a straight dynamic call to the C module and
saving yourself the trouble.

Compile and Link the "Call" program such that it can be executed via
JCL.  Then make sure it is in a steplib or the system linklist and
execute CSQUTILD with the "CALL myCpgm USING myParm RETURNING
dummyArea END-CALL".

Even without a return parameter I suggest using the RETURNING clause,
otherwise you might someday find yourself in the position of having
strange return codes for your job step.  Then again, maybe not.

Let me know if it helps,

Joe Z.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling a C program from COBOL.

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2003-01-12T23:00:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0301122300.25a1a326@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0301070742.6a92580a@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0301071352.4d799d9c@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote in message news:<dcedb8d0.0301071352.4d799d9c@posting.google.com>...
> manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj) wrote in message news:<ec0f6848.0301070742.6a92580a@posting.google.com>...
> > //SYSIN    DD *
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe Z.

Thanks, after changing my linkage statements and making my program
call the C program dynamically, it worked.  The one additional thing I
had to do was to pass the number of parameters also as a parameter. 
This is because I am calling the main() which expects (int argc, char*
argv[]).

So I had to do CALL PGM-CALL USING EMM-COUNT,PARM-EMM.  (EMM-COUNT=1).
If calling from JCL, this is not needed for some reason.  We are just
passing the argument.

- Manoj.
```

---

<a id="t-bfe5558222"></a>
## Thread: Segmentation Faults and Bus Errors — 4 msgs

### Segmentation Faults and Bus Errors

- **From:** Richard.Sgrignoli@highmark.com (Richard E Sgrignoli)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T09:54:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.unix.programmer,comp.unix.questions,comp.unix.admin,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a3c69df.0301070954.656c6801@posting.google.com>`

```
Question below on what may be causing "Segmentation Faults" (coredump)
(Return Code=139) and "Bus Errors".....



First, some info you may need.....
Primary Players:  PeopleSoft Release 8 and MicroFocus COBOL (Server
Express Version 2.0.11)
Operating System:  Solaris 8 Kernel Level 108528-17
Hardware:  Sun E10000 4-Board Domain with 14 CPUs (400MHz) and 8Gb of
Memory



Description of Problem:

We run a handful of COBOL programs within our batch
cycle.....PeopleSoft-delivered General Ledger, Accounts-Payable,
Purchasing, et cetera.....however, of this handful of modules, the
error ONLY seems to affect ONE -- the General Ledger.....

The jobs are initiated from the mainframe (MVS) and execute scripts on
the Open Systems (UNIX) server.  These jobs may run SUCCESSFULLY many
times, anywhere from 10-60 times, but then suddenly a subsequent batch
may abend, randomly.  The main errors are either "segmentation fault"
or "bus error".

------------------- Script Error Message --------------------
signal fault in critical section
signal number: 11, signal code: 1	fault address: 0xfff
libthread panic: fault in libthread critical section : dumping core
(PID: #####
stacktrace:
	feeb4e10
	feebcc18
	0
<directory path>/hm_cobol_delivered.262.:27490 Segmentation
-------------------- End of Script Error Message --------------------

Abended in PROC09 with Return Code = 139 (Segmentation Fault)
Abended in PROC04 with Return Code = 139 (Segmentation Fault)

When the job abends, it is simply reinitiated from the MVS side at the
step where it failed, and it completes successfully.  NOTE: In these
situations where the jobs were re-run, there were NO changes to the
program, environment, configuration, arguments being passed, et
cetera.....it just simply completed successfully on the re-try.



Attempts to Resolve:

PeopleSoft has been consulted on this issue, which has been plaguing
us for several weeks ever since upgrading to PeopleSoft Release 8;
however, there has been no progress.  PeopleSoft is unwilling to move
beyond current level of support if the problem cannot be replicated on
a CONSISTENT basis.  Again, these abends occur randomly.



Questions:

Unsure if this is a PeopleSoft issue, a COBOL (Server Express) issue,
or a UNIX issue.  Could this be related to a memory issue?  I am
providing some "vmstat" data which surrounds the times of some of the
abends for your review.

cpu

What are some of the areas which we should be looking at to resolve
these abends?  Do you feel this is a PeopleSoft issue, and what should
we look for and pass on to PeopleSoft that will persuade them to look
into this further?  Is this a UNIX issue?  What changes can/should be
made on the UNIX side to potentially resolve the issue and/or
eliminate that as a contributing factor?

Any info and/or pointers you may be able to provide, either from
specific experience with this problem or your expertise with how all
the above interact, would be GREATLY appreciated.

You may either respond within the newsgroup or, preferably, direct to
my e-mail address:
	Richard.Sgrignoli@highmark.com

Many thanks.

Richard E Sgrignoli

#### ↳ Re: Segmentation Faults and Bus Errors

- **From:** Paul Pluzhnikov <ppluzhnikov@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T06:04:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.unix.programmer,comp.unix.questions,comp.unix.admin,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvg0yoqip.fsfYB8X@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9a3c69df.0301070954.656c6801@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard.Sgrignoli@highmark.com (Richard E Sgrignoli) writes:

> Question below on what may be causing "Segmentation Faults" (coredump)
> (Return Code=139) and "Bus Errors".....

Any number of things, but most likely a program bug.

> Description of Problem:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> or "bus error".

These symptoms are quite typical for heap corruption and stack
overflow bugs -- most of the time things that were "stepped on"
are not critical, but once in a while they are.

> signal fault in critical section
> signal number: 11, signal code: 1	fault address: 0xfff
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> <directory path>/hm_cobol_delivered.262.:27490 Segmentation

Somebody corrupted libpthread's data.

> When the job abends, it is simply reinitiated from the MVS side at the
> step where it failed, and it completes successfully.  NOTE: In these
> situations where the jobs were re-run, there were NO changes to the
> program, environment, configuration, arguments being passed, et
> cetera.....it just simply completed successfully on the re-try.

That is often the case for non-threaded programs, but with
threads even slight change in timing or system load could
make the bug disappear.

> Attempts to Resolve:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> a CONSISTENT basis.  Again, these abends occur randomly.

Tell them you are not going to renew maintenance unless and until
this issue is resolved.

> Questions:
> 
> Unsure if this is a PeopleSoft issue, a COBOL (Server Express) issue,
> or a UNIX issue.  

It could be any. 
Does the program that abends include your code, or is it
a pure PeopleSoft-compiled binary? If the latter, COBOL
probably has nothing to do with it. 
I have no idea what you call "a UNIX issue".

> Could this be related to a memory issue?

Are you having any other programs "randomly" coredump?
If not, you can probably exclude your hardware from the
list of culprits. And since your other "modules" (just what
do you call modules?) do not exhibit the same problem, you can
probably exclude Solaris libraries as well.

>  I am providing some "vmstat" data ...

Irrelevant, I think.

> What are some of the areas which we should be looking at to resolve
> these abends?

Are you *qualified* to look at these abends?
Do you know how to use a debugger? 
If so, what is the crash stack trace?

If not, hire someone who does. 
An expert will likely be able to tell you a short list of suspect
areas within a day or two.

>  Do you feel this is a PeopleSoft issue, and what should

It doesn't matter what we feel. You haven't supplied nearly enough
details to feel anything. You may have better luck talking to other
people who run the same release of PeopleSoft on similar hardware.

Cheers,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Segmentation Faults and Bus Errors

- **From:** those who know me have no need of my name <not-a-real-address@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T07:46:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.unix.programmer,comp.unix.questions,comp.unix.admin,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m1wule3h71.gnus@usa.net>`
- **References:** `<9a3c69df.0301070954.656c6801@posting.google.com> <uvg0yoqip.fsfYB8X@earthlink.net>`

```
[fu-t set]

in comp.unix.questions i read:

>> Could this be related to a memory issue?
>
>Are you having any other programs "randomly" coredump?
>If not, you can probably exclude your hardware from the
>list of culprits. 

provided that at least one other program has been run on the system that
stresses it to the same degree as the one that faults.  if the system is
typically quiescent, existing only to run this job, then it might be that
it is the only one to hit the bad memory, or controller, or cpu, or various
bus interfaces, or a myriad of other devices that may exist.

>If so, what is the crash stack trace?

if it's in the peoplesoft code it's not likely that much beyond a hex dump
of the stack frames will be available, and given that peoplesoft doesn't
seem to be responding usefully it might be something of a problem actually
finding the culprit.
```

Jack Sleight <jacksleight@hotmail.com> wrote:
>Naw, it's a term of endearment that Doc uses. I just borrowed it for

Hmmmmm... I never thought of it as a particular endearment; my use of it 
is most often prompted by the desire to show the absurdity of obscenity or 
ad-hominem arguments ('You say I am a motherless fornicator of underage 
goats?  Well, *I* say *you* are a poopie-head!').  On the other hand... 
obscenity is quite often expressed when Old Buddies meet so the 
association with endearment is readily understandable; when old sailors 
meet they use terms towards each others that would... well, that would 
make a sailor blush.

DD

## Thread: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T17:49:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,

I need help here.

I was doing my year end program to clear out some records in a file.

I was using DELETE abc-file command in the program. After deleted, I
am no longer able to access those records deleted.

But when I type the file out int DOS PROMPT, it seems that the record
still there with a special character in front of it (could be an
indicator in DOS).

I would like to ask to help on how to get back those deleted records?
using COBOL commands or DOS commands?

Thank you in advance.

Regards,

Calvin
```

#### ↳ Re: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T22:34:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uadnesGEbIBN4ajXTWckw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com>`

```

"Calvin" <calvinchin@fm333.com> wrote in message
news:62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com...
> Hi there,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you in advance.

Write a program to copy the file in sequential format (instead of relative)
and change the first byte during the copy to whatever character does NOT
mean deleted.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin)
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T20:54:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<62843e96.0301082054.7d4ab190@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com> <5uadnesGEbIBN4ajXTWckw@giganews.com>`

```

> "Calvin" <calvinchin@fm333.com> wrote in message
> news:62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> mean deleted.


Thanks for your answer. However, when reading the file, COBOL will not
be able to read those deleted records, so how can I write it to the
sequential format?

Thank you.

Regards,
Calvin
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T01:20:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cdpT9.35236$p_6.2995688@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com> <5uadnesGEbIBN4ajXTWckw@giganews.com> <62843e96.0301082054.7d4ab190@posting.google.com>`

```

Calvin <calvinchin@fm333.com> wrote in message news:62843e96.0301082054.7d4ab190@posting.google.com...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<5uadnesGEbIBN4ajXTWckw@giganews.com>...
> > "Calvin" <calvinchin@fm333.com> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> sequential format?

Read the file in straight sequential format also.  You are basically treating it
as a simple text file.  Read each record, manipulate the first byte if necessary,
and write each record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T18:17:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301091817.5f3d228@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com> <5uadnesGEbIBN4ajXTWckw@giganews.com> <62843e96.0301082054.7d4ab190@posting.google.com>`

```
calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin) wrote 

> Thanks for your answer. However, when reading the file, COBOL will not
> be able to read those deleted records, so how can I write it to the
> sequential format?

The suggestion was that you _read_ the file as if it were a sequential
file.  A sequential file is just 'one record after another' and has no
concept of whether a record is deleted or not.

Whether this can be done or not is entirely dependant on how the files
are structured and this is different for each vendor, and sometimes by
version.

For example with MF Level II format indexed files an indexed file is
in two parts: a data file and an index file.  The data file is just a
collection of fixed size records, each terminated by x'0D0A'.  A
deleted record is marked by changing this to x'0D00'.

It is possible to read the data part of an indexed file as
organization sequential and to specify the record as 2 bytes longer to
cover the record marker.  Reading this file will give all records
including deleted ones and 'junk' records where file space has been
allocated and no record has yet been written.  The records will be in
physical order.

However, different Cobol systems have different mechanisms and there
is no guarantee that a system will be able to read the data file in
any way that would be useful.  For example Fujitsu Cobol may have its
indexed file as a single file with data records and index blocks
mixed.  This may make it impossible to access deleted records.

As you have not mentioned the brand of Cobol, the version, the
operating system or any other relevant details, any suggestions are
merely guesses and may be less than helpful.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to Recover records Deleted using COBOL physical delete comman?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-07T22:41:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301072240.23f51c86@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0301071749.742a9704@posting.google.com>`

```
calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin) wrote 

> I was using DELETE abc-file command in the program. After deleted, I
> am no longer able to access those records deleted.

That is correct, DELETE makes them no longer accessable.
 
> But when I type the file out int DOS PROMPT, it seems that the record
> still there with a special character in front of it (could be an
> indicator in DOS).

That is quite likely.  The file still occupies the same space on the
disk and the DELETEd records are marked as being reusable space.  If
you write new records to the file then the file probably won't expand
in size, the new records will overwrite the deleted ones.
 
> I would like to ask to help on how to get back those deleted records?
> using COBOL commands or DOS commands?

In general you can't.  However, if you mention the brand of Cobol and
version, and how the SELECT..ASSIGN then someone may be able to
suggest something.
```

---

## Thread: Shareware: txt2pdf 6.3 — 1 msgs

### Shareware: txt2pdf 6.3

- **From:** "Sanface Software" <sanface@sanface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T13:24:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ed2e410345c118f387def332b948b284.93245@mygate.mailgate.org>`

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.3 version. 
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in every
operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS and
EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/invoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
Remember to read the Hachette Filipacchi Media U.S. project at
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/?AURL=%2FArticles%2FV%2FV40603%2Easp

What's new in this version

Korean font support
Updated FAQ 

Test txt2pdf 6.3!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html

## Thread: calling assember to get memory — 9 msgs

### calling assember to get memory

- **From:** 1aeb0@gmx.de (Detlef Giller)
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T08:59:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70962b9a.0301080859.68d9a2ca@posting.google.com>`

```
Hallo,

I call an assembler program to build a memory pool, this works fine.
Now I wish to use this memory in the main cobol program. I'm working
on a mainframe (fujitsui-Siemens OSD) with compiler cobol85 v2.2 and
assembh v1.2b.
Can anybody tell me how I can allocate the memory pool to a cobol
calling parameter ? Where is my misunderstanding ?

Thank's for your help

Detlef Giller



WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  ZZPOOL-MEMORY.
  02 FELD-1       PIC X(1000).
  02 FELD-2       PIC X(1000).
   :
   :

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
**Before the call, adress of ZZPOOL-MEMORY points to the
working-storage.

CALL "ZZPOOL" USING ZZPOOL-MEMORY

**After the call, I hoped the adress of ZZPOOL-MEMORY points to the
memory pool,
**but it is not so. ZZPOOL-MEMORY points always to the adress in the
working
**storage-area. 

MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJK" TO ZZPOOL-MEMORY
                                           .


My assembler program ZZPOOL 
MAIN     STM   R14,R12,12(R13)            Save all reg's
         BALR  R3,R0                      
         USING *,R3
         ST    R1,R1SAVE                  Save reg 1 
         :
         :
         Get Memory pool and save adress in POOLADR
         :
PGMEXIT  EQU   *
         L     R1,R1SAVE                  reload reg1
         MVC   0(4,R1),POOLADR            POOLADR back to main ?
         LM    R14,R12,12(R13)            
         BR    R14                        back to caller

POOLADR  DS    F
R1SAVE   DS    F                          REG1 SAVE

         END
```

#### ↳ Re: calling assember to get memory

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreas@andreas-lerch.de>
- **Date:** 2003-01-08T18:44:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030108.18444257@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online>`
- **References:** `<70962b9a.0301080859.68d9a2ca@posting.google.com>`

```


>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprüngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

Am 08.01.03, 16:59:34, schrieb 1aeb0@gmx.de (Detlef Giller) zum Thema 
calling assember to get memory:


> Hallo,

Hello

<snip>

> My assembler program ZZPOOL
> MAIN     STM   R14,R12,12(R13)            Save all reg's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>          L     R1,R1SAVE                  reload reg1
This only addresses the beginning of the parm-list. R1 Points to a 
list of fullword addresses, one for each parm. Bit 31 is one if the 
last parm is reached


Here you must insert:
		L R1,0(R1)        adress first parm

>          MVC   0(4,R1),POOLADR            POOLADR back to main ?

this Points to the address of the first parm on the addresslist

>          LM    R14,R12,12(R13)
>          BR    R14                        back to caller

> POOLADR  DS    F
> R1SAVE   DS    F                          REG1 SAVE

>          END

Have a nice day


Andreas
```

#### ↳ Re: calling assember to get memory

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T07:23:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1D23C1.425E0B7@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<70962b9a.0301080859.68d9a2ca@posting.google.com>`

```
I think you would have to reference the memory pool using the LINKAGE
SECTION instead of the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.  Here is a possible
example:

LINKAGE SECTION.

01  POOLADR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

01  ZZPOOL-MEMORY.
    02 FELD-1       PIC X(1000).
    02 FELD-2       PIC X(1000).

And then somewhere in your procedure division you would have to do
this:

CALL "ZZPOOL" USING POOLADR-POINTER.
SET ADDRESS OF ZZPOOL-MEMORY TO POOLADR-POINTER.
MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJK" TO ZZPOOL-MEMORY.

This assumes that your ZZPOOL assembler program does a GETMAIN or
equivalent to acquire the memory pool, and then returns the address of
this memory pool to the COBOL calling program.  The calling program
would then establish addressability to the memory pool (using the SET
ADDRESS statement).  Only then would the COBOL calling program be able
to read or write to the memory pool.

I hope that helps!


Detlef Giller wrote:
> 
> Hallo,
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>          END
```

Well, I'm confused. By the Assembler code, it looks like you are on OS/390 or
z/OS, but then I'm not sure what you are talking about "cobol85"; there is no
compiler by that name on those platforms.

Anyway, if you are on OS/390 or z/OS, you should look at using the callable LE
services for dynamic storage management. Powerful, easy to use, supported, and
no requirement for any assembler at all.

<ad>
We just happen to teach courses on how to do this, and have taught them around
the US as well Scandanavia and the Middle East. Love to come to Germany and do
the same.

If you are on an IBM COBOL - VS COBOL II or later (and you should move
quickly to the most current COBOL you can) you have USAGE POINTER
because they implemented it to support CICS.

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: calling assember to get memory

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T08:48:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1E7AC7.4AF68C44@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<70962b9a.0301080859.68d9a2ca@posting.google.com> <3E1D23C1.425E0B7@worldnet.att.net> <70962b9a.0301090521.44bfe76b@posting.google.com>`

```

Detlef Giller schrieb:
> 
> thank you for your help. unfortunately we use cobol85 and this
> compiler version don't have the data class pointer.
> 
hello Detlef,
may be it is possible for you to switch to compiler COBOL2000 version
V1.0B (or greater): it supports data pointers; the code Arnold has
proposed would need only two little changes: POOLADR-POINTER should be
defined in WORKING-STORAGE section and ZZPOOL-MEMORY should have the
BASED clause (and your assembler program then needs the 'L R1,0(R1)'
proposed by Adreas). Incompatibilities between COBOL85 V2.2 and
COBOL2000 are none to speak of and concern mostly compiler options and
and increased set of reserved words (for details refer to
'Freigabemitteilung' for COBOL85 V2.3A and COBOL2000).
if you need to stay with COBOL85, an other possibility for your COBOL
program to access the reqested memory would be to call the COBOL program
*as a subprogram* after requesting the memeory by the caller and handing
over the address of the memory as a parameter, that is defined as
ZPOOL-MEMORY in the LINKAGE section: you could thus avoid data pointers
in COBOL

hope that helps
Karl
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: calling assember to get memory

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** 1aeb0@gmx.de (Detlef Giller)

Unfortunately, I'm not sure either.   I do know that in mainframe environments
that I have been in, if I do maintenance on a called routine, I have to
extensively test every program that uses that called routine, making re-use very
expensive.  But OO advocates say re-use saves money.   At any rate, for selfish
reasons, I would love to get some OO CoBOL experience.  (commercial experience -
home and school experience are not worth much in a resume).   I also want to be
ready if we ever use Java on our mainframe - to interface CoBOL and Java. 
Currently Java is used elsewhere here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

```
Howard, 

I would love to be encouraging here.  But I can't be.  When I still
had access to COBOL for this and that, I messed around with it for a
bit for the OO stuff.  The implemtation was so marginal it was next to
useless.  IBM since "Improved" it.  Now they REMOVED SOM - and tell
you that the only purpose for the syntax is so you can invoke Java
routines and that it is not designed for OO development - basically
steering people AWAY from OO on the mainframe.

If you want to see a good OO implementation on the mainframe - check
out the Siemans/Fujitsu OO COBOL Compiler - these folks are very far
along their implementation of the 2002 standard re: OO (And other
areas).  They may be able to help you find some examples.

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T00:27:53
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> I would like to find an example of a couple or three IBM mainframe CoBOL
> programs that I can compile, run, and show others that use OO.
Preferably, when
> I show them to others, they will see the advantage over using called
routines.

Howard,

I considered for a while to send you something that is OO and will compile
on the mainframe and DOES demonstrate an advantage over a normal called
module, but I reckon you're just trolling <G>.

If you seriously want to gain some OO experience, get Rogers Cadenhead's
book "Java 2 in 24 hours". Work your way through it (it is very enjoyable
and extremely well written), THEN start thinking about OO COBOL (I recommend
Wil Price's book, "Elements of Object-Oriented COBOL" [in fact, mine goes
all over the World with me and that's how the CD inside the back cover got
broken...<G>]).

If and when you do BOTH these things, you will gladly write three items that
meet the requirements you specified, yourself....

For Raul, here are some advantages of OO classes over called modules, in a
mainframe  (or any other) environment:

1. Classes can be instantiated as often as required and therefore cope with
recursive processing much better than called modules. Yes, with the latest
compilers you can use local storage in a called module to handle recursion,
but it will never be as elegant as an object instance. (Electronic Data
processing on commercial installations rarely requires recursive processing
so I admit this one is weak...<G>)

2. You can easily extend a class without having to regression test
everything that uses it. Only objects that use the new or overridden method
need be tested.  (If you modify a called module EVERYTHING that uses it
needs to be reviewed.)

3. You can add a new interface to a class without having to re-compile every
module that calls it. Only modules that use the new interface need be
re-compiled and regression tested.(If you change the interface to a called
module by one byte, EVERYTHING has to be recompiled... )

In cases 2 and 3 above everything that DOESN'T use the new facilities
continues to function exactly as before and does NOT require to be
re-compiled. (Irrespective of whether the system uses static or dynamic
linking).

OO and Classes allow you to thoroughly test aspects of your system then
"extend" them without having to go back to square one and rewrite. The
system can be developed on a "trial and error" basis, with successful
functionality being "trickled out"to the Users rather than  them having to
wait 9 moths to get EVERYTHING, and then find it is something like what was
wanted 9 months ago, but is no longer relevant because the Business has
moved on in the face of a dynamic market place...
This approach lends itself to RAD much more than the Waterfall, and that is
why most mainframe installations (that have had the Waterfall ingrained in
them over the last 40 years) don't understand the advantages of it.

4. Using non-OO structured code has some inherent safety flaws. Because the
data structure is separate from the code that accesses it, maintenance
programmers can bypass an installation standard routine and simply write
their own code instead. (Sometimes this happens by accident, because the new
bloke simply didn't know there was an installation standard routine for
calculating pricing plans (for example) and did a whole series of complex
calculations that took several hours (of his time, by the time he ran around
and gathered the information he needed to do it...) and duplicated existing
code, and was not entirely correct because there were exceptions that the
analyst he spoke to about it wasn't aware of, and 9 months later in the
middle of the night... <many reading this have been there...>. And the poor
sod who was called out expected the installation standard routine to have
been used and spent an hour looking for a non-existent bug in the standard
routine, then finally realised the calling program did its own calculations
and for the past 9 months the wrong pricing plans have been used... <G>)

Can't happen with OO because the data and the methods are combined and
encapsulated. The Pricing plan object contains it's own code to manipulate
its own data structure. Rather than having to know that there is an
installation standard module, if you want a pricing plan you instantiate the
pricing plan object and invoke one of its methods. (This is second nature to
OO programmers and is consistent across all OO systems.)

OK, it isn't the end of the world if it is a wrong pricing plan, but suppose
it had been a data structure that was controlling heavy machinery and people
were working on it or near it...?

I have no intention of re-running another Religious war in CLC.

I really don't care whether you use OO or not...I am not on commission here
and I have no axe to grind. The reason I am posting is because it gets
tiresome to see a campaign against something that the campaigners THINK they
understand ("Well it's just modular structured programming re-invented,
right?") when their posts demonstrate that they really have no idea.

OOP is not a silver bullet or panacea for bad programming, but I like it and
I use it. As my entire working life has been dealing with computers (I
started programming in 1965), it is fair to say that when it comes to
programming, I know my way around.

(My experience covers 7 different brands of mainframe  (IBM, ICL, and the
BUNCH), countless PC platforms, including n-tier networks and client/server
and I am fluent in 4 different Assemblers, 3 High level languages, 9
different Operating systems (excluding versions), and a plethora of
application products and tools. As far as experience goes, my full CV is
over 30 pages and my career covers 37 years all over the World...)

I don't believe I have been "conned" into using OO...<G>  And there is no
way now that I would go back to structured programming.

The final and, for me at least, MOST significant advantage of OO...

5. OO enables the construction of components. And THAT is the Keys to the
Kingdom...

Pete.

I would appreciate your providing a recent reference to
someone, in this group, using "modular structured
programming" with reference to OO, other than your own use.

I can think of no reason why you would post this statement,
unless you were on a crusade. First you set up, what appears
for all purposes, a straw man; then you knock it down. Who are
you disagreeing with? <g>

> I don't believe I have been "conned" into using OO...<G>  And there is no
> way now that I would go back to structured programming.

It is not "technically" correct to contrast structured programming
and object-oriented programming. One proper contrast is
structured design versus object-oriented design; another is
procedural programming versus modular programming versus
object-oriented programming. (See Stroustrup, "The C++
Programming Language," Third Edition, �2.2 ff.)

Note that both, modular programming and object-oriented
programming, hide data; but they do so, differently. They
exist in nearly the same form in non-pure OO languages because
they both came from the same roots. If one wishes to view a single
level class as a module, that is not unreasonable. Furthermore,
each is accessed through an "interface" making their use quite
similar. Externally, they are nearly the same, internally they are
different. <G>

I find it tiresome to see the use of "structured programming"
to mean more than it does, and to see the use of "object-oriented
programming" to mean less than it does. <G>
```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:avn8bo$3hp$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>

no
> > > way now that I would go back to structured programming.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Isn't most OO CoBOL also structured?

Structured programming is a means of ordering code
into single-entry single-exit blocks (structures) and applies
to all imperative languages without regard to the means
used to decompose a design. It occurs often that some
use "structured programming" to mean more than Dijkstra
said.

I believe that Pete, probably, meant, at least technically,
that he would not go back to "procedural programming."
Most existing COBOL programs are based on the
procedural "paradigm". Even the term "procedure" is
defined as part of the COBOL standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T14:05:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avn925$9f0$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com> <v1u7oltm5q2m8e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick,
  You may have missed the ORIGINAL note in this thread that said (and this
is ALL that it said),

"I would like to find an example of a couple or three IBM mainframe CoBOL
programs that I can compile, run, and show others that use OO.  Preferably,
when
I show them to others, they will see the advantage over using called
routines."

    ***

Therefore, I think that Pete's reply that compared OO to "modular
programming" is totally valid.  I'll admitted that Howard used the term
"called routines" - but if that isn't the same (or at least VERY SIMILAR to)
"modular programming" then I don't see the significance.

I *do* think that contrasting "structured" versus "OO" programming is *not*
a fair comparison and was NOT what Howard originally asked about.
Therefore, if your objection was to Pete adding the word "structured", that
seems totally reasonable.

P.S.  In case it isn't clear to others, in my view
   OO can be compared to modular (although it is totally "conforming" in
COBOL to mix the two)
   Structured can/should be compared to unstructured (aka "spaghetti" code -
which can be created in OO or modular)

OO is also (sometimes) compared to "procedural" code.  I (personally) find
this confusing - as the way that COBOL works, OO code is still
"procedural" - while things like Report Writer and Validate (part of the
2002 Standard) are "data driven" in COBOL which is what I would (normally)
contrast with "procedural".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T17:32:17-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<v1uhrj8gm73qd3@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com> <v1u7oltm5q2m8e@corp.supernews.com> <avn925$9f0$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:avn925$9f0$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> Rick,
>   You may have missed the ORIGINAL note in this thread that said (and this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs that I can compile, run, and show others that use OO.
Preferably,
> when
> I show them to others, they will see the advantage over using called
> routines."
>
>     ***

I didn't miss it!

> Therefore, I think that Pete's reply that compared OO to "modular
> programming" is totally valid.  I'll admitted that Howard used the term
> "called routines" - but if that isn't the same (or at least VERY SIMILAR
to)
> "modular programming" then I don't see the significance.

This is perhaps one of those touchy areas that depends upon
who one quotes. Parnas in "On the Criteria to be Used in
Decomposing Systems into Modules," 1972, provides a
description of modular programming from Gouthier and Pont
which states, in part, "A well-defined segmentation of the
project effort ensures system modularity. Each task forms
a separate, distinct program module. At implementation
time each module and its inputs and outputs are well-defined."

Armstrong in "Modular Programming in COBOL," 1973, states,
"We have identified, then, three types of modularity:
"1. Applicational--involved with the business at hand, ...
"2. Procedural--concering the basic computing tasks, ...
"3. Structural--the separation of data, code and control."
Of structural modularity, Armstrong writes, "A given module may
be conceived of as consisting of three basic parts: data,
code (or process), and control. Data enter the module as
input, are operated upon by the code, and leave as output."

Both of these early references show that concepts of modular
programming were simple data flow transformations or
process isolation which allowed for extensive testing.

During the 1980s a change in usage ocurred in conjunction
with new languages such as C, Modula, Ada, etc. McConnell
in "Code Complete," 1993, says, "A module is a collection
of data and the routines that act on that data. A module might
also be a collection of routines that provides a cohesive set
of services even if no common data is involved."

Stroustrup in "The C++ Programming Language," 1997,
writes, "Over the years, the emphasis in the design of programs
has shifted from the design of procedures and toward the
organization of data. Among other things, this reflects an
increase in program size. A set of related procedures with
the data they manipulate is often called a module."

Hence, the significance lies in how one defines a module,
old or new. A "called routine" is modular programming
in the old sense. My reference is to modular programming
in the new sense. And in this new sense modular
programming is similar to simple uses of object-oriented
programming.

> I *do* think that contrasting "structured" versus "OO" programming is
*not*
> a fair comparison and was NOT what Howard originally asked about.
> Therefore, if your objection was to Pete adding the word "structured",
that
> seems totally reasonable.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Structured can/should be compared to unstructured (aka "spaghetti"
code -
> which can be created in OO or modular)

Unstructured? This was discussed recently. The "politically
correct" term is non-structured!

> OO is also (sometimes) compared to "procedural" code.  I (personally) find
> this confusing - as the way that COBOL works, OO code is still
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T14:35:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301101435.1560aaaa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote

> 1. Classes can be instantiated as often as required and therefore cope with
> recursive processing much better than called modules. Yes, with the latest
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> so I admit this one is weak...<G>)

Recursion is only a special case of re-entrancy, it is
self-re-entrancy.  Re-entrancy is the actual reason that LOCAL-STORAGE
was introduced - for Windows API programs.

While Classes can be instantiated as often as required, each instance
still needs some form of storage for the object handle.  This means
that there is still a requirement for, say, an array that can hold all
the instances. There is also a need for Local-storage in re-entrant
programs where the object handle needs to be stored per iteration.

> 2. You can easily extend a class without having to regression test
> everything that uses it. Only objects that use the new or overridden method
> need be tested.  (If you modify a called module EVERYTHING that uses it
> needs to be reviewed.)

This may be an advantage in the short term.  For a module that may be
quite volitile over a long term each overridden method in a new class
is in a new branch that could become a tangled web of weeds that is
completely unmanageable.

Of course this mechanism of deriving a new module does not require OO.
 It could be done quite easily by creating a new module that
intercepts new functions and passes all unchanged requests back to the
original module.

> 3. You can add a new interface to a class without having to re-compile every
> module that calls it. Only modules that use the new interface need be
> re-compiled and regression tested.

You can add a new function to a called module without ... 

> (If you change the interface to a called
> module by one byte, EVERYTHING has to be recompiled... )

That depends entirely on what is changed.

> In cases 2 and 3 above everything that DOESN'T use the new facilities
> continues to function exactly as before and does NOT require to be
> re-compiled. (Irrespective of whether the system uses static or dynamic
> linking).

And this is different from carefully crafted modules in what way ?

> OO and Classes allow you to thoroughly test aspects of your system then
> "extend" them without having to go back to square one and rewrite. The
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> them over the last 40 years) don't understand the advantages of it.

This is a design issue more than it is an OO programming issue.  In
order to have a good OO system it is necessary to start by
deconstructing the system down into its objects and to specifiy the
functionality that is to be applied to the object.  Given the design
then building the system may be done with OO programming syntax or
with some other set of standards.

> 4. Using non-OO structured code has some inherent safety flaws. Because the
  [contrived example ommitted]
 
> Can't happen with OO because the data and the methods are combined and
> encapsulated. The Pricing plan object contains it's own code to manipulate
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> OO programmers and is consistent across all OO systems.)

While this may be true in a development environment I have heard
stories about OO systems some way down the track where the class
hierarchy becomes so convoluted (due to new classes being derived for
each change request to save having to retest existing classes - see
above) that it becomes easier to rewrite a new class rather than
trying to untangle the existing ones.

Again short term gains - long term pains.
 
> OOP is not a silver bullet or panacea for bad programming, 

No, but it is a silver bullet for system design and it helps to
implement that design directly.

> 5. OO enables the construction of components. And THAT is the Keys to the
> Kingdom...

Many languages have had to fit themselves into a language neutral
mechanism for calling components and the OO facilities added to
existing languages was an excuse for changing to some other
interfacing mechanism - that of the other OO languages.

In the 1960s the ICL 1900 series had a single routine calling
mechanism that allowed 'components' written in any language to be
called from any other.  Since then there has been increasing anarchy
where languages became increasingly incompatible, especially on PCs. 
Now there is some co-operation where mechanisms exist to interface to
some standard, these are usually incorporated into the OO mechanisms -
for example using name mangling and/or dynamic redirection tables.

However, the interfacing can be built and used without needing the OO
mechanisms, these just make it easier, and usually automatic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-14T14:35:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v28p19imvkjh68@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com>`

```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com...
[...]
> OO and Classes allow you to thoroughly test aspects of your system then
> "extend" them without having to go back to square one and rewrite. The
> system can be developed on a "trial and error" basis, with successful
> functionality being "trickled out"to the Users rather than  them having to
> wait 9 moths to get EVERYTHING, and then find it is something like what
was
> wanted 9 months ago, but is no longer relevant because the Business has
> moved on in the face of a dynamic market place...
> This approach lends itself to RAD much more than the Waterfall, and that
is
> why most mainframe installations (that have had the Waterfall ingrained in
> them over the last 40 years) don't understand the advantages of it.

Pete, could you please clarify your use of OO.

OO, object-orientation, covers analysis, OOA; design, OOD;
and programming, OOP. The areas of analysis and design
include formal notions of these processes including several
different methods for presenting designs graphically.

RAD is, basically, an informal means for providing solutions
by using ideas from any source, such as different software
development methodologies. Hence, use of RAD could
include ideas from SA and OOA, SD and OOD, and these
may be implemented using ideas from PP, MP, OOP, etc.

OO is formal. RAD is informal. It is not possible to accomplish
the formal by informal means; therefore, one cannot use RAD
to do OO!

I believe this is a source of much of the disagreement; that is,
some of your comments appear to conflict with ideas from
object-oriented design.


On the subjects "trial and error" and "trickled out", similar
issues were discussed in Barry Boehm's article, "The Spiral
Model of Software Development and Enhancement," 1988.
It is offered for your information and consideration.

"... The above concerns [failure of waterfall to address user
requirements, quickly] led to the formation of the evolutionary
development model(4), whose stages consist of expanding
increments of an operational software product, with the
directions of evolution being determined by operational
experience.

"The evolutionary development model is ideally matched to
a fourth-generation language application and well matched to
situations in which users say, 'I can't tell you what I want, but
I'll know it when I see it.' It gives users a rapid initial operational
capability and provides a realistic operational basis for
determining subsequent product improvements.

"Nonetheless, evolutionary development also has its difficulties.
It is generally difficult to distinguish it from the old code-and-fix
model, whose spaghetti code and lack of planning were the
motivation for the waterfall model. ..."

"4. D.D. McCracken and M.A. Jackson, "Life-Cycle Concept
Considered Harmful," ACM Software Engineering Notes, Apr.
1982, pp. 29-32."

Incidentally, waterfall was 1970. It was a refinement of stagewise,
which originated in 1956. (According the same Boehm article.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T01:08:17
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e24b4ee_4@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e1e13ec_7@news.newsgroups.com> <v28p19imvkjh68@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Sorry Rick.

I'd like to debate this with you but I honestly don't have the time. There
is too much in it.

I have watched your posts here over some time and have respect for your
knowledge and capability, but I believe (and this is not intended to be
personal or offensive) that your approach is based on what you have studied
rather than what you have actually used.  I say this because of your
continual (and perfectly legitimate) use of the argument from Authority.

I've always had a problem with that <G>.

It's not that I don't respect Authorities or that I haven't done the formal
stuff, it's just that I'd rather be in the real world actually doing it. I
won't hesitate to change "accepted practice" (even so-called "best
practice") if I can see an advantage in doing so. Having had a lifetime of
experience (37 years) in the field gives me the confidence to do so and
challenge the "Authority" if I please. I'm not evangelising MY way; on the
contrary, I have a very laid back approach to what other people do, but I
will do whatever I believe it takes, whether that conforms to the theory or
not. The fact that what I am doing may be "heresy" has never stopped me and
I hope it never will...

Our definitions are at variance and that also makes debate difficult.

I accept your point that OO is formal but MY use of it isn't. (in fact,
there is very little formalism in my life; that's why the image of me in a
Tuxedo is intended to be a joke...<G>). I use the essentials of RAD
(empowerment, JDW, iteration, prototyping where applicable, prioritisation,
timeboxing, and UAT jointly by Development and the end users). It is very
successful. I am a freelance and I'm not looking for work. I have a track
record of satisfied Blue Chip companies.

In my current Management role I am encouraging Analysts to use RAD for small
"Quick Win" situations where we can deliver something useful to the Business
as quickly as possible. We have run several of these projects and they have
been extremely successful. The programmers didn't need to be inducted into
OO OR RAD, as they are all using it and dealing with Objects and Object
technology every day.We also make use of components which we build ourselves
using OO. The main tools are SQL server, ORACLE, VB, and Standard solutions
like spreadsheets. Nearly everything is Web enabled using XML and all the
other alphabet soup that goes with it.

As I'm sure you are aware, RAD enables functions to be "trickled out" of the
continuing development process so that the Users can get hold of stuff as it
becomes available and meets the requirements. My comment about OO was that
it lends itself to this approach. You can define a Class, use it, then
extend it later to increase its functionality. As trial and error is the
basis of  an iterated RAD approach, so can classes and OO be used  to
implement this iterative approach,  in my opinion, more easily than a
methodology that is based on sign-offs and not starting a phase until the
previous one is complete.

I find your statement that it is impossible to implement RAD and OO
together, to be quite incomprehensible. (However, I understand why you made
it and the basis on which you arrived at it. )

I'm afraid that this is, for me, a bit like the aeronatical engineer and the
bumblebee...<G>

You will say that I couldn't  possibly have used REAL OO (or else I couldn't
possibly have used REAL RAD...).

That's OK too. I've banked the cheques...<G>

There are a few further comments below.

And that is really as much as I'm prepared to say on the subject <G>.

Pete.

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:v28p19imvkjh68@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > functionality being "trickled out"to the Users rather than  them having
to
> > wait 9 moths to get EVERYTHING, and then find it is something like what
> was
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > why most mainframe installations (that have had the Waterfall ingrained
in
> > them over the last 40 years) don't understand the advantages of it.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And combinations of these that have been found to work and are not too
onerous to produce.... This is what I meant when I said above that our
definitions differ.

> RAD is, basically, an informal means for providing solutions
> by using ideas from any source, such as different software
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


> OO is formal. RAD is informal.

The DSDM consortium would take issue with this as their RAD is quite
formalised (and very useful...). However, I agree very much with the spirit
of what you are saying and as long as the fundamental RAD principles of
empowerment, iteration, joint working, prioritisation, and timeboxing are
observed, I would have no problem with it.

> It is not possible to accomplish
> the formal by informal means; therefore, one cannot use RAD
> to do OO!
>

Well Gee, Rick, I guess we better recall all those successful systems that
we used a "RAD-Like" Methodology to implement our OO code with....<G>

So you don't buy "informal formality"? An oxymoron? I don't think so... what
about using the best of the formal ideas but using them in a way that fits
most comfortably into the actual environment? Not necessarily implementing
everything that would be required by a rigourous approach? If we can have
rigourous formality then we can have relaxed (informal?) formality. See, it
is NOT the "going by the book" that is really important (at least it isn't
in the real world...) it is the achievement of results, and that is
sometimes achieved by cutting through some of the formalism... We're not
writing our PhD theses here, we are trying to implement functionality in the
most speedy and cost effective way possible.



> I believe this is a source of much of the disagreement; that is,
> some of your comments appear to conflict with ideas from
> object-oriented design.
>
Boy, I really hope so! And I have no problem whatsoever with
disagreement...<G>

>
> On the subjects "trial and error" and "trickled out", similar
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am familiar with Boehm's article and the spiral (evolutionary) model,
which I endorse. I disagree with the bit about it leading to spaghetti code
which you quoted below. It CAN do, but it doesn't HAVE to. Just because it
says it does in this article certainly doesn't mean it does in real life.


> "... The above concerns [failure of waterfall to address user
> requirements, quickly] led to the formation of the evolutionary
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not when I do it, it isn't...<G>

> "4. D.D. McCracken and M.A. Jackson, "Life-Cycle Concept
> Considered Harmful," ACM Software Engineering Notes, Apr.
> 1982, pp. 29-32."
>
My two least liked names in IT <G>. I remember teaching COBOL in the late
60s with McCracken's book as the "approved" text. I found it boring, dry,
and de-motivating. Compare it with Fritz McCameron (PhD) or Thane's COBOL in
24 hours... I went to a seminar in London with Michael Jackson when he was
first promoting  JSP... You can imagine my reaction to it...<G>


> Incidentally, waterfall was 1970. It was a refinement of stagewise,
> which originated in 1956. (According the same Boehm article.)
>

Well, I never used stagewise, but I did use the Waterfall in 1970. And many
sites used it (in one form or another) from then, up until the present day.

I hate it, and make no apology for doing so. I hate it because it delivers
too little too late, and is an excuse for bureaucrats to keep their arses
covered ("Well, sorry, you signed it off. If you want it changed now we'll
have to take another 3 months..."). Bullshit. RAD is dynamic and
incorporates change, even as the model is developing. This is much closer to
real life where things change very rapidly

Hi:

Howard, give up.

Even the self-vaunted Dashwood won't send you anything.

Remember the Great Programming Contest a couple of years ago?
This would have let anybody design and program a simple application
using their chosen methodology. This was a perfect opportunity
for anyone to demonstrate OO.  Nobody entered the contest except me.
For anyone with an interest, read about it at foodman123.com/tcs.

Why did no-one enter the contest using OO, why not?  Why don't
they do it now. Are they ashamed, do they know how, or what?

opportunity for, perhaps, Mr. Dashwood, to demonstrate to
everyone how OO works.  Come on, Dashwood, you can do it.
(Or, can you?)

Has anyone, anywhere, ever, developed a real live business application
and actually implemented it using OO?  Tell us about it, please.

Let's face it, OO is the invention of the academia nuts who have
to justify their existence developing solutions to problems which
they don't understand. (Also see thread 'The Wrong People'.)

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
