##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe OO

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T09:46:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E205885.1050409@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <93dfc7c7.0301110458.5b23df87@posting.google.com>`

```


Anthony Dilworth wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<avhnc6$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Tony, I do not have time to enter contests. I have a living to make. 
The last contest I entered, I spent 1500 hours on.  I won the Canadian 
shareware contest sponsered by Fujitsu.  I won a T-shirt, which they 
stiffed me for. That was back in the 80's sometime.  I sold about 500 
copies of the program, had another 10,000 stollen thanks to the contest, 
and made about $3500 for the 1500 hours of work.  You wanna play 
programmng contest shit, go find a gullible student.

> Has anyone, anywhere, ever, developed a real live business application
> and actually implemented it using OO?  Tell us about it, please.
> 

I have two.  One was a half million dollar continental project, 
basically covering Houston to norhern Ontario, but not going west of the 
mississippi.  The side we were talking to was SAP.  Our side was Fujitsu 
OOP Cobol.  We were the only portion of the entire project that was 
always ahead of schedule.

The other is my own truck dispatch system, also written in OOP Fujitsu 
Cobol.  I have made my living from that package for twenty years now, 
and I converted it to OOP about 4 years back.

Both packages use Bob Wolfe's SP2 for the screens.  His packages 
generates Cobol code that is copied into Cobol, and normally called.  I 
simply changed the script file to generate the code as screen objects 
and made the parameters properties.  That made everything completely 
re-entrant by allowing every screen to invoke another instance of any 
other screen, including itself.  Now I am starting to turn that package 
into a component that can be used in accounting systems.


> Let's face it, OO is the invention of the academia nuts who have
> to justify their existence developing solutions to problems which
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony Dilworth

Ok.  You go your way, and I will go mine.  Howvever, that contest stuff 
was written in 8086 assembler.  The number of lines of code is roughly 
the same as that SAP project mentioned above.  I've benn programming now 
since 1967, and professionally as a degreed comp sci grad since 1973. I 
started with Cobol in 68, and actually met Grace.  Grace thought in 
components.  A Cobol program itself is set up as an object, with each 
sub-object within it being a precisely defined entity with precicely 
with defined attributes.  It is just taking the rest of us fourty years 
to catch up.

The truth of the matter is that I have a whole bunch of shitty code, 
right now, but I have learned how to use the tools, and am now starting 
to think in those terms.  That takes a while, and it was not until the 
80's that you sae even standard Cobol really get going in Var apps. 
Probably the Altos was the first, and I think that was 1980's, 20 years 
after Grace started.

Ignore new technology at your peril, Tony.  You will get left behind.

Donald
```

<a id="t-0cb59f7979"></a>
## Thread: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3 — 4 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2002-05](2002-05.md#t-0cb59f7979)._

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** dracoolio <member22040@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T00:31:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2364844.1042072280@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de>`

```

Numbers are just symbols that were arbitrarily set up on this planet.

Hexadecimal is base 16: 10 in HEX is really 16 in decimal format
Binary is base 2
Decimal is base 10 (humans have 10 fingers - go figure !)

In order for civilization to survive, there must be order in everything.
That is why humans have set up standards for measuring anything to avoid
any kind of miscommunication between humans.

Even though all data is binary (bits are just 2-state switches: on or
off) as far as the computer is concerned, human beings find it easier to
represent it in HEX or hexadecimal.

A byte consists of 8 bits or 2 HEX digits (each HEX digit has 4 bits).
For example: 123 in decimal format is F1 F2 F3 in HEX format. This is
actually 3 bytes. Remember that each HEX digit is 4 bits, so F1 in
binary is 1111 0001. F2 is 1111 0010. F3 1111 0011. Notice that each bit
is a power of 2 (from 2 to the zero which is 1 in deciaml up to 2 to the
3 which is 8 in decimal). The example I just showed is really a COBOL
PIC 9(3) field or a display numeric field.

On the other hand, a COBOL S9(9)V99 COMP-3 is a packed decimal field and
this is 2 bytes. Let me explain why. A packed decimal field is
represented as HEX digits followed by a C or D as the last HEX digit for
the sign. In this case, S9 means it is a signed numeric field. The
(9)V99 means that this number is composed of 1 integral position
followed by 2 decimal places. The V in the V99 is implies that there are
decimal places to follow. For example: If the number is +3.14, it is
represented as HEX 31 4C which is 2 bytes. If this was -3.14, it is now
HEX 31 4D. The C means positive (it could also be an F) while D is
negative. Keep in mind that the decimal point is implied and does not
take up space. A COMP-3 field is used for computations, so any numeric
field in COBOL always gets converted to a COMP-3 field (if it was a PIC
9(9)V99 display field) no matter what. That is why COBOL programs should
always use a signed COMP-3 field when computing efficiently. The
programs will run faster if you practice this.

In ASSEMBLER code, the instruction to do this is a PACK instruction.
What this does is convert a display numeric field into a PACKED DECIMAL
field or COMP-3 in COBOL before doing any numeric comparisons or
computations.

There is a numeric PIC S9(9) COMP field or binary field, but I will not
go into this. Suffice to say that this is useful for subscripting
purposes (addressability) when dealing with tables or arrays.

Dinosaurs still rule !
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T02:00:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4J4T9.1258$z36.38393040@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <2364844.1042072280@dbforums.com>`

```
If you can fit a PIC S9(9)V99 field into 2 bytes, you're a better man than
I; it takes me 6 bytes.  :)  Also, I believe that binary is sometimes better
than packed decimal for calculations.  It depends upon the number of digits
and the calculation involved.  Either one is better than display.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T13:55:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wbfT9.6367$qU5.5000266@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <2364844.1042072280@dbforums.com> <4J4T9.1258$z36.38393040@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:4J4T9.1258$z36.38393040@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com...
> Also, I believe that binary is sometimes better
> than packed decimal for calculations.  It depends upon the number of
digits
> and the calculation involved.

math package option selected at compile time.

"In general" though, either is better than USAGE IS DISPLAY for arithmetic.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "Dietmar Volkmann" <dvolkman@ford.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T14:02:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avjrtj$rbk8@eccws12.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <2364844.1042072280@dbforums.com>`

```
1.The number of bytes required for a packed-decimal item containing n
decimal digits is INT((n+2)/2). E.g. n=2-3 needs 2 byte, n=10-11 needs 6
bytes.
(from C. Baroudi "Mastering Cobol")

2. For a binary data item the most efficient code is generated if the item
has a sign and has 8 digits or lower.
When the data item is larger then 8 items or is also used with display, the
PACHED-DECIMAL is a good choice.
The most efficient code for PACKED-DECIMAL is generated if the item has a
sign and an odd number of digits
(http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/library, COBOL for
OS/390 & VM: Programming guide; 4.6.1.2 Data Usage)

Happy new year!

Dietmar
```

## Thread: COBOL COmpilers for Tuxedo8.0 on Windows2000 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COBOL COmpilers for Tuxedo8.0 on Windows2000

- **From:** Ganeshmuruga <member22105@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T11:50:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2366317.1042113015@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi,
    I am using tuxedo8.0 calls wrapped in cobol on windows2000 and the
    recommended cobol compiler for this is Net Express from Micro Focus.
    Is there any other compiler for cobol available as a trial version
    or a free download supporting the above.

I am able to compile the programs using mainframe express but it is not
recognised by tuxedo.

Ganesh
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COmpilers for Tuxedo8.0 on Windows2000

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no_dgallan1_spam@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T02:16:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8LT9.7047$2z1.2050@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2366317.1042113015@dbforums.com>`

```
If I recall the MF samples correctly, the trick was setting the
call-convention for the tux api calls. If another compiler can setup the
call the same way then I dont see why it shouldn't work. Of course, you also
have to find the right pieces of the COBOL support to include as part of the
TUXEDO buildserver process.
```

```
As I do not know anything about Tuxedo, I can only say it may be
possible to use RM/COBOL V7 to do it. It will imply creating a C
interface following a very specific construct. If you can supply the
list of sub-routines (call's), and their parameters, then this is
duable, and may prove less expensive than MF.

FF
```

<a id="t-3381079e0e"></a>
## Thread: How do I read and write bits? — 1 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-3381079e0e)._

#### ↳ Re: How do I read and write bits?

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T11:54:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0301091154.644cb967@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d7d0c297.0212301352.43889aaa@posting.google.com>`

```
Interesting problem. Here's 2 possible approaches for your
consideration:

The first will "flip" one specific bit in a specified byte. The other
will scan 8 bytes of data and determine the "on/off" status of each
bit in the string. In the code supplied here I've "flipped" each bit
in the string. The routine can be used to selectively "flip" the bits,
or, if the string represents an option list or some such, it can be
used to process the options.

Because the code hasn't been tested, the usual caveats and copouts
apply. I don't know how this will "wrap", but here goes.....

FLIPONE.....


01  WS-WORK-AREAS.
    05  BIT-NBR                 PIC  9(04) COMP.
    05  BIT-FLIPPER             PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE 0.

    05  FLD-1.
        10  1ST-CHK-BYTE        PIC  X(01).
            88  CHK-BIT-IS-ON   VALUE X'80' THRU X'FF'.
            88  CHK-BIT-IS-OFF  VALUE X'00' THRU X'7F'.
        10  FILLER              PIC  X(01).
    05  CHK-BITS                REDEFINES
        FLD-1                   PIC  9(04) COMP.

    05  CHG-BITS                PIC  9(04) COMP.

    05  BIT-SHIFT-VALUES.
        10  BIT-1               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE   1.
        10  BIT-2               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE   2.
        10  BIT-3               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE   4.
        10  BIT-4               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE   8.
        10  BIT-5               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE  16.
        10  BIT-6               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE  32.
        10  BIT-7               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE  64.
        10  BIT-8               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE 128.
    05  BIT-SHIFT-TBL           REDEFINES
        BIT-SHIFT-VALUES.
        10  SHIFT-ENT           PIC  9(04) COMP
                                OCCURS 8.


    MOVE     your data  TO CHK-BITS(1:1)
                           CHG-BITS(1:1)
    MOVE     bit nbr    TO BIT-NBR
    MOVE     ZEROS      TO BIT-FLIPPER
    MOVE     X'80'      TO BIT-FLIPPER(1:1)
    COMPUTE  CHK-BITS    = CHK-BITS    * SHIFT-ENT(BIT-NBR)
    COMPUTE  BIT-FLIPPER = BIT-FLIPPER / SHIFT-ENT(BIT-NBR)

    EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN CHK-BIT-IS-ON
         SUBTRACT BIT-FLIPPER FROM CHG-BITS
    WHEN CHK-BIT-IS-OFF
         ADD BIT-FLIPPER        TO CHG-BITS
    WHEN OTHER
         DISPLAY 'HOUSTON WE HAVE A PROBLEM'
         STOP RUN
    MOVE CHG-BITS(1:1)  TO your receiving area
    .

FLIPALL.....


01  WS-WORK-AREAS.
    05  FLD-1.
        10  1ST-CHK-BYTE        PIC  X(01).
            88  CHK-BIT-IS-ON   VALUE X'80' THRU X'FF'.
            88  CHK-BIT-IS-OFF  VALUE X'00' THRU X'7F'.
        10  FILLER              PIC  X(07).
    05  CHK-BITS-64             REDEFINES
        FLD-1                   PIC  9(10) COMP.

    05  CHG-BITS-64             PIC  9(10) COMP.
    05  BIT-FLIPPER             PIC  9(10) COMP VALUE ZEROS.
    05  LOOP-CNTL               PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE ZEROS.
    05  BIT-LOC                 PIC  9(04) COMP VALUE ZEROS.
        88  ONE-BIT             VALUE 1 THRU 8.
        88  BIT-STRING          VALUE 0.
        88  ERROR               VALUE 9 THRU 65535.



    MOVE     your data    TO CHK-BITS-64(1:nbr of bytes)
                             CHG-BITS-64(1:nbr of bytes)
    MOVE     nbr of bits  TO LOOP-CNTL
    MOVE     ZEROS        TO BIT-FLIPPER
    MOVE     X'80'        TO BIT-FLIPPER(1:1)

    PERFORM  XXXX-CHK-A-BIT  LOOP-CNTL TIMES
    MOVE CHG-BITS-64(1:nbr of bytes)
      TO your receiving area
    .
XXXX-CHK-A-BIT.
    EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN CHK-BIT-IS-ON
         SUBTRACT BIT-FLIPPER FROM CHG-BITS-64
    WHEN CHK-BIT-IS-OFF
         ADD BIT-FLIPPER        TO CHG-BITS-64
    WHEN OTHER
         DISPLAY 'HOUSTON WE HAVE A PROBLEM'
         STOP RUN
    END-EVALUATE
    COMPUTE  CHK-BITS-64 = CHK-BITS-64          * 2
    COMPUTE  BIT-FLIPPER = BIT-FLIPPER          / 2
    .








vibroplex@mindspring.com (Derek Cohn/WB0TUA) wrote in message news:<d7d0c297.0212301352.43889aaa@posting.google.com>...

> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Looking for some ideals for shareware products — 5 msgs

### Looking for some ideals for shareware products

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T17:34:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avktec$5c1$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```
It seems I have some free time on my hands...I'd like to keep myself busy
rather than sitting around...I've been trying to come up with some good
ideals on shareware products...Something to sell for less than say $50.00 a
pop, but at the same time sell a lot of them...

Anyone have any ideals?
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for some ideals for shareware products

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-09T23:38:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1E07BF.B0A2070@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<avktec$5c1$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```


Ken Mullins wrote:

> It seems I have some free time on my hands...I'd like to keep myself busy
> rather than sitting around...I've been trying to come up with some good
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have any ideals?

Ken,

Ignoring that runtime fee problem <G>. This sort of touches on that other topic
Components. Call it what you like. Appointment List, Booking List and the
Calendar feature (class TimePicker in N/E)..

Whether you are talking, beauty/hairdressing  salons, dentists, medics,
chiropractors, veterinarians, lawyers, accounting firms...... the list is
almost endless - they all have a requirement for a 'booking application'.
Agreed there are differences, but essentially the requirement is the same - the
ability to flip backwards and forwards through an appointment register and book
slices of time to potential 'customers'.

I call my own family GP a computer freak - I've told him so <G>. The only pen I
ever see in his hand is a light pen !

Problem is - not sure it will jive with an API approach - I think it would have
to be OO-based sending and receiving messages, allowing COBOL to interface with
other languages. Perhaps you should take a look at the N/E Java classes, and
the White Papers on Java use. There are some very sophisticated components out
there already, which also link to DBs - but can't see any harm in one being
available in COBOL.

PS: Picky, picky. It's not exactly shareware if you want to price it - but I
understand what you are saying

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```


#### ↳ Re: Looking for some ideals for shareware products

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T10:48:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301101048.1c5d697c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<avktec$5c1$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```
"Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in message news:<avktec$5c1$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>...
> It seems I have some free time on my hands...I'd like to keep myself busy
> rather than sitting around...I've been trying to come up with some good
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have any ideals?

As this is comp.lang.cobol I'll throw out one of my wishlist items
that I never seem to have enough time to write, but would happily pay
<$50.00 if it was available.

A Cobol source pretty-printer and/or html-izer that is not dialect
specific.  Possibly with a plug-in extension architecture for user
written munging.

Of the two features I would consider an html-izer to be more valuable.
 I often like to post example code as part of system documentation,
but it can be painful and time consuming to convert a program into
good html (variables as hotlinks to defs, procedure names as hotlinks,
etc).  More often than not I end up wrapping each line in a set of
&ltcode&gt&lt/code&gt tags saying "I'll get back to it later".

I often wish I had a pretty-printer that I could use as a preprocessor
for version compares to make it easier to identify what is really
different and what just got reformatted/uppercased/name-changed/etc.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger


BTW -- I know about CobolTransformer and CobClean.  But CobClean
charges $350, FAICT, to add flower boxen and remove sequence numbers. 
CobolTransformer would easily do what I want for the price of a small
vehicle.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for some ideals for shareware products

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T04:14:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2b927$d56dc840$65ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<avktec$5c1$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <dcedb8d0.0301101048.1c5d697c@posting.google.com>`

```
If you have ROSCOE I have a RPF that removes line numbers form columns 1 to
6.  ROSCOE also has a line command: nc which stands for "note Cobol" which
will make flower boxes.
```

```
If I understand what you're asking for, then BEA's WebLogic server may be
the ticket. They had a Jolt api that allowed java code to call TUXEDO
services (and probably the reverse but I don't know). I think in a recent or
upcoming release of WebLogic that Jolt was going to be replaced by something
else. Of course, that's the most direct route to Tuxedo from java. I would
assume there are other ways to get there but I don't know them.
```

<a id="t-989098aa3c"></a>
## Thread: Question on closing files — 28 msgs

### Question on closing files

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T08:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Had an odd question from a co-worker today.

First, we have two Z900 model 2064's.  We've been on OS/390 2.10 since
last summer, and expect to upgrade to z/OS 1.4 next summer.  Current
compiler is IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.2.  We still have production
programs executing that were last compiled with VS COBOL II and COBOL
for MVS & VM.  We're pretty sure that we do not have programs running
that were last compiled with OS/VS COBOL.  And we only run LE/MVS as
the runtime library.

The programmer said he had been told that our COBOL programs will
abend when we upgrade to z/OS 1.4, if they do not close VSAM and QSAM
files.  I can't remember if this has been discussed in
comp.lang.cobol, and I am wondering if this is true.

Apparently, this programmer NEVER closes VSAM or QSAM files in batch
programs, and trusts COBOL, or the runtime environment, or the
operating system to do it for him.  I always close all my files before
terminating a batch program, and I do not endorse his shortcut.

Does anyone know if this is true?  Will my co-worker be forced to fix
all his programs to do what he should have been doing anyway?
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T12:47:45+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vt8t1v0n7ictcb7c8skvtdoscj34hp0j3i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Fri, 10 Jan 2003 08:13:00 GMT Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

:>Had an odd question from a co-worker today.

:>First, we have two Z900 model 2064's.  We've been on OS/390 2.10 since
:>last summer, and expect to upgrade to z/OS 1.4 next summer.  Current
:>compiler is IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.2.  We still have production
:>programs executing that were last compiled with VS COBOL II and COBOL
:>for MVS & VM.  We're pretty sure that we do not have programs running
:>that were last compiled with OS/VS COBOL.  And we only run LE/MVS as
:>the runtime library.

:>The programmer said he had been told that our COBOL programs will
:>abend when we upgrade to z/OS 1.4, if they do not close VSAM and QSAM
:>files.  I can't remember if this has been discussed in
:>comp.lang.cobol, and I am wondering if this is true.

:>Apparently, this programmer NEVER closes VSAM or QSAM files in batch
:>programs, and trusts COBOL, or the runtime environment, or the
:>operating system to do it for him.  I always close all my files before
:>terminating a batch program, and I do not endorse his shortcut.

:>Does anyone know if this is true?  Will my co-worker be forced to fix
:>all his programs to do what he should have been doing anyway?

The technical issue is where are the DCB's/ACB's located, who controls the
storage, etc.

You will get abends if:

1. The DCB/ACB is allocated in dynamic storage  and
2. LE is aware of the storage but is not aware that there are DCB's there  and
3. LE explicitly frees the storage at termination  and
4. The COBOL runtime is not aware that the program is ending and files need be
closed

I doubt that this is true, but it is possible.

At any rate I would suggest explicit closing of files. This would help if
there is an abend after the file should have been closed because when an abend
causes the CLOSE there may be some lost writes.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T10:05:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1ED31C.513B2908@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <vt8t1v0n7ictcb7c8skvtdoscj34hp0j3i@4ax.com>`

```
The COBOL 85 standard requires that the program close all open files
upon termination of the run unit (normally via STOP RUN).  How IBM
handles GOBACK I don't know.

Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 10 Jan 2003 08:13:00 GMT Arnold Trembley
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T07:26:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avmhmh$14c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Well, ....

Arnold, - as I recall, your site (for various performance reasons) may use
Assembler drivers or for various "functions".

In an all COBOL application, you will NOT see any ABENDs when you STOP RUN
(or GoBack from a main program) with OPEN files.  This is because ANSI
*requires* COBOL to work this way.  However, if you have used Assembler to
do various things, it *sometimes* possible to see such ABENDs.

If your application programmer doesn't believe this is the way COBOL works,
have them check out

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/6.2.36

which says,

 "The STOP RUN statement closes all files defined in any of the programs
comprising the run unit."

P.S.  I agree with those who say that this is a BAD feature to rely on.
Explicitly closing a file is ALWAYS a good idea.

P.P.S.  There is a separate "question" of file status "97" when your program
OPENs a VSAM file that wasn't "properly" closed by the last application that
used it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T07:39:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1FCA6F.C1899FE@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <avmhmh$14c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
First, I would like to thank everyone who replied.

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Well, ....
> 
> Arnold, - as I recall, your site (for various performance reasons) may use
> Assembler drivers or for various "functions".

Yes, that's true.  And it is apparently related to the problem.  

> 
> In an all COBOL application, you will NOT see any ABENDs when you STOP RUN
> (or GoBack from a main program) with OPEN files.  This is because ANSI
> *requires* COBOL to work this way.  However, if you have used Assembler to
> do various things, it *sometimes* possible to see such ABENDs.

The symptom is a C03 abend.  And they're definitely seeing them.  

> 
> If your application programmer doesn't believe this is the way COBOL works,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> comprising the run unit."

I am certain that COBOL will attempt to close any open files at
program termination, but if a file has not been closed AND its DCB has
been FREEMAIN'ed or overlaid, IBM says the close will fail.  

> 
> P.S.  I agree with those who say that this is a BAD feature to rely on.
> Explicitly closing a file is ALWAYS a good idea.

I will cut and paste some IBM APAR text that relates to this problem. 
But it's not a z/OS problem because the APAR was closed in 1990!  
  
> 
> P.P.S.  There is a separate "question" of file status "97" when your program
> OPENs a VSAM file that wasn't "properly" closed by the last application that
> used it.

I've encountered this problem ("97") at least once.  If I need to
deliberately ABEND a batch COBOL program, I try to close any open VSAM
files before forcing a storage dump.  It might save a verify on the
restart.

Here's the whole story.  Another department in my company provides
financial software to banks in COBOL source format.  Our COBOL source
program does not close the files (although it WOULD if I had written
it), instead relying on STOP RUN to close files automatically.  

One customer upgraded to z/OS (I don't know which version) and got C03
ABENDS.  Naturally, they blame us.  Our applications programmers tell
our Sysprogs "it's always worked before, so it must be a problem with
z/OS".  I don't know which version of COBOL was used by the customer,
or whether the program was recompiled for z/OS, or what runtime
library they use.  But I do know that our source code calls
subprograms dynamically.  It's even possible that some files are
opened in a dynamically called subprogram.

Our of our sysprogs researched IBM APARs for this, and his advice to
the applications programmers is to change the COBOL program to
explicitly close the files.

Here's the text of the IBM APAR which was closed 11/21/1990, and last
updated 09/25/2002.  Note also that in some cases a file cannot be
closed, or a file can be closed but the last buffer may not be
written.  And the APAR also says "In order to maintain proper
programming, it is recommended that all APPLICATIONS that open
datasets, close them before task termination."


Item II04712


  APAR Identifier ...... II04712       Last Changed..02/09/25
  ABENDC03 MSGIEC999I DEB ADDR = XXXXX REG15 = 00000004
  APPLICATION DEBUG ASSISTANCE PROCEDURE

  Symptom ...... AB ABENDC03          Status ........... CLOSED  CAN
  Severity ................... 4      Date Closed ......... 90/11/21
  Component .......... INFOV2LIB      Duplicate of ........
  Reported Release ......... 001      Fixed Release ............
  Component Name V2 LIB INFO ITE      Special Notice
  Current Target Date ..              Flags
  SCP ...................
  Platform ............

  Status Detail: Not Available

  PE PTF List:

  PTF List:


  Parent APAR:
  Child APAR list:


  ERROR DESCRIPTION:
  System C03 abends occur at task termination when a program opens
  a data set and DOESN'T CLOSE it before returning to the system.
  This can be verified with REG0 (REG 0) at the time of the CLOSE
  (SVC14) in the trace table.  If REG0 = E3C3F0C1, then the close
  was issued by IFG0TC0A and if there are no previous errors, the
  ERROR IS IN THE APPLICATION.
  See also II02095 for additional information.
  *************************************************************
  NOTE:  OW00185 fixes problem where ABENDC03 is issued with NO
         IEC999I message
  *************************************************************
  .
  As part of task termination, RTM will free user subpool storage
  that was still allocated when the program terminated.  The DCB
  for the open data set can reside in that storage.  When the data
  management resource manager IFG0TC0A detects the open data set,
  it program checks when it attempts to access the DCB.
  IFG0TC0A returns to RTM with return code 4, who then issues
  the ABENDC03.
  -NOTE--
  RTM does nothing with datasets at all.  At task termination, RTM
  calls a DFP module, IFG0TC0A.  IFG0TC0A runs the DEB chain for a
  terminating task, closing each dataset as it comes to it.
  If any CLOSEs don't work, IFG0TC0A gives RTM back a bad return
  and RTM responds by issuing the abendC03.
  When the ENTIRE PAGE occupied by the DCB(or ACB) has been free'd
  CLOSE can't work and the abendC03 is inevitable.  If, however
  there is anything else still getmained on the same page as the
  DCB and the area the DCB is in hasn't been reused, the residual
  information on that page would be sufficient to close the
  dataset and the abendc03 does not get issued..
  In all events (even when the abendC03 is issued) the dataset is
  "cleaned up."  The difference is, when an abendC03 (and its
  corresponding msgIEC999I) is issued, you may not get the last
  buffer written to the dataset.  The dataset treatment itself is
  not inconsistent, but the environment in which this treatment
  is occurring may not be quite the same.
  ***************************************************************
  THE PURPOSE OF THIS APAR IS TO PROVIDE TRACES THAT CAN BE USED
  IDENTIFY THE APPLICATION THAT IS TERMINATING WITHOUT CLOSING
  ALL OF ITS OPEN DATA SETS AND TO IDENTIFY THE DATA SETS THAT
  HAVE BEEN LEFT OPEN.
  ***************************************************************


  LOCAL FIX:
  TO DEBUG AN ABENDC03:
  1). Find the program that is accessing the data set.
      The data set name will be in the IEC999I message.
  2). In this program, add WTOs after the opens (SVC 19,22) and
      the closes and verify that each DCB that gets opened also
      gets closed. (SVC 20)
  3). If you cannot find the program that is causing the failure,
      set the following slips to find the program, the DEB, and
      the DCB.
  A)  SLIP SET,SB,A=TRACE,TRDATA=(STD,REGS,2R%,+53,11R%,+3F),
      RANGE=XXXXXX,JOBNAME=JJJJ,ID=C03A,END
      where:
      XXXXXX = EPA of CSECT IFG0198N to be found in a LPAMAP.
               CSECT IFG0198N is in LMOD IGC0001I.

  B) Set a slip on the IEC999I message to get a dump.
     SLIP SET,IF,ACTION=SVCD,LPAMOD=(IGC0003E,0),JOBNAME=xxxxxxxx,
       DATA=(1R%+4,EQ,C9C5C3F9,1R%+8,EQ,F9F9C940),END



     Initialize GTF as follows:
     Start GTF with TRACE=SLIP,SVCP,JOBNAMEP

  Use trace event keywords: SVC=(13,19,20,22,117),JOBNAME=JJJ,END
  For the DUMP dataset:  (IPCS)
  OPTION 4: VERBX MTRACE will give the IEC999I message
  Get the DEB address from the IEC999I message.

  For the TRACE dataset: (IPCS)
  OPTION 4: GTF SVC SLIP will initialize the trace

  There will be SLIP S+U records everytime IFG0198N is entered.
  This is the last module in OPEN processing, so we are assuming
  that this data set gets opened correctly.

  USR 0040 data will be the DEB in the associated slip USR record.
  USR 0054 data will be the DCB in the associated slip USR record.

  THE DCB + X'2D' points to a 3 byte DEB address.
  THE DEB + X'19' points to a 3 byte DCB address.
  THE DCB + X'28' for 2 bytes will point to the offset into TIOT

  Using the DEB address from the IEC999I message, issue find
  commands until the DEB address is found in the DCB + x'2D'
  (USR 0054).  Find the associated 3 byte DCB address in the
  DEB + x'19' (USR 0040).  Go to the top of the trace and issue
  a find command on the 3 byte DCB address until it appears in a
  PLIST of a SVC 19 or SVC 22.  The DCB will be the lower 3 bytes
  of the PLIST. The OPSW in this SVC entry will point to the
  APPLICATION that issued the open.
  ************************************************************
  **      The APPLICATION is in error and the owners        **
  **      should be contacted for problem resolution        **
  ************************************************************
  There will also be a MODN (module name) field in the SVC entry
  that may contain the module name that issued the open.  See if
  there is an SVC 20 with a PLIST (lower 3 bytes) that matches
  the DCB address.  If there is not an SVC 20 with a PLIST
  (lower 3 bytes = DCB address), then the dataset was not closed
  by the APPLICATION and before the TASK terminated, the DCB was
  either FREEMAINED or OVERLAYED.
  .
  The ABENDC03 usually occurs when task termination passes the
  TCBDEBs to task close for final clean-up.  If the DCBs are
  closed properly, then the DEBs are removed from the TCBDEB
  chain.
  .
  In order to maintain proper programming, it is recommended that
  all APPLICATIONS that open datasets, close them before task
  termination.  A good check is to put in WTOs before opens,
  SVC 19 or 22, and before the closes, SVC 20, to ensure that
  all datasets that get opened, get closed.


  PROBLEM SUMMARY:


  PROBLEM CONCLUSION:


  TEMPORARY FIX:


  COMMENTS:
  THIS IS AN INFORMATIONAL APAR.
  Following are some problems seen by L2:
  This ended up being a user error. A user is executing
  DB2 dsntiual in tso batch, the call is getting a sql -911
  resource timeout. The REXX code does not check return code back
  from sql call and temp data sets created earlier were not
  closed.  Symptoms: MSGIEC999I IEC999I IFG0TC0A MSGIEC161I
  IEC161I 056-084


  MODULES/MACROS:


  SRLS:


  RTN CODES:


  CIRCUMVENTION:


  MESSAGE TO SUBMITTER:

For IBMLink customer support in the U.S, call 1-800-543-3912.
                                                                              
                                                         


> 
> --
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> > http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T13:08:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avpq3i$s21$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <avmhmh$14c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3E1FCA6F.C1899FE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold,
  Have you (and/or your programmer) checked out,

"3.2.4 Closing files in non-COBOL and OS/VS COBOL programs"

at

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/3.2.4

One other question - is your COBOL program compiled with CMPR2 or NOCMPR2?
The rule about closing files is an '85 Standard rule - so CMPR2 *might* not
(is not required to) do this.

I would certainly ASK the "customer site" to show you the compiler options
from the current *and* original compiles of "your source code".  You
probably ALSO want to see the LE run-time options for both environments.

Bottom-Line:
  Personally (and I could be mistaken on this), I do NOT believe that this
is a z/OS issue - but could well be a "z/OS *installation* choices" issue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Arnold,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/3.2.4

I will forward this information to them.  It described some possible
causes that I am sure do not apply (assembler MAIN with COBOL
suprograms).  While I have not seen the source code with the problem,
I am pretty certain that it involves a COBOL main program, with
dynamically called assembler subprograms that either open VSAM files
and/or perform specialized communications with our network.  That
combination could cause SC03 abends and is mentioned on a subordinate
page.

> 
> One other question - is your COBOL program compiled with CMPR2 or NOCMPR2?
> The rule about closing files is an '85 Standard rule - so CMPR2 *might* not
> (is not required to) do this.

That's a good question.  Since we're talking about an external
customer there's no telling what compile options may have been used. 
I'm pretty sure we've never used CMPR2 at our own site, because
certain departments were so slow in moving away from OS/VS COBOL.  By
the time they started using COBOL II, it was almost out of service,
and CMPR2 has been discouraged here.  Most of our programmers don't
know about CMPR2/NOCMPR2 and the default compile procs only use
NOCMPR2.  And isn't it the case that CMPR2 is not supported in COBOL
for OS/390 & VM?  

Actually, we have several departments that provide COBOL source code
to external customers and in the early 1990's we had complaints from
customers because they no longer had the OS/VS COBOL compiler and
wanted COBOL II versions of our programs.  

Y2K caused all departments to convert to at least COBOL II, so we
shouldn't be delivering any code now that requires OS/VS COBOL syntax.


> 
> I would certainly ASK the "customer site" to show you the compiler options
> from the current *and* original compiles of "your source code".  You
> probably ALSO want to see the LE run-time options for both environments.

This sounds eminently sensible, but it's not my dog.  I will
definitely pass your suggestions to the interested parties, but it's
up to them to fix it.  I'm certain our programs were tested in our
environment and didn't experience the problem, so the customer's
configuration might be a contributing factor.  Our configuration of
COBOL or LE options might be contrary to the customer's normal
practice or shop standards.  

> 
> Bottom-Line:
>   Personally (and I could be mistaken on this), I do NOT believe that this
> is a z/OS issue - but could well be a "z/OS *installation* choices" issue.

I agree.  I do not believe it's a fault in z/OS, but there's still
some possibility of various configuration problems between z/OS,
COBOL, and LE.  

As Steve Thompson pointed out in his reply, the SC03 abends could be
generated with much older releases of MVS, well before z/OS or OS/390
was even announced.  He believes the application program should
explicitly close all files and not rely on automatic clean-up.  I
agree that's a good place to start.

> 
> --
> Bill Klein
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T23:04:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E20E93C.1BFC0253@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <avmhmh$14c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3E1FCA6F.C1899FE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
<snip> 
> The symptom is a C03 abend.  And they're definitely seeing them.
> 
<snip>

I hate to say this, but the APAR does not correctly state
what the problem is and then goes on to say lots of stuff
that unless you work in "systems", probably won't make a lot
of sense.

The DEB chain is run during task termination (because of
"normal" termination or ABEND). This is done to ensure that
all files are closed.

When a DEB is found to be OPEN but the storage to which it
points (DCB, ACB, etc.) is NOT available, the only choice
here is the ABENDSC03.

This is not something new with z/OS. This may be the result
of something different about the LE runtime, or DFSMS, or
some other item that the COBOL program is using that causes
the DCB/ACB storage to go away. But it is not new with z/OS
because I used to force this type of thing to happen to test
the behaviors of certain recovery routines at one time (this
was at MVS/SP3.x). I've also seen this same thing happen in
MVS/SP1 (what you might call MVS/370).

Typically, I would get this by doing a GETMAIN for storage,
build a DCB in that storage and then "forget" to close a
file before doing the FREEMAIN. Task ended with ABENDSC03
regardless of anything else I did.

Your programmer(s) has implemented a bad design, COBOL
development has issued warnings about this behavior, and now
they are getting caught by this. This is similar to the
problems in the VSE world where one had access to the I/O
areas before OPEN and after CLOSE for release after release.
People were warned that this would no longer be a valid
operation as DOS moved to be closer to the behavior of VM
and MVS.

The COBOL compiler can't necessarily make you put a CLOSE in
your program(s) for a file as you can use a subroutine to do
some kind of action(s) on files defined within the calling
program (OPEN/CLOSE, READ, WRITE, DELETE, etc.).

I think you need to tell your people that it is now time to
pay the piper.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-13T08:53:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E22B6CB.A34A7956@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <avmhmh$14c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3E1FCA6F.C1899FE@worldnet.att.net> <3E20E93C.1BFC0253@ix.netcom.com>`

```
If all of the modules that open the files are COBOL II or later, then
the files should be properly closed upon run-unit termination.  COBOL
can not take care of files opened by other languages because COBOL I-O
routines handle the files and pass the information from COBOL control
blocks to the operating system control blocks and back.  

Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> attorney's fees) for collecting this fee.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-18T12:26:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> schrieb in 
news:3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net:

> The programmer said he had been told that our COBOL programs will
> abend when we upgrade to z/OS 1.4, if they do not close VSAM and QSAM
> files.  I can't remember if this has been discussed in
> comp.lang.cobol, and I am wondering if this is true.

Jm2p: This might be one of the reasons why several mvs shops in Germany
decided NOT to use COBOl internal I-O features. Guess you have a central
program providing all file-io options and you are calling this program
from COBOL without using WRITE, READ or REWRITE. Even OPEN and CLOSE could
be handled this way, and, off course, implicit CLOSE.

In case you migrate to another OS you just have to check and maybe change
this single central io module.

Together with language indipendent record descriptions converted to cobol
file descriptions before compile this might be a good idea for centralized
code in heterogeneous environments.

As I said, just my two pennies...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-19T20:06:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> schrieb in 
news:01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel:

> Yeah right.  People who are too lazy to use OPEN/CLOSE will gladly call a
> subroutine and pass it all the correct parameters.  NOT!

Why NOT? Just two parms needed. 

1 - DDNAME
2 - COBOL Data Structure name (record layout)

If you omit 2 an open or close will be performed (depending on file status 
kept in subroutine).

All other calls are reads ore writes (depending on DDNAME prefix: A* for 
Ouptut files, E* for input files)

That's it. And it works, if no other file io functions are available. Even 
the laziest consultants (oops, duck and cover ;-))) are happy with it (at 
least in our shop).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T21:18:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> schrieb in 
news:3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com:

> Donald Tees wrote:
>> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> 
[snip]
>>> Wow... a shop where a record is never deleted or updated!
>>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> DDNAME is IBM MVS JCL (and may be others, too.)

Close, guys, very close. Sequential files are deleted via IEFBR14 but never 
updated. Updates only happen in DB2 or DLI environments. And from the COBOL 
point of view this is a call of an external module, isn'it <eg> ?? 

Updates and reorgs of flat files are done in the 'write new version, delete 
the old one' way.

The principles of philosophy of theses ideas can be obtained in 'Sein und 
Zeit' by Martin Heidegger, Tuebingen 1926 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T17:13:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home>`

```
Howard,
  I frequently have to dispelled this misconception of IBM QSAM files, i.e.
that they are never "updated".  As IBM's compiler is ANSI conforming, it
works PERFECTLY well if you open a QSAM file with
   OPEN I-O
and do READ and REWRITE

It is true that you must access it sequentially, but it is ENTIRELY possible
to update individual records of a QSAM file via COBOL.  (And of course also
an ESDS file accessed via ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL as well)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T13:16:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0301211316.3476173c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com>...
>
> As I recall, VSAM files may be opened I-O and read randomly, 
> and rewritten as needed.

I think you are making the mistake of thinking that VSAM files are all
of one type. While what you say is strictly true for the ESDS, KSDS
and RRDS variants, it is unusual to read an ESDS (entry-sequenced data
set (i.e. sequential) randomly, it can only be done with the RBA or an
alternate index.  KSDS is key-sequenced data set (i.e. index
sequential) while RRDS is relative-record data set (i.e. random).


Robert
```

```
product) was the ability to have KSDS and RRDS (with KSDS 
being the more popular).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T09:58:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2E6B59.6050205@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com> <fcd09c56.0301211316.3476173c@posting.google.com> <3E2DC35C.6080004@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham wrote:
> 
> Robert Jones wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> (with KSDS being the more popular).

ESDS's make wonderful online CICS log files because CICS supports VSAM 
files, but not QSAM (except as Extrapartition datasets which are 
accessed via GET/PUT and they have embedded OS WAITs). ESDS's are easy 
to process, too, if the records are fixed length. Plus, as someone 
already pointed out, you can build AIX's across them.

For the sake of completeness I'll mention Linear datasets, which do not 
contain records and which are not updated in the traditional sense of 
READ (for update) & REWRITE.
```

- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com> <fcd09c56.0301211316.3476173c@posting.google.com> <3E2DC35C.6080004@nycap.rr.com>`

```

On 21-Jan-2003, Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote:

> No, I was not thinking that all VSAM files were all one
> type.  However, most of the places I've worked at (perhaps
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> being the more popular).

So what made RRDS better than old relative files?   How bad was the old IBM VSAM
to make everybody want to convert?

I worked at a place that had Univac 9030.  When we converted to IBM, I chose to
make everything VSAM as it logically was the same as what we already had.   What
we had was files we created once and then reused.   So I made ESDS, RRDS, and
KSDS.   We used RRDS a lot in that shop, and KSDS not very often.

Other computers don't have two flavors of indexed files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T17:51:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2EDCA9.9010903@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com> <fcd09c56.0301211316.3476173c@posting.google.com> <3E2DC35C.6080004@nycap.rr.com> <b0mdn3$s3g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 21-Jan-2003, Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Other computers don't have two flavors of indexed files.

ISAM preceded VSAM and was sold by IBM as being better than 
sliced bread at the time.  But whenever you updated a record 
the original was marked for delete and the "rewritten" 
record was placed at the end of the file.  The index and 
file had to be reorg'ed nightly in order to keep the file in 
sync and cut down on processing time.  One insurance 
company, using it with CICS, found that it was taking almost 
the entire night to do the reorg and nothing else could be 
done against the file until it was finished.  VSAM, with an 
actual update in place capability, well... that was like a 
gift from the gods (the gods being Big Blue, again with 
something "new and improved").
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T07:16:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2F96C1.7000305@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<3E1E80D4.915E0028@worldnet.att.net> <bldb0b.0n7.ln@news.hinznet.home> <01c2bf6f$383270a0$3795f243@chottel> <ivse0b.phh.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0fbel$6m0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E2C2868.2090401@Sympatico.ca> <3E2C1B0A.6030107@nycap.rr.com> <eilh0b.fc1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <b0hvqe$qmh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E2D4C4D.1010007@nycap.rr.com> <fcd09c56.0301211316.3476173c@posting.google.com> <3E2DC35C.6080004@nycap.rr.com> <b0mdn3$s3g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 21-Jan-2003, Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> to make everybody want to convert?

IBM's old access method was ISAM, not VSAM.

> I worked at a place that had Univac 9030.  When we converted to IBM, I chose to
> make everything VSAM as it logically was the same as what we already had.   What
> we had was files we created once and then reused.   So I made ESDS, RRDS, and
> KSDS.   We used RRDS a lot in that shop, and KSDS not very often.

Did you have a lot of direct access files?

> Other computers don't have two flavors of indexed files.

IIRC, IBM considered VSAM a "strategic" product and a replacement for 2 
older access methods, ISAM & Direct Access. The RRDS was the DA 
replacement. ESDSes were the VSAM version of QSAM; but it turned out 
that there was no way to replace QSAM, which had features 20+ years ago 
that ESDS clusters didn't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question on closing files

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T13:29:45+00:00

### tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

- **From:** sachin_satam <member22224@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T11:34:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2371531.1042198497@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi all,
We need to migrate an application from tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe
MVS COBOL.
We have to convert SCOBOL, ENFORM, TAL programs as well as TACL/NETBATCH
JOB Control on MVS IBM Mainframe.
I would like to know whether any tool is available for this.

Also please help me to find Tandem COBOL documentation on the net.

Thanks in advance
Sachin
```

```
available for use as both a starting point and as a target for the
translation.
```

#### ↳ Re: tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-13T13:55:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2335E4.4FB9B8E4@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<2371531.1042198497@dbforums.com>`

```
I know nothing about the Tandem platform.  However, I believe that COBOL is
the most "portable" of the "third generation" programming languages.  Have
you compiled your Tandem COBOL on the IBM mainframe yet, to see what
language elements do not compile cleanly?  If the code doesn't use too many
Tandem language extensions, this part of your conversion may be trivial.
You will almost certainly have to convert SELECT ... ASSIGN clauses, and a
few other "implementor defined" elements of COBOL.  But this may prove
simple enough that you can write your own conversion program.

Be sure you use the very latest IBM COBOL compiler that you have available;
it may save you from further conversions in the future.

Also, if possible, get the specs of any assembler language routines that
have to be carried to the new platform.  It may be possible to code some of
them in COBOL, using "new" features like STRING, UNSTRING, INSPECT, etc.
that have actually been available for over 20 years.

sachin_satam wrote:

> Hi all,
> We need to migrate an application from tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-13T22:16:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E233D05.4060409@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2371531.1042198497@dbforums.com> <3E2335E4.4FB9B8E4@attglobal.net>`

```
Most of the time you'll find differences in the file 
description formats, identification information, etc.  The 
logic should need very little changes.  Once the 
Identification and FD stuff is changed then compile to fine 
tune the language specific differences.  Most conversions 
are fairly easy (from COBOL to COBOL that is).

The big problem may be in the files themselves.  Does Tandem 
use ASCII?  IBM uses EBCDIC so files may need conversion. 
Some systems also sort numbers before letters so the outcome 
of sorts may be different.  IBM it's letters first.  I did a 
conversion from Honeywell to IBM and that was the big issue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-14T15:35:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R6WU9.7711$qU5.5940111@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2371531.1042198497@dbforums.com> <3E2335E4.4FB9B8E4@attglobal.net> <3E233D05.4060409@nycap.rr.com>`

```
"Robert Graham" <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3E233D05.4060409@nycap.rr.com...
> The big problem may be in the files themselves.  Does Tandem
> use ASCII?  IBM uses EBCDIC so files may need conversion.
> Some systems also sort numbers before letters so the outcome
> of sorts may be different.  IBM it's letters first.  I did a
> conversion from Honeywell to IBM and that was the big issue.

Um, the reason the numbers and letters sort in different priorities is
BECAUSE of the difference in character set (EBCDIC-ASCII).

Of course, there's always COLLATING SEQUENCE...or for that matter, CHARACTER
SET in the FD, but I have NEVER seen the latter  used 'for real'.

MCM
```

```
sachin_satam wrote:
> Dear Robert,
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1)Can you pl tell me the challenges faced in this assignment?

The night the product had to be installed, New York City was 
in the midst of a blizzard, and I had to get home to New 
Jersey - over an hour in good weather, almost impossible 
when the trains are either not running or delayed for hours. 
  Other than that, no challenges.

> 2)Have you used any tool for migration exercise?

Just my brain.  It was in the 1980's and we didn't even have 
real PC's, just dumb terminals.  (At least the IBM 3270 Mark 
IV's were in color - an improvement over the normal sickly 
green.)

> 2)How you  done the file conversion?

I had to create a table to convert ASCII to EBCDIC character 
for character in each record.  Read in the original file, 
translated the characters, wrote out new file.

> 3)Have you done any CICS/BMS Screens translation on IBM system?

No.

> 4)Have you done any JCL translation on IBM system?

Not in case above.  But I have done IBM VM/CMS to IBM MVS 
JCL conversions, along with changes to COBOL programs.  MVS 
JCL is more wordy, requiring more "cards".  Change to 
programs was minimal, mostly in file identification area.

> 
> Again thanks for your reply to my post.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


You're welcome.  Lots of luck.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T22:49:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2CC349.FF8137D6@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<2371531.1042198497@dbforums.com> <3E2335E4.4FB9B8E4@attglobal.net> <3E233D05.4060409@nycap.rr.com> <2410332.1043038684@dbforums.com>`

```
I did a Honeywell to IBM conversion in the '80s.

sachin_satam wrote:
<snip>
> 1)Can you pl tell me the challenges faced in this assignment?
<snip>
Data names and paragraph names can be the same on the
Honeywell system. They cannot in VS/COBOL.

ASSIGN/SELECT clauses are different because the way files
are handled is a bit different.

"CARD" based data is different! Because the Honeywell is a
36 bit, word oriented architecture, you could have more
characters of data in a card than IBM could. However, the
actual card (assuming you still have them) can only have 80
characters of data (also assuming the 80 column card is in
use).

Binary data (fixed point or floating point) is different
between the two architectures. Again, because of 36 bit word
sizes v. 32 bit word sizes, there are some problems in
converting/migrating the data.
<snip>
> 2)Have you used any tool for migration exercise?
<snip>
No tool for the COBOL. We used Hyper_Channel for
communications between the two systems. It was able to
handle 90+% of the data migration issues.
<snip>
> 2)How you  done the file conversion?
<snip>
Via Hyper_Channel. However, for those cases where the data
formats are/were not equivalent, I wrote a program to
convert all data to ASCII, numeric fields would have sign
separate following. Then on the IBM side I would read that
data and move it into the output record field by field to
get my conversion.
<snip>
> 3)Have you done any CICS/BMS Screens translation on IBM system?
<snip>
No. In my case, there was another group that was doing the
online program conversions. So I never saw the equivalents
in/from the Honeywell system.
<snip>
> 4)Have you done any JCL translation on IBM system?
<snip>
Yes. I even had jobs submitted to the Honeywell system from
IBM that would get data for me, then cause a job to be run
on the IBM system to process the data, then run a follow-on
job on the Honeywell system... So I had JOBs in my libraries
that had both type of job control intermixed and on both
systems!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: tandem COBOL to IBM Mainframe MVS COBOL Conversion

- **From:** sachin_satam <member22224@dbforums.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2410334.1043039152@dbforums.com>`

<a id="t-cd4ceda530"></a>
## Thread: Internal vs External SORTs — 173 this month / 174 total

### Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** Ubiquitous <weberm@polaris.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T17:58:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```
I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
depend on what one is sorting?
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T18:06:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avn23o$t09$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```

On 10-Jan-2003, Ubiquitous <weberm@polaris.net> wrote:

> I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
> program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
> depend on what one is sorting?

Depends.

Some people curse anybody who does internal sorts, some people never do external
sorts.   IMHO, I believe many small steps are easier to maintain than one
complicated step, so I evaluate to see if there is a particular need for an
internal sort - if not, then I do external sorts.


I don't use performance as an element of my decision - I use maintainability.  
After all both sorts use the same sort program anyway.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@nildram.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T17:29:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.0000000b.01a21d9f@nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <avn23o$t09$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

> I don't use performance as an element of my decision - I use maintainability.  

Good man.

> After all both sorts use the same sort program anyway.

Yup.


Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T13:24:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avn34i$ku3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```
In article <avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>,
Ubiquitous  <weberm@polaris.net> wrote:
>I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
>program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
>depend on what one is sorting?

The answer is predicated on the criteria one uses for 'better'.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T12:38:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avn3vv$3s9$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```
(Assuming an IBM mainframe environment - from "context")

If you use the FASTSRT compiler option, there is probably little performance
difference.  Probably the *most* important thing to "look into" is what is
most commonly used in *YOUR* shop - so that other programmers can "maintain"
(easily) whatever you create.

I have read several (semi-internal) posts between the IBM COBOL and SORT
developers and they both find reasonable "justification" for each approach.

My *personal* opinion - is that it depends on what "processing" of input or
output records you use.  I (again personally) don't really like creating and
maintaining E15 and E35 exits - and much prefer COBOL Input/Output
procedures. (If the only "processing" of your input/output can be done via
SORT control cards, then this issue doesn't apply)  Similarly, if you
have/use COBOL report writer, I find that a GREAT tool in Output
Procedures - as opposed to the DFSort (or SyncSort) "reporting" facilities.
On the other hand,  if you  have a basic

   - process an entire file (to get it ready to SORT)
  - sort that file
  - process the output file (often in multiple different ways)

type logic,  I (again personally) see this as an EXCELLENT target for an
"external sort".

OBVIOUSLY, if you happen to be a "contractor" (as many CLC readers are),
then it is CRITICAL that you find out what the "shop standard) is (if any).
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T19:44:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```

Ubiquitous wrote:
> I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
> program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
> depend on what one is sorting?
> 

What I've experienced is it depends on the shop.  One place 
I worked at "required" all sorts to be external in a 
preceding JCL step.  Since their JCL streams were miles 
long, it made for restarts after an abend much easier.  (The 
JCL steps were performed based upon return codes from 
previous step).

Other places don't care and so what ever makes the most 
sense in the given situation is the way I go.  I have never 
seen any shop question the performance of either sort.  You 
can get very creative with external sorts, but why fill your 
mind with all the little tricks of SyncSort when it is just 
as easy, if not more easy, in COBOL.  Especially if you want 
to filter the data in any way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T17:48:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0301101748.a824729@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com>`

```
From what I understand, if the internal sort uses fastsrt and
using/giving instead of input/output procedures, the performance is
comperable. Otherwise, external wins hands down, because sort i/o is
much faster than cobol's.

Jack 


Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com>...
> Ubiquitous wrote:
> > I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> to filter the data in any way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-11T22:50:45+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rq012v000ig1u3iovafejnj08ldd2b9nvn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com> <8a2d426e.0301101748.a824729@posting.google.com>`

```
On 10 Jan 2003 17:48:40 -0800 jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight) wrote:

:>From what I understand, if the internal sort uses fastsrt and
:>using/giving instead of input/output procedures, the performance is
:>comperable. Otherwise, external wins hands down, because sort i/o is
:>much faster than cobol's.

If one is using/giving, one is really doing an external sort under the covers.

:>Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com>...
:>> Ubiquitous wrote:
:>> > I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
:>> > program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
:>> > depend on what one is sorting?
:>> > 
:>> 
:>> What I've experienced is it depends on the shop.  One place 
:>> I worked at "required" all sorts to be external in a 
:>> preceding JCL step.  Since their JCL streams were miles 
:>> long, it made for restarts after an abend much easier.  (The 
:>> JCL steps were performed based upon return codes from 
:>> previous step).
:>> 
:>> Other places don't care and so what ever makes the most 
:>> sense in the given situation is the way I go.  I have never 
:>> seen any shop question the performance of either sort.  You 
:>> can get very creative with external sorts, but why fill your 
:>> mind with all the little tricks of SyncSort when it is just 
:>> as easy, if not more easy, in COBOL.  Especially if you want 
:>> to filter the data in any way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-13T11:26:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v25pi2l3fpiqc0@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com> <8a2d426e.0301101748.a824729@posting.google.com> <rq012v000ig1u3iovafejnj08ldd2b9nvn@4ax.com> <8a2d426e.0301121232.56f9624f@posting.google.com> <bkd52vgbpenveqd8vbje92p3682ep47pdd@4ax.com>`

```

Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:bkd52vgbpenveqd8vbje92p3682ep47pdd@4ax.com...
[...]
>
> I fail to see a case where doing a SORT USING/GIVING would gain anything
over
> an external sort.

It is not a typical mainframe, business application; but
I have an optimization solution that makes multiple
passes on partial solutions from the input file. Each pass
and for each partial solution, the program works on it for
a limited time generating more partial solutions, then
writes these to the output file. At the end of input, the
output file is sorted to become the input file for the next
pass. The program terminates after a predetermined
number of passes even though many partial solutions
are untried.

With SORT USING/GIVING, it is not necesary to exit the
program between passes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-13T13:33:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avv4ab$6c0$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <3E1F250C.3070600@nycap.rr.com> <8a2d426e.0301101748.a824729@posting.google.com> <rq012v000ig1u3iovafejnj08ldd2b9nvn@4ax.com> <8a2d426e.0301121232.56f9624f@posting.google.com> <bkd52vgbpenveqd8vbje92p3682ep47pdd@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin,
  A couple of points:

1) COBOL uses "native" SORT for I/O when FASTSRT is specified, if *either*
the USING or GIVING phrase is specified.  Therefore, if you have
     USING ... OUTPUT PROCEDURE
        or
     INPUT PROCEDURE ... GIVING
then SORT will do the I/O for one - but not both of the phases (similar to
using either an E15 or an E35 exit - but not both)

2) The other "advantage" of using a COBOL internal SORT (with FASTSRT
specified and both USING/GIVING) is if the "program logic" does "common"
things both before *and* after the actual SORT.  Although you (the
programmer) *COULD* break this up into 3 steps Program-Logic-1 then SORT
then Program-Logic-2, if the actual "code" has common logic in the before
and after sections, (which often happens) then keeping to an internal SORT
allows for "performed" common procedure - without having to maintain it in 2
different programs.  (Obviously, this "assumes" that although there is SOME
common logic, not ALL the logic is identical in the "before" and "after"
portions of the application.

    ***

My *guess* is that this isn't REAL common - but I would also guess that
almost every "large" system has at least one example of this type of
situation.

Bottom-Line (as stated before):
  I don't think that the original question has ANY "hard and fast" answer -
but there are things that can be considered "if all else is equal".
```

#### ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T16:00:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mo-dnSwlUblX34KjXTWciw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com>`

```

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com...
> I'm curious; is it better to perform a SORT within a COBOL
> program or better to perform it in the JCL, or does it
> depend on what one is sorting?


With an internal sort you can control WHAT sub-set gets sorted.

That is, in a 1 million-record file, you may be creating a report of only
500 records. Instead of an external sort shuffling these 500 to the front,
you can select only the 500 in which you're interested and sort them.

Related topic:
One of the rules (attributed to Moses, but the file was truncated after ten
records): "Thou shalt not sort the master file."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internal vs External SORTs

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-10T16:18:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E1F62DC.8040500@vnet.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<avn1kv$n9j$6@news.utelfla.com> <mo-dnSwlUblX34KjXTWciw@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

>With an internal sort you can control WHAT sub-set gets sorted.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If I understand what you're saying, you can, in fact, do that with 
DFSORT by using an INCLUDE or OMIT statement to remove the records you 
don't want to sort.  INCLUDE or OMIT processing takes place before SORT 
processing, so you will only sort the records you include.   DFSORT also 
has a SKIPREC=n option that removes the first n records before sorting. 
  Sure, there's things you can do with a COBOL program that you can't do 
with DFSORT, but there are plenty of things that you can do with DFSORT.

I know this is a COBOL group and you guys like to write programs for 
everything, but I think you'd be surprised if you actually looked at the 
DFSORT books to see ALL of the many things you can do with DFSORT 
without writing a program.  I'm not saying that an external sort is 
better or worse than an internal sort, only that you shouldn't assume 
that you can't do things with DFSORT that you can do with an internal 
sort without actually finding out.   Even an "internal sort" can use 
DFSORT control statements (like INCLUDE) very easily via a DFSPARM data 
set, e.g.

//DFSPARM DD *
   INCLUDE COND=(...)
/*

All of the DFSORT books are online.  You can access them from:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/srtmpub.html

In particular, you might want to look at the "Summary of Changes" for 
all of the DFSORT Releases and PTFs to see how much we've put into 
DFSORT over the years that you might not be aware of.  Here's the URL 
for the Summary of Changes on the DFSORT website:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/summary_changes/srtmsocc.html

Note that I'm not looking to start a fight here - just providing 
information for those who want to know.
```

```
ten

can't do things ..." assume facts not in evidence.
```

```
> If I understand what you're saying, you can, in fact, do that with

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

That last line is a reason I often use CoBOL sorts.  Usually this involves
lookups to a database to determine which records to include, but occasionally it
will involve a complex calculation.   The other reason I use CoBOL sorts is when
the sort order depends upon a user parm.

Also, when programmers are unfamiliar with the more complex DFSORT options, my
tendency will be to use a tool (such as CoBOL), that they are more familiar with
- unless I am simply extracting test data for myself.
```

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>

```
Doug Scott wrote:
> JerryMouse,
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Perhaps he intended to say "The shalt not sort the master 
file AND REWRITE IT" .  One should be able to sort anything 
he darn well pleases.

As two someone else's pointed out, lots can be done with 
DFSORT and SyncSort - you just have to know how, and lots of 
places seem to shred all manuals except one set kept in the 
systems programmer's locked closet.  I've used both for one 
shot file fixes and had good results.  If you are going to 
use "USING/GIVING" you might as well use an external sort as 
it is just a couple of JCL lines as opposed to having to 
write all the COBOL code.  The only real reason not to use 
external is to change the file in some way that not possible 
(or at least EASY) in an external sort.
```
