be SPAM?  You don't consider everytime you load an upgrade 
of some program that the AOL sign-up icon shows up on you 
desktop and in your programs list to be SPAM?

You don't consider the pervasiveness of the wavy window to 
be SPAM?  Or the fact that most of the posts on this site 
were probably written on a MS controlled PC (This one included)?

I've had Yahoo e-mail for years and I get no spam or porn 
offers in it.  I've also had Netscape e-mail (owned by AOL) 
and the filter list to eliminate porn just keeps growing and 
growing.  If it comes from AOL in any way, shape or form 
(including print or tv ads) IT IS SPAM!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: for your amusment

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-14T22:25:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E24D496.BA51E4ED@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3e2448b2.3287089@newsgroups.bellsouth.net> <3E248029.4010600@Sympatico.ca> <3E246E7F.2000108@nycap.rr.com> <3E24B68C.3020508@Sympatico.ca> <fKSdnYKFq83cCrmjXTWchA@giganews.com> <3E24A55B.1000905@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham wrote:
<snip> 
> I've had Yahoo e-mail for years and I get no spam or porn
> offers in it.  
<snip>

Thou art blessed above all people. I've had a Yahoo! email
account for years, and 90+% of all the email I receive is
SPAM (BCE or UCE). I get offers to enlarge body parts, I get
offers for kiddie porn, I even get notices alerting me to
the fact that I've been re-instated to some porn site!

Your other points are bogus. That AOL has pop-up ads and the
like is not SPAM, but it is insidious and quite annoying.

Since we are already off topic, I find it very interesting
that even though I am running three NT based systems (one
being an NT 4.0 Server), we don't have "virus" problems
because we do not use IE, Outlook (or Outlook Express). That
should tell you something. Meanwhile, we are slowly
migrating off to Linux (we ordered and are testing a Lindows
box that is quite nice).
```

- **References:** `<3e2448b2.3287089@newsgroups.bellsouth.net> <3E248029.4010600@Sympatico.ca> <3E246E7F.2000108@nycap.rr.com> <3E24B68C.3020508@Sympatico.ca> <fKSdnYKFq83cCrmjXTWchA@giganews.com> <3E24A55B.1000905@nycap.rr.com> <3E24D496.BA51E4ED@ix.netcom.com>`

"Steve Thompson" <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
>
> Since we are already off topic, I find it very interesting
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> box that is quite nice).

You probably don't have virus problems, not because you avoid IE, etc., but
because you and your users are not stupid. You have up-to-date virus
checkers, you have a policy of not opening unknown attachements, and
practice all manner of safe computing policies.

It may interest you to know that 8 of the 10 most recent/destructive virii
and exploits recorded by CERN are against Linux machines. There may be many
reasons to implement Linux, but immunity from attack is not one of them.

I note you're two generations of operating systems behind current offerings;
we've been using XP for about a year. It is so far ahead of 2000, NT Server,
and 2000 Server, we're all amazed. Before you try to emulate Windows on an
operating system that's a knock-off of a 30-year old operating system
designed by a money-losing division of the telephone company, give XP a try.
Of course, I own a bunch of Microsoft stock, so you should take what I say
as Gospel.

>
> --
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> attorney's fees) for collecting this fee.

> and exploits recorded by CERN are against Linux machines. There may be many
> reasons to implement Linux, but immunity from attack is not one of them.

You have a choice, either use an operating system that is wide spread enough to
have the applications you need - and is wide spread enough to be an attractive
target for vandals - or don't.

Linux is becoming popular enough to be such a target, especially in business
environments.

"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote in message news:DUAV9.702707$WL3.726561@rwcrnsc54...
> "Steve Thompson" wrote:
> > Not only will I not use XP (nor are we allowing it to be
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, actually, no, Linux and uniX based systems are *not* just as vunerable to
viruses, Trojan horses, and worms. This is pure Microsoft dis-information. For
one thing, there are a heck of a lot LESS UNIX machines around than Windows
machines, and yet, the UNIX admins catch any and every virii, trojan, or other bad boy
that comes along. Quickly, and usually with little or no damage.

And by the way, there are no "macro" virii or that ilk currently out there for UNIX.

Now given that, Windows XP is much *much* better than Windows 2000 in every respect
except one, and that is the licensing nonsense. But there is a version available under Open
License or some such that doesn't require activation thing on each workstation and bypasses
the licenseing objections.

Or better yet, go to Citrix and load Linux machines with the ICA client. That works fast, easy,
and you only have one machine (or however many Citrix servers you put up) to worry about.

Much nicer.



> http://techupdate.zdnet.com/techupdate/stories/main/0,14179,2908715,00.html
>
> Gazaloo.
>
>
```

I agree with the above, but...

Searching Cert for advisories I found 12 win (1 on XP) VS 2 Linux 
dated 2002 (didn't involve third-party software). I found 12 for 
Solaris, but most of them required being logged on to use, and
many which would be N/A on a server, or ONLY on a particular server 
like DNS, or Sendmail,...

Now, you can use statistics (lies) to make reports...

Total Vulnerabilities:
   Unix (all brands)  
+ Linux (all brands) 
+ Open Sys Soft (all of them)
------------------------------
More than windows

Note: BEA+Apache+iPlanet = 3 vunerabilities
      but IIS = 1.

Gary

>

> Have you gotten the Lindows box yet?  Those look awfully tempting.
> I'd love to hear your impressions of the system they have put
> together.

So far I have one. I have two NON computer users using it.
They actually have found it quite easy to use. Now it is
time for me to configure SAMBA so that they can get to the
printers on our network (I've been out on a project at a
client's site for 3+ months and just haven't been here to
finish setting things up).

StarOffice seems to do everything that the non-power users
need to do, and far more cheaply than Office 97 (no, we will
not be going to XP or 2000).

As I have time to work with this box I might be posting
more, but it will be ON TOPIC because I intend to do COBOL
development on that box (just as I do on the NT boxes).

- **References:** `<3E24D496.BA51E4ED@ix.netcom.com>`

```
As you can see from the top I'm on AOL. Why - because it's there ::shrug:: and
why not change to elsewhere - ::shrug::.

Anyway - today when I signed on I had 16 e-mails.  2 from clubs I belong to, 1
from a list I belong to, and 13 'spam'.  Of those 13 'spam' 4 were Yahoo, 3
were hotmail and 1 from myself!  The rest were from who knows where.

My biggest complaint about AOL's e-mail is that I cannot get the full length of
an e-mail address.  I see 'customerservi......' which I assume is
'customerservice' of which I may be interested in because at times I do order
things on-line and maybe this is from them with a problem with the order, or
the answer to a question I had about a product.  Sorting of my e-mail is the
least of my desires!

Ah well.  I don't spend enough time on-line to get all in a lather about it :)
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T08:13:57-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<9eScne2qrN5y8bijXTWcjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e2448b2.3287089@newsgroups.bellsouth.net> <3E248029.4010600@Sympatico.ca> <3E246E7F.2000108@nycap.rr.com> <3E24B68C.3020508@Sympatico.ca> <fKSdnYKFq83cCrmjXTWchA@giganews.com> <3E24A55B.1000905@nycap.rr.com>`

```

"Robert Graham" <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
> >
> >
> > Virtually no spam originates from AOL or MSN (Yahoo Groups is a
completely
> > different story). Both AOL and MSN enforce outbound mail limits -
spammers
> > don't like the limits. The headers you're seeing are probably forged.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> desktop and in your programs list to be SPAM?

No. SPAM(tm) is a registered trademark of Hormel. I get it at the grocery
store. AOL CDs, sign up icons, and the like are neither SPAM nor spam, they
are simply disgusting.

>
> You don't consider the pervasiveness of the wavy window to
> be SPAM?  Or the fact that most of the posts on this site
> were probably written on a MS controlled PC (This one included)?

Nope. What I call stuff like that can't be repeated in this family-oriented
group. However, if what you're getting is not email, it can't be spam.
("Spam" = UCE, Unsolicited Commercial Email or UBE, Unsolicited Bulk Email).
Further, if you signed up for it (as an AOL subscriber), it can't be spam
since whatever it is, it is not "unsolicited."

>
> I've had Yahoo e-mail for years and I get no spam or porn
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (including print or tv ads) IT IS SPAM!

Perhaps in some alternate universe. Whether you GET spam has nothing to do
with whether AOL is the source of spam, which was entirely my point. AOL is
odious on several scales, but not as a spam source. AOL actually has a
pretty good filtering scheme to discard incoming spam, too.

>
```

Perhaps I should have enclosed spam in quotation marks as in 
"SPAM",
capitalized to indicate volume of voice.  I used "SPAM" to 
indicate
any junk that arrives via any means - in solid form from the 
postal service,
or electronic in the form of e-mail, unwanted icons, sign-up 
  exe's and so on.

As to the SPAM(tm) by Hormel - around this part of the world 
it is referred to in a very sneering tone of voice.  People 
on welfare don't even eat it.


> Perhaps in some alternate universe. Whether you GET spam has nothing to do
> with whether AOL is the source of spam, which was entirely my point. AOL is
> odious on several scales, but not as a spam source. AOL actually has a
> pretty good filtering scheme to discard incoming spam, too.
> 

The original point was that lots of junk seem to have a 
return address with ".aol" on it.  It may be that the 
address is a fake (I should say almost always it is). But 
why hasn't AOL done something to prevent their name from 
being linked with spam/Spam/SPAM(tm)?  Their lack of 
interest regarding the world outside AOL shows a corporate 
arrogance that definitely reminds people of SPAM(tm).
```

     AOL is working fine for me.  I like it.
But as for spam I receive on hotmail account, there should be some way these
originators of spam (no matter on what place the spam is recievied , hot mail
or not, these persons should be sued for fraud. as if I remember correctly, one
of the main things that defines fraud is "reckless negligence". surely, the
spam senders are recklessly negligent in sending spam to people that they know
dont want to receive the spam.  And it is also a fact that lot of the spammers
use false origin name and address etc.  this surely makes it a fraud which can
be actionable.  Maybe some gain in this to if damages of 100 or 1,000 per
incident are asked for in the judgment.
  it is just another case of a relatively few persons causing a relatively lot
of nuisance value to the rest of us, as they think they will get away with it
forever.
   most spam I get is trying to get me to refinance my mortgage.  sent with

coke last night. The whole point is why would someone pay twice as much for
"Coke" as for any of the other dark colas?  Answer: They are buying into the mystique and magic of "The Real Thing."

AOL is the same thing in the internet world, people think they are buying safety and convenience and good service all wrapped up in
a neat package. I suspect they have Digital Cable mixed up with Internet Service!

-Paul

<a id="t-aba28db308"></a>
## Thread: File status 96 - File system not available — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### File status 96 - File system not available

- **From:** klaus.duttle@online.de (m8java)
- **Date:** 2003-01-14T17:03:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi coboler,

my problem occurs in a tiny program, that runs on the host very fine.
To run it on the AIX platform I've compiled with cob2, IBMs Cobol Set
for AIX version 1.1. Now I get in the open-routine a file status 96
and nowhere I found any useful information about this message. Only
in the COBOL Language Reference on page 274 exists a table with brief
hints for each file status:

96 For VSAM file under OS/390 and VM: An OPEN statement with the
   OUTPUT or EXTEND phrase was attempted for an optional file, but no DD
   statement was specified for the file.
   For QSAM file under OS/390 and VM: An OPEN statement with the
   OUTPUT or EXTEND phrase was attempted for an optional file, but no DD
   statement was specified for the file and the CBLQDA(OFF) run-time option
   was specified.
   Under AIX, OS/2, and Windows: File system not available.
   =========

How I can get the file system available?

Here is a snipet of the cobol source:

FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT LISTE1 ASSIGN TO 'STL-LISTE1'
            FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.
...
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD  LISTE1
    RECORDING MODE    IS F.
       01  DL1-ZEILE.
           05 DL1-VSZ          PIC X.
           05 DL1-DATEN        PIC X(132).

       01  QLDD-ZEILE.
           05                    PIC X(001).
           05 QLDD-DATEN         PIC X(132).
...
OPEN-ROUTINE SECTION.
    OPEN  OUTPUT LISTE1
...

Also I've tried an alternate construction in the FILE-CONTROL.
Coding

FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT LISTE1 ASSIGN USING 'STL-LISTE1'
               FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.

will result in a severe compiler error IGYDS0093-S:

IGYDS0093-S   "'STL-LISTE1'" was found in the "ASSIGN" clause.
              The clause was discarded.

And again there are nowhere useful documentation for such
error numbers.

The last question to the Cobol experts:
When I have to use the STL file system?
Maybe the file 'LISTE1' has to be a VSAM file.
However the file 'LISTE1' should be created
by the cobol program and located on the AIX
file system.

Thanks

Klaus Duttle
```

#### ↳ Re: File status 96 - File system not available

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T01:33:12
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b02dni$tkb$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com>`

```
Is this '96' a two byte variable (h3936 or h3906) taken from the
file status ? h3906 would be 'unable to write to a read-only file'
on the PC. I have no description for h3936 again on the PC.

"m8java" <klaus.duttle@online.de> wrote in message
news:ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com...
> Hi coboler,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>    statement was specified for the file and the CBLQDA(OFF) run-time
option
>    was specified.
>    Under AIX, OS/2, and Windows: File system not available.
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> Klaus Duttle
```

#### ↳ Re: File status 96 - File system not available

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T02:25:11-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b02dk3$1f2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com>`

```
m8java wrote:

> my problem occurs in a tiny program, that runs on the host very fine.
> To run it on the AIX platform I've compiled with cob2, IBMs Cobol Set
> for AIX version 1.1. 

Isn't that a variety of MicroFocus ?

> Now I get in the open-routine a file status 96

Status codes 9x are vendor specific.  If it is MF then the '6' is the ASCII 
character for decimal 36 and this means: 'File already exists'.  This may 
be a permission issue in that the file exists but you do not have write 
access to it, or to the directory that this is in.

AIX is a variety of Unix and you have to take notice of users, groups and 
permissions.


> Also I've tried an alternate construction in the FILE-CONTROL.
> Coding
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>                FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.

What is 'ASSIGN USING' supposed to be ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File status 96 - File system not available

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T12:11:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0488k$li7$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com> <b02dk3$1f2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b02dk3$1f2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> m8java wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Isn't that a variety of MicroFocus ?

No, CobolSet is *not* an  Micro Focus based product.  (There is a Micro Foc
us product for AIX - and IBM used to "repackage" it as an IBM product but
that hasn't been true for MANY years)
```

#### ↳ Re: File status 96 - File system not available

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T12:16:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b048ib$dn2$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0301141703.4426fe3e@posting.google.com>`

```
1) I think you will find that "ASSIGN USING" requires a data-name following
the USING - while ASSIGN TO can be followed by a literal.

2) In general, have you read the section
    "Identifying Files to the Operating System"

on page 17 of the COBOL Set Programming Guide - available on line at:

    http://www-3.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/aix/library/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL compilation errors/ Debugging

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T13:49:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sFdV9.7928$qU5.6109143@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pan.2003.01.15.01.40.14.211192.14535@entropy.muc.muohio.edu>`

```
All compilation - and most run-time -  error codes are compiler-specific. I
think about the only thing "standard" are the FILE STATUS codes.

Best (and possibly only) bet is the support material provided with the
specific compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL compilation errors/ Debugging

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T14:39:52+00:00

- **References:** `<pan.2003.01.15.01.40.14.211192.14535@entropy.muc.muohio.edu>`

```

On 14-Jan-2003, dSb <afterthefall@entropy.muc.muohio.edu> wrote:

> 	Does anyone know of a good website, or of any location of a good source
> for COBOL compilation errors and debugging questions?  I've Googled until
> I'm blue!

This is totally dependent upon what compiler and system you are using.   An IBM
Mainframe manual (available on line) won't help you with a MS-DOS based
compiler.

So tell us more.
```

- **From:** Ganeshmuruga <member22105@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T10:08:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2390355.1042625310@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi,

1. I run Tuxedo8.0 on WIndows2000. I use NetExpress3.1 Complier for
   compiling the tuxedo calls wrapped in cobol.

I set the following environmental variables,
COBCPY, COBDIR, PATH, LIB, TUXDIR

COBCPY=c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\cobinclude

COBDIR=C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\BIN;
C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\INCLUDE;
C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\LIB;

Path=C:\WINNT\system32;C:\WINNT;C:\WINNT\System32\Wbem;
C:\PROGRA~1\ABSOLU~1;
c:\bea\tuxedo8.0;c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\bin;
f:\Common\MsDev98\bin;
f:\Vc98\bin;f:\VC98\include;f:\VC98\ATL\Include;f:\vc98\mfc\include;
C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\BIN;
C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\INCLUDE;
C:\WINNT\system32;C:\WINNT;C:\WINNT\System32\Wbem;
C:\PROGRA~1\ABSOLU~1;
c:\bea\tuxedo8.0;c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\bin;
f:\Common\MsDev98\bin;f:\Vc98\bin;f:\VC98\include;
f:\VC98\ATL\Include;f:\vc98\mfc\include;

LIB=c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\lib;
C:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\LIB;
f:\vc98\lib;F:\VC98\MFC\Lib;
c:\Program Files\Micro Focus\Net Express\Base\LIB;

TUXDIR=c:\bea\tuxedo8.0

I set APPDIR and TUXCONFIG in the cmd prompt as follows,
set APPDIR=c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\samples\atmi\csimpapp
set TUXCONFIG=c:\bea\tuxedo8.0\samples\atmi\csimpapp\tuxconfig


I get the .obj file using the following command

cobol csimpcl.cbl omf(obj) litlink

Now, I do a buildclient as follows,

buildclient -C -o CSIMPCL -f CSIMPCL.obj

I get this error,

LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
TUXNT_CAT:77: ERROR: Can't execute cl  -I"%TUXDIR%"\include
-FeCSIMPCL CSIMPCL.o
bj    "%TUXDIR%"\lib\libcobatmi.lib cblrtssi.lib
"%TUXDIR%"\lib\libtux.lib  "%T
UXDIR%"\lib\libbuft.lib  "%TUXDIR%"\lib\libfml.lib
"%TUXDIR%"\lib\libfml32.lib "
%TUXDIR%"\lib\libengine.lib  wsock32.lib kernel32.lib advapi32.lib
user32.lib gd
i32.lib comdlg32.lib winspool.lib   -link -implib:BC-3c0.lib

It is not able to find the cblrtssi.lib. This .lib file is present in
the 'lib' directory of the netexpress compiler and I have set this path
as LIB=%lib%;<netexpress lib's path>; in the environment.

2. Since the above wasn't working I tried to build the .exe using
   cbllink as follows,
cbllink -ocsimpcl.exe csimpcl.obj

but again I get this error,

csimpcl.obj
cbllds.obj
   Creating library csimpcl.lib and object csimpcl.exp
csimpcl.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _TPINITIALIZE
csimpcl.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _TPCALL
csimpcl.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _TPTERM
csimpcl.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _USERLOG
csimpcl.exe : fatal error LNK1120: 4 unresolved externals

These are related with tuxedo libraries and includes.

buildclient does not recognise cobol lib path and cbllink does not
recognise the tuxedo lib path, Ultimately am not able to get my
executable.

3. If I need to include few libraries along with cbllink, what option
   must I use?.

Is there any other env settings to be made apart from the above or have
I set something wrong?

Anyone who has worked on the cobol wrapped tuxedo programs please help
me out. I would appreciate if I get an answer for my first question.

Thanks in advance
Ganesh

## Thread: COBOL and XML — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL and XML

- **From:** Ubiquitous <weberm@polaris.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T13:01:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
I am about to rewrite this old COBOL subsystem of mine that used
to process flat files that are FTP'd to a mainframe so that it
uses XML files instead. As this is totally new to me (I took an
introductory class on XML but haven't had any hands-on experience
with it yet), I am looking for resources (both on-line and off)
on how to approach this. Any suggestions on where to look or find
examples of how this can be handled would be greatly appreciated! :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and XML

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T13:22:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tgdV9.37568$aG4.2076610@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Well Ubiquitous - if your mainframe is up to date, you probably just want to call a decent Java XML parser from the COBOL code.
Other than that, it is a much more difficult proposition to parse XML than a
fixed format flat file.

-Paul

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and XML

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T15:20:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b03qmm$shp$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
I've recently written a paper on integration of COBOL with webservices and included an example of how to transfer COBOL to XML and vice-versa. Perhaps the following may clarify things:

COBOL data structure (internal):

01  report-query.
  03  report-ID      PIC 9(9).
  03  report-style      PIC 9(2).
  03  query-data.
    05  department-ID  PIC 9(2.
    05  the-date.
      07  year      PIC 9(4).
      07  month      PIC 9(2).
  03  show-return      PIC 9. 
 
 
XML data structure (output):

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<report-query>
  <report-ID>1289</report-ID>
  <report-style>08</report-style>
  <query-data>
    <deparment-ID>02</department-ID>
    <the-date>
      <year>1999</year>
      <month>11</month>
    </the-date>
  </query-data>
  <show-return>1</show-return>
</report-query>


If you write down in more detail what you want to do I might be able to help. Do you only want to rewrite XML to COBOL? Or do you also need things like SOAP etc.?

Rik Hendriks

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and XML

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T12:30:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301231230.449f15ee@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com> <b03qmm$shp$1@mailnews.kub.nl> <b04889$fk2$4@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Ubiquitous <weberm@polaris.net> wrote 

> I have no more details as of yet, other than we might be receiving
> accounting transactions from a client in XML format and we want to
> load them into an IMS database. I'm sorry I cannot be more specific 
> right now.

There is a simple XML Parser written in Cobol at Miami-Dade Community
College that works as a state machine (without any go to).  This reads
an XML message into memory and is called repeatedly until the end,
each return replies with the nesting level, tagname, data type and
value.  It doesn't validate though, expecting the XML to be correctly
structured.

I can a EMail a copy to you, plus a test program that I wrote, if you
want.

Writing XML is relatively easy, preferably using a templating
mechanism, I find that my templating code for HTML and/or forms can
also be used easily for XML.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and XML

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T17:24:13
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b044ts$kpt$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
IBM have recently included XML PARSE within thier compiler on the mainframe.

For Windows and Unix, Micro Focus are implementing this in the soon to be
released Net Express version 4.0 and Server Express 4.0 will follow later in
the year.

Micro Focus are also extending file I/O syntax in these products so as to be
able to deal with XML.

www.microfocus.com

```

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:b03m3q$3st$1@news.utelfla.com...
> I am about to rewrite this old COBOL subsystem of mine that
used
> to process flat files that are FTP'd to a mainframe so that
it
> uses XML files instead. As this is totally new to me (I took
an
> introductory class on XML but haven't had any hands-on
experience
> with it yet), I am looking for resources (both on-line and
off)
> on how to approach this. Any suggestions on where to look or
find
> examples of how this can be handled would be greatly
appreciated! :-)

I can't speak to the details of implementation, but I can make
a decent suggestion.  When writing an XML document generator,
consider part of its documentation to be a document schema.
They are fairly simple to do, and can preserve the type
information that's ostensibly discarded in Rik's example.

Here's an example of a schema that describes the aforementioned
document:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<xsd:schema xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">

  <!-- Elements -->

  <xsd:element name="report-query">
    <xsd:complexType>
      <xsd:sequence minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1">
        <xsd:element name="report-ID"    type="xsd:string"
minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1"/>
        <xsd:element name="report-style" type="reportStyle"
minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1"/>

        <xsd:element name="query-data">
          <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:sequence  minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1">
              <xsd:element name="department-ID"
type="xsd:string"/>
              <xsd:element name="the-date"
type="reportMonthlyDate"/>
            </xsd:sequence>
          </xsd:complexType>
        </xsd:element>

        <xsd:element name="show-return"  type="xsd:integer"
minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1"/>
      </xsd:sequence>
    </xsd:complexType>
  </xsd:element>

  <!-- General Data types -->

  <xsd:complexType name="reportMonthlyDate">
    <xsd:sequence minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1">
      <xsd:element name="year"  type="reportYear"
minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1"/>
      <xsd:element name="month" type="reportMonth"
minOccurs="1" maxOccurs="1"/>
    </xsd:sequence>
  </xsd:complexType>

  <xsd:simpleType name="reportMonth">
    <xsd:restriction base="xsd:integer">
      <xsd:minInclusive value="1"/>
      <xsd:maxInclusive value="12"/>
    </xsd:restriction>
  </xsd:simpleType>

  <xsd:simpleType name="reportYear">
    <xsd:restriction base="xsd:integer">
      <xsd:minInclusive value="1999"/>
      <xsd:maxInclusive value="9999"/>
    </xsd:restriction>
  </xsd:simpleType>

  <xsd:simpleType name="reportStyle">
    <xsd:restriction base="xsd:string">
      <xsd:enumeration value="01"/>
      <xsd:enumeration value="02"/>
      <xsd:enumeration value="03"/>
      <xsd:enumeration value="08"/>
    </xsd:restriction>
  </xsd:simpleType>

</xsd:schema>

________________________

You can reference a schema in the document.  (In this simple
example, both documents are in the same folder...)

<?xml version="1.0"?>

<report-query
xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
              xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="report.xsd.xml">
  <report-ID>1289</report-ID>
  <report-style>08</report-style>
  <query-data>
    <deparment-ID>02</department-ID>
    <the-date>
      <year>1999</year>
      <month>11</month>
    </the-date>
  </query-data>
  <show-return>1</show-return>
</report-query>

<!--
01  report-query.
  03  report-ID      PIC 9(9).
  03  report-style      PIC 9(2).
  03  query-data.
    05  department-ID  PIC 9(2.
    05  the-date.
      07  year      PIC 9(4).
      07  month      PIC 9(2).
  03  show-return      PIC 9.
-->

__________________

Even if you do not describe the schema, you can validate the
document with a variety of xml-validators.  This command line
utility had this to say about the example xml:

     6    23    The 'deparment-ID' start tag on line '6'
doesn't match the end tag of 'department-ID'
    11     5    The 'deparment-ID' start tag on line '6'
doesn't match the end tag of 'query-data'
    13     3    The 'deparment-ID' start tag on line '6'
doesn't match the end tag of 'report-query'
                This is an unexpected token. Expected
'EndElement'. Line 13, position 15.
```

Technical Support Magazine, May 2002 has an article on XML at URL below:

http://www.naspa.com/02articlesbymonth.htm

Generation X Meets Tyrannosaurus Rex: Working with XML on the Mainframe
By Larry Kahm

This article describes a new feature in each of two venerable mainframe
products: the XMLGEN utility in Compuware Corporation�s File-AID/MVS
Release 8.8.1 and the XML PARSE statement in IBM�s Enterprise COBOL for
z/OS and OS/390 Version 3 Release 1. The former helps generate XML
documents; the latter makes it relatively easy to obtain data from these
documents.

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message news:<01c2bd03$adb0ec80$1cccf943@chottel>...
> I see the book "Microsoft .NET for Cobol Programmers" by Howard Hinman is
> available.  Has anyone read this book? If so how about a short book review.

Hi,
I've ordered it but it hasn't arrived yet. However, I followed the
course on CD and it is amazing. If it costed 10 times it's actual
price, it would still be worth it. It doesn't lecture you on theory,
or presents pre-compiled examples; every thing is done online, with
you seeing the typos and compilation errors. Really, a jewel. The only
drawback is that at the time my CDs where published, Fujitsu was on
release 1. Since then, they have a version 1a (with more support for
WinForms and WebForms) and the new features aren't covered on the
course.

best regards
Paulo Vieira

## Thread: Help - I need Example of Code to Help Print National Characters — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help - I need Example of Code to Help Print National Characters

- **From:** lcjarvis@sbic.co.za (Lee)
- **Date:** 2003-01-15T23:51:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c4867c8.0301152351.4673836c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi
I need some assistance in creating a program using MVS Cobol (on
mainframe), that will print such characters as ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½.
I have tried the following...
(1) Wrote a program that reads an input file that contains these
characters and writes them to a report. The report does not produce
these characters.
(2) I have moved the HEX value of these characters to the report and
similar results.
I have been informed that an IBM setting on the mainframe called the
CODE PAGE is where the problem lies. The code page contains a
character set which allows a variety of characters to be used. Can
this setting be manipulated to help get the results I'm looking for,
without necessitating a recompile of all other programs compiled on
the mainframe? And will it do the job I'm hoping it to achieve.
Desperate!
```

On Thu, 16 Jan 2003 09:28:50 +0000, Frederico Fonseca
<frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com> enlightened us:

>On 15 Jan 2003 23:51:48 -0800, lcjarvis@sbic.co.za (Lee) wrote:

…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>FF

Just to summarize, in order to produce natinal characters on a report
you need along with your coding two other things:  You need the code
page on the mainframe and you need the characters on the printer
assuming it isn't a laser printer.

You cannot control those things.  Talk to your system's programmer
and/or operations manager.

Regards,

can do nothing, but together can decide that nothing can
be done. - Fred Allen 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

#### ↳ Re: Help - I need Example of Code to Help Print National Characters

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-01-17T01:38:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2bdc8$eba28240$6accf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0301152351.4673836c@posting.google.com>`

```
I have the Programming Guide V3R2 for Enterprise Cobol for z/OS and OS/390
in .pdf foramt and searching it for the words "code page" turned up a lot
of hits.  There is a CODEPAGE compiler option and some sample code.  There
may be more as I had so may hits that I did not look at them all.

Converting from one code page to another
You can nest the DISPLAY-OF and NATIONAL-OF intrinsic functions to easily
convert
from any code page to any other code page. For example, the following code
converts an EBCDIC string to an ASCII string:

77 EBCDIC-CCSID PIC 9(4)BINARY VALUE 1140.
77 ASCII-CCSID PIC 9(4)BINARY VALUE 819.
77 Input-EBCDIC PIC X(80).
77 ASCII-Output PIC X(80).
*Convert EBCDIC to ASCII
Move Function
Display-of
(Function National-of
(Input-EBCDIC EBCDIC-CCSID),
ASCII-CCSID
)
to ASCII-output
RELATED CONCEPTS
�Formats for numeric data� on page 40
RELATED TASKS
�Assigning input from a screen or file (ACCEPT)� on page 29
�Displaying values on a screen or in a file (DISPLAY)� on page 30
�Converting national data� on page 107
RELATED REFERENCES
NUMVAL (Enterprise COBOL Language Reference)
NUMVAL-C (Enterprise COBOL Language Reference)
�ARITH� on page 291
98 Programming Guide

<2c4867c8.0301152351.4673836c@posting.google.com>...
> Hi
> I need some assistance in creating a program using MVS Cobol (on
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Help - I need Example of Code to Help Print National Characters

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-16T21:33:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E276B62.6473F8D4@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0301152351.4673836c@posting.google.com>`

```
Lee wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Desperate!

From what I see of your posting I think your problem is
actually related to NLS (National Language Support).
National Characters are (or were) considered to be $ @ #
(Dollar Sign, AT sign, Number/Pound Sign) -- what you have
in the subject. 

Meanwhile, it is what JES (2 or 3) is defined to do with
printers that counts.

If the printer(s) are not defined to JES (2 or 3) with the
right character set, then you will not be able to print the
special characters you are after.

As was stated by someone else, you will need to talk to
someone in the systems programming area. Probably the
person(s) who handle all the printer definitions.
```

### File Status 35

- **From:** paul.schultz@isinet.com (Paul Schultz)
- **Date:** 2003-01-16T05:11:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<27ac4162.0301160511.4331b25d@posting.google.com>`

```
We are in the process of migrating our existing COBOL programs that
are currently running on the mainframe to a UNIX (RS6000 AIX)
environment. We are using IBM COBOL on the UNIX box.

I am getting a file status 35 error when I attempt to open a file.
Here is the select statement:

SELECT SORTINFO-FILE-IN
       ASSIGN TO SYS015
       ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
       ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
       STATUS IS SORTINFO-IN-STATUS.

Here is the corresponding FD statement:

 FD  SORTINFO-FILE-IN
     DATA RECORD IS SORTINFO-REC-IN
     RECORDING MODE IS F
     RECORD CONTAINS 41 CHARACTERS
     BLOCK CONTAINS 200 RECORDS
     LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
01  SORTINFO-REC-IN                  PIC X(41).

Here is the script that is calling the program:

#!/usr/bin/ksh

SYS015=/proddev/cobol/data/J6CDM210.050

export SYS015

/proddev/cobol/src/cdm210

where cdm210 is the Cobol program.

Any help would be greatly appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status 35

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-16T08:11:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b06eij$jec$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<27ac4162.0301160511.4331b25d@posting.google.com>`

```
Do you know what the permission settings are for that file? I think that is
(often) the problem with "35" on Unix-type systems.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Status 35

- **From:** Vaclav Snajdr <vsn@snajdr.de>
- **Date:** 2003-01-16T17:39:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b06jjm$u6s$05$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<27ac4162.0301160511.4331b25d@posting.google.com>`

```
File status 35 means that "an open with i-o, input, or extend
has been tried on non-optional file that is not present"

you can explain you file as

export dd_SYS015=/proddev/cobol/data/J6etc

then you write - assign to "SYS015"

or you declare

01 SYS015       pic x(100)      value
"/proddev/cobol and so on"

then you write - assign to SYS015

Servus Vaclav
------------------------------------------

has not been initialized to a numeric value. An error 163 happens,
for example, when the content of a 'pic 9' field is a space.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - Strange error message. Help?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-17T11:25:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2gb2e7ppekscf@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301170215.4760abc8@posting.google.com> <v2fmutmhbiek08@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301170728.76986844@posting.google.com>`

```
This combination should be fine.

        move start-fn to fn-reqd.
        perform
            accept-function-key through file-manager
        until fn-reqd = quit-program.

The reason removing the 'until' worked is that the problem occurs
in the comparison. 'fn-reqd' had a space instead of a number.
*That* was the "Illegal character in numeric field." The comparison
is done before the procedure is performed. So Animator stops
on the 'perform'.

As for the "strange" behavior, we have been there before.
Either source or INT may be out-of-sync with the IDY. Was the
highlighted line, the perform, when you examined 'fn-reqd' ?


Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:fb10cc3a.0301170728.76986844@posting.google.com...
> Rick: 'fn-reqd' is defined as follows:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v2fmutmhbiek08@corp.supernews.com>...
> > Off-hand, I would suggest that 'fn-reqd' is a numeric field that
> > has not been initialized to a numeric value. An error 163 happens,
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >          <snip>
```

> compiled .INT and .IDY files, and then rebuild, the initialization of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> VisOC's project control system?

Maybe! I never used the product, so I know nothing of its
maintenance history or revisions.

Known problems with using the product are documented in
'doc' files in one of the subdirectories where the product was
installed. For the system I have, it is 'cobol\docs'. Sometimes
"work arounds" are provided. You might choose to review the
files in your 'docs' subdirectory.

If the problem is with the product, the final revision with fixed
'bugs' might be available at their web site.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - Strange error message. Help?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-17T18:08:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2846CF.EB1C303B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301170215.4760abc8@posting.google.com> <v2fmutmhbiek08@corp.supernews.com> <fb10cc3a.0301170751.15be998d@posting.google.com>`

```


Paul Richards wrote:

> Rick: further to my reply, it seems that if I delete previously
> compiled .INT and .IDY files, and then rebuild, the initialization of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> VisOC's project control system?

Paul,

Rick has already pointed out where you are having a problem.

As regards your last comment, I doubt it very much. I originally suggested that you delete
compilation files and rebuild, to see if that would highlight your problem - it gave you the 173
'Program Not Found'. Having to delete compilation files and rebuild the project is not a panacea
for all ills- in fact it would be a lousy compiler if you had to keep doing this all the time.
(There is another compilation file - xxxxx.msg, listing compilation error messages. Delete that
also along with the INT and IDY files. Pure speculation on my part, but I suspect that VERY, VERY
occasionally, the compiler gets out of sync because of  the errormessage file).

It's aeons since I used VISOC but I can say with a fair degree of confidence that my OO code in
Net Express V 3.1 would work with VISOC. Sure, VISOC had bugs which people found, but I never
came across them, bearing in mind the OO syntax was an entirely new baby. What you are doing with
VISOC for Screen handling is an extension of what was well proven in the DOS (Text) V 3.0
compiler, long before VISOC was introduced.

As you know from the IDE to compile you can choose - (1) CTRL F7 - recompile current source, (2)
F7 - Build all programs changed and  (3) Rebuild ALL. Until you are totally comfortable with the
product, choose # 2. When satisfied the project is complete, then you can opt for #3 to make sure
all is kosher.

One thing I have found - something doesn't work and I say to myself, "Bloody compiler !". I solve
it, then say to myself, "You dumb bugger - it's your fault !". <G>

Please confirm, are you using the editor in the VISOC IDE or are you jumping out to your
'favourite' Editor to create/change your source ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: OT: Cost of manuals — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Cost of manuals

- **From:** Gary Drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-17T18:48:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E28A471.67C16A4E@sbcglobal.net>`

```
It was brought to my attention, forcefully I should add, that the cost
of manuals is VERY high. Looking back three years, I found what my
company charged for the manuals needed to do about anything needed in
COBOL development,

COBOL             $40
OS-1100/2200 PRM  $40
MASM-1100         $40

Total            $120

I quit counting after $500 for Windoze and any COBOL version...


Why? Does an API interface and third-party compiler cost that much more
to print?

Perhaps there is a little profit built into the pricing? 


Gary
```

```

Sorry, I forgot this punchline on costs of converting to the new
paradigm...

www.actscorp./technologyshowcase/boothill.htm

Gary
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Cost of manuals

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T00:12:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA524102032FAFBD18541F00@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3E28A471.67C16A4E@sbcglobal.net>`

```
On Fri, 17 Jan 2003 19:48:49 -0500, Gary Drummond wrote
> Perhaps there is a little profit built into the pricing? 

Printing does cost a lot. It costs even more in small quantities. You 
listed some Unisys manuals. Unisys makes almost its entire 
documentation library available on the web at 
http://public.support.unisys.com/os1/txt/web-verity?type=list to all 
comers, and on CD to customers, whom it encourages to make copies. As a 
result, when it prints manuals, the quantity is very small -- and 
correspondingly expensive.

In any case, those prices don't look that bad. I remember that some 30 
years ago, a B-6700 COBOL manual cost around $30. Whereas now, if you 
are willing to live with viewing it on screen, it's free.

Edward Reid
```

## Thread: Prentations from last August's SHARE — 3 msgs

### Prentations from last August's SHARE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-17T23:43:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0apii$7e0$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
To comp.lang.cobol people:

I know that they were LATE in getting posted, but the presentation
(slides/handouts) from last August's SHARE in San Francisco are now
available online at:

  http://www.share.org/proceedings/sh99/share.html

(it takes a long time to load)

Of particular interest to (some) CLC readers would be:

   "What's New in COBOL for z/OS"

at:
 http://www.share.org/proceedings/sh99/SHARE/data/S8211.pdf

Some CLC readers might want to "expand" the "Sessions by Number" option on
the homepage  and do a search on "COBOL" and/or "LE" and see such sessions
as:

 8203 - 1001 Things to Do With a Mainframe COBOL App.
 8207 - LE Setup and Customization
 8208 - Diagnosing Application Problems Under LE
 8211 - What's New in COBOL for z/OS
 8216 - Assembler Issues When Migrating to LE and/or Amode 31
 8219 - Introduction to IPCS for Application Programmers
 8230 - Abend-AID and LE
 8231 - How to Perform an LE Migration Assessment
 8234 - Writing User Condition Handler Routines for Language Environment
 8239 - What Is ILC and How to Use It
 8240 - Migrating COBOL & PL/I Run-times to LE - Common Issues
 8241 - Migrating to COBOL Compilers Under LE
 8244 - Using the Edge Portfolio Analyzer for LE Migration

Obviously, other sessions (such as CICS or IMS or DB2 - or even Java) might
interest some of you.
```

<a id="t-0eff75d882"></a>
## Thread: Trend of Cobol programming? — 20 this month / 22 total

### Trend of Cobol programming?

- **From:** "John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-19T17:44:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
  I find it amazing that company's consider tossing out anything as
reputable and solid as cobol.  A professor at my university recently told me
that 80% of existing code is written in cobol and that 20% was written in
other languages predominantly c++.  He continued to say that 80% of NEW
development is being done in c++.
  I understand that company's want the new visual, gui, look on their old
mainframe applications, so the ceo can see how his assetts are doing from
his web enabled cell phone while he's golfing in australia.
  But honestly...  With the invention of such gui cobol environments as
fujitsu visual cobol, and others, it would seem they can have the  best of
both worlds.  So why even think about using another language.
  I program in many languages, but I absolutely love cobol.  It's
straightforward, it makes sense, and it's procedural so it goes step by
step.  I like cobol because you don't have 500 things happening
simultaneously ( as can be the case with object oriented languages such as
C++ and others. )
  Is traditional cobol dying?  What do you think?
```

#### ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T00:05:47-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0fbc6$ncg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
John Hastings wrote:

>   I find it amazing that company's consider tossing out anything as
> reputable and solid as cobol.  A professor at my university recently told
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> development is being done in c++.

80% of all statistics is made up.

While C++ may be used in a significant amount of new development, so is C, 
VB, Java, C#, Python, PERL, PHP, ASP, Kylix, and many others, including 
Cobol.

I doubt that any single language can justify a claim to as much as 20% by 
any measure.

> fujitsu visual cobol, 

Fujitsu's GUI is called PowerCobol.  Visual Cobol is a completely different 
product from another company.

> I like cobol because you don't have 500 things happening
> simultaneously ( as can be the case with object oriented languages such as
> C++ and others. )

Having '500 things happening simultaneously' is not a function of the 
language itself, but of an environment where threading and callbacks 
require re-entrancy.  Given that Cobol can exist in these environments, may 
be threaded, and can be re-entrant, then it, too, can have '500 things 
happening simultaneously' just like the 'others'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T14:51:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hu2o2vo9ou6a4bfn9htjtoagoolrod9mnd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net> wrote:

>  I find it amazing that company's consider tossing out anything as
>reputable and solid as cobol.  A professor at my university recently told me
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

John:

At the risk of upsetting your professor, you might ask him to cite his
sources for his statement that 80% of new development is being done in
C++.

Although I personally do not have the facts, I have doubts about his
claim.

As a student, you are taught to seek knowledge.  When an educator
makes a claim purporting it to be fact, I think that it is reasonable
to ask where you might find such facts published in order to expand
your own knowledge.

I suspect that his claim may be more opinion than fact, but as I
stated earlier, I am not aware of the actual facts.

...just don't get yourself in hot water over the issue.....it's not
worth jeopardizing your course grade.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T08:45:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net> wrote in message news:<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com>...
> I find it amazing that company's consider tossing out anything as
> reputable and solid as cobol.  A professor at my university recently told me
> that 80% of existing code is written in cobol and that 20% was written in
> other languages predominantly c++.  He continued to say that 80% of NEW
> development is being done in c++.

That would completely exclude all the other languages in existance.

>   I understand that company's want the new visual, gui, look on their old
> mainframe applications, so the ceo can see how his assetts are doing from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> both worlds.  So why even think about using another language.

Languages are easy to mix.  The GUIs in larger applications don't tend
to run on the same giant server or mainframe that hosts the
application, but on smaller client machines or via a web browser with
network connections.  The more things change, the more they look the
same.

>   I program in many languages, but I absolutely love cobol.  It's
> straightforward, it makes sense, and it's procedural so it goes step by
> step.  I like cobol because you don't have 500 things happening
> simultaneously ( as can be the case with object oriented languages such as
> C++ and others. )

Objects happen in step by step sequence, but they do so automatically,
thus freeing the programmer from keeping up with all his states.  It
takes some time to get used to it, but it has some distinct
advantages.  And Cobol is starting to stick its toes into the
object-oriented waters.

>   Is traditional cobol dying?  What do you think?

Traditional-Cobol is dead.  But so is Traditional-C,
Traditional-Pascal, Traditional-Basic, Traditional-Fortran.  There
have been many extensions, enhancements and improvements to Cobol that
let it keep up with the industry.

BTW, I noticed the knology.net address.  Are you at CSU?

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger
zberger_AT_knology.net
```

```
Define dead?  well, I don't suppose he means dead, like dead languages...
There will be people who will continue to use a product 10 years after it is
'dead'.  So to those people it's not dead at all...  Now...  To my
opinion...  Describing a language as dead is really relative.  Because some
people may still use it every day.
   As an example, after I took a college course in ASP programming,
microsoft came out with C # (see sharp), which more or less makes accessing
access (and other databases) through html with the ASP bridge obsolete.  In
fact, you develop a web enabled database program in 5 minutes in C# that
might take you 5 hours in html and ASP.  LOL.  amazing how quickly things
change.
  But I can't help it.  I absolutely LOVE cobol.
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T08:53:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com> <b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 21-Jan-2003, jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Define "dead".

No longer taught; superseded; in sparse usage for maintenance only.

Even IBM, that bastion of backwards compatibility, has removed support
for all but the latest Cobol compilers.  And those latest compilers,
as well as the standard Cobol language have evolved away from their
primitive roots to something more modern.

In that sense, "traditional-Cobol" is dead, it has been replaced by
"modern-Cobol".  Just as K&R C is dead, replaced by modern C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T13:05:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2EDD4D.213598B7@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com> <b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
<snip>
> > Define "dead".
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> "modern-Cobol".  Just as K&R C is dead, replaced by modern C.
<snip>

I think you have contradicted yourself and are drawing
invalid conclusions. Similar to someone I know that went to
Purdue and thinks he knows the IT world cold.

COBOL is still taught. It is still used for new development
(I'm still using it, and I'm a mainframe systems programmer
that would rather being doing everything in ALC!). I also
know that it is still taught because I've seen current
curricula listing it.

In fact, I'm looking at doing a migration from one computer
language to another, where the target language may well be
COBOL (390 or LE, I can't keep IBM's names for COBOL
straight). The company that wants this is not a dying
company that is stuck in the past.

Meanwhile, IBM is working at dropping support for all of its
older systems, period. Why? How would you like to support
MVS/SP5, OS/390 V2 r4-10, z/OS 1.x, VM/HPO, VM/ESA, z/VM,
DOS/VSE, DOS/VSE/ESA, z/VSE, and all the various levels of
products that run on/with them? The ISVs have those kinds of
headaches and would love for IBM to kill off some of those
older systems so they could stop having to have a way to
support them. By getting to ONE level of these various
products and their families, the expense for support is cut
tremendously.

Examples of support where multiple releases of COBOL must be
supported: IMS 4.1 (should now be dead), 5.x (again, should
now be dead), 6.x, 7.x, CICS V3 thru CICS/TS 1.3, CPCS, DB2,
and shall I go on? And some of those products run on
multiple O/S versions/releases. You can see perhaps why IBM
would like to cut down on support requirements?

Again, that IBM is getting everyone up to the current
release of COBOL so that they can use *ONE* LE runtime for
PL/1, C, C++, COBOL, etc. is no indication that the language
is DEAD.

Now, what exactly is MODERN COBOL? Is this the '85 Standard
COBOL, or is this the 200x Standard of COBOL? Interesting
that I mentioned STANDARD here. The language is ALIVE and is
growing because there is a STANDARDS committee that works at
defining standards for the language. Why would there be such
if the language was DEAD?

It is also interesting to note that the COBOL Language has
been defined such that it can incorporate (or, is that
encapsulate) OO! I've used that as well and expect to be
doing more.

Therefore, it is my assertion that your definition of DEAD
is rather misleading and myopic.

I shall continue to a short degree with "K&R" C. The code
that I've written in "K&R" C is still quite functional, and
still compiles, and runs, using my Borland C++ environment
as well as my Linux environments! I can still write code on
that basis and it will still compile. Now will I have a
problem with the standards? Could be, but what I write seems
to match the standards and libraries.

I think that declaring a computer language DEAD requires
knowing that there is NO development or support in and for
that computer language.

Candidates (off the top of my head) for DEAD status:

NEAT3
Autocoder
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T13:16:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301231316.425f615c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com> <b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com> <3E2EDD4D.213598B7@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<3E2EDD4D.213598B7@ix.netcom.com>...
> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> <snip>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Purdue and thinks he knows the IT world cold.

I just think you are either misunderstanding me or you failed to
actually read what I wrote.

> COBOL is still taught. It is still used for new development
> (I'm still using it, and I'm a mainframe systems programmer
> that would rather being doing everything in ALC!). I also
> know that it is still taught because I've seen current
> curricula listing it.

I have to beg to differ here.  Most of the IT programs I have looked
at (and I looked at a lot through the 90's) have a core language,
usually C/C++, Java or VB, with some Pascal hangers-on.  The all
required a quarter or semester of Cobol, Fortran, Lisp and/or some
Assembler.  But none had a core language of Cobol (or assembler, lisp,
prolog, etc).  What you get out of these programs is a 4-year C++
programmer (or Java or VB) that has been exposed to Cobol.  In this
case teaching C++ is not the same as teaching Cobol.  The students are
not going to leave the university looking for Cobol jobs, but C++
jobs.  If you ask them they will say "I'm a C++/Java/VB programmer".

If you do know of a university that actually does have a core language
of Cobol in its CompSci programs I'd love to know where it is.

I use Cobol-85 every day as well for new development, though I too
would prefer assembler.

(FTR, I found plenty of Micro$oft based programs and plenty of Unix
based programs and plenty that were mixed.  Some even threw in Macs. 
Almost none had any mainframe in them and none had mainframe as the
core focus of the program.)

><much snippage/>
> Now, what exactly is MODERN COBOL? Is this the '85 Standard
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> if the language was DEAD?

Interesting.  I mentioned it as well, as in Joe Zitzelberger said
"latest compilers, as well as the standard Cobol language".  And I
also indicated that it was very alive and well.  I only suggested that
the older Cobol was dead.  Some things like EXAMINE and TRANSFORM and
OTHERWISE and ALTER/DEPENDING ON have gone away.  Lots of new things
have been introduced.  The latest Cobol does not look very much like
the earliest Cobol.  Cobol is dead, long live Cobol.

> It is also interesting to note that the COBOL Language has
> been defined such that it can incorporate (or, is that
> encapsulate) OO! I've used that as well and expect to be
> doing more.

Didn't I actually imply that with "evolved away from their primitive
roots to something more modern."

> Therefore, it is my assertion that your definition of DEAD
> is rather misleading and myopic.

How many places still teach and/or develop in Flowmatic? Cobol-66?  Is
it dead?
What about Cobol-74?  Life support?  Cobol-85?  Healthy?

> I shall continue to a short degree with "K&R" C. The code
> that I've written in "K&R" C is still quite functional, and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to match the standards and libraries.

Breaks some standards and requires compatibility switches for the
compilier.  If you turn off those switches all of your function
signatures will break.  But, using my defination of "Dead" -- K&R C is
not taught anymore, what you get today is C-98 or C-99 (whatever it
takes).  New development is not done in K&R C.  And K&R C has indeed
been super-C-eeded.
 
> I think that declaring a computer language DEAD requires
> knowing that there is NO development or support in and for
> that computer language.

So what you are doing is lumping all versions of a language together
and if any version or offshoot is still in use the entire collection
is ALIVE?  In this case, support is a problem.  If you get some
Cobol-74 to compile with a Cobol-85 compiler, haven't you actually
created Cobol-85?  If your K&R C can function (pun intended) on a new
C compiler without changes or compiler compatibility flags then isn't
it really new C?

> Candidates (off the top of my head) for DEAD status:
> 
> NEAT3
> Autocoder

A few of my candidates for DEAD status are:

All forms of line-number BASIC.
Any pre-77 Fortran.
Any pre-74 Cobol.
B is dead.  So are the early C's.

My critera for DEAD status is a bit more flexible.  I just try to
imagine the reaction I would get trying to get people to join a
development project in that language.  For example, if I were to
suggest "We should write the new enterprise web services framework in
Cobol-66"...your reaction should serve to indicate the deadness of
that language.

Of course, there was much misunderstanding of my original post.  I
clearly said that Cobol was alive and well, only that some things had
fallen by the wayside.  I'm not sure how many years it takes to make
something "traditional", but I know it when I see it.  Perhaps
"historical" would be a better term.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T20:45:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA57566800FD87061831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com> <b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com> <3E2EDD4D.213598B7@ix.netcom.com> <dcedb8d0.0301231316.425f615c@posting.google.com> <3E30BD81.3A308D64@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 23 Jan 2003 23:12:44 -0500, Arnold Trembley wrote
>> A few of my candidates for DEAD status are:
>[...]
> ALGOL 

Algol as used for systems programming on the Unisys NX/LX line (MCP 
systems, descended from the Burroughs B6700) is very much alive. I've 
probably written 30-35,000 lines of it in the past decade, and I'm far 
from alone. Of course, this language is about 80% extensions to Algol60 
-- but then Algol60 wasn't a complete programming language.

It hasn't changed much. With some fairly minor exceptions, programs 
written 30 years ago will compile and run today. Does this make it 
traditional, modern, dead, alive? Hoare's comment about Algol and its 
successors comes to mind.

Edward
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> Even IBM, that bastion of backwards compatibility, has removed support
> for all but the latest Cobol compilers.  And those latest compilers,
> as well as the standard Cobol language have evolved away from their
> primitive roots to something more modern.
 
> In that sense, "traditional-Cobol" is dead, it has been replaced by
> "modern-Cobol".  Just as K&R C is dead, replaced by modern C.

Note that 'modern-Cobol' compilers will still compile 'traditional-Cobol' 
programs.  In fact most 'modern C' compilers will still compile K&R C 
programs too.

You come across as a typical VBer who thinks that all languages are like MS 
ones where rewrites are required by new versions of compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T13:59:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301231359.37d2a23f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301210845.7aa793bb@posting.google.com> <b0jv30$lis$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com> <b0mpkr$sc6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<b0mpkr$sc6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs too.

If your pre-current-level Cobol compiles with a current-level Cobol
compiler sans compatibility switches then it is by definition a
current-level Cobol source program.

Your K&R source will fail if you turn on strict checking of the ANSI C
standards, specifically function signatures, that most C compilers
allow by default, some with a warning.

> You come across as a typical VBer who thinks that all languages are like MS 
> ones where rewrites are required by new versions of compilers.

I never said any such thing.

The original post was comparing C++ to traditional Cobol and
questioning if traditional Cobol was dead.  How do you read that? 
Since new Cobol has most of the elements of C++ and context suggested
that traditional Cobol did not, I can only assume that the poster
intended older Cobol.

This is an apples and oranges comparison.  You could compare
traditional Cobol to traditional C?  Or modern versions of each, but
how do you cross those lines?  Even if you don't use the modern
features (OO, et al) they are still there and available, that does not
automatically make the instance program traditional.  Therefore, what
is traditional and how does one get to it?  I suggest that you can't
if you are using new hardware and new compilers -- even if you don't
use new features.

Additionally, there is no need to be insulting.  I am neither typical
or a VBer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

If it compiles with a 20 year old compiler too then it is by definition a

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T06:32:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301240632.6ac864eb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com> <b0mpkr$sc6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <dcedb8d0.0301231359.37d2a23f@posting.google.com> <b0ppi8$fpo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<b0ppi8$fpo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'traditional-Cobol' source program.

There are some things that can't be mixed.  BLL cells come to mind,
also some obsolete keywords.  Cases where a clear line if distinction
could be drawn between the old and the new.

A statement like:

     IF A = B ADD 1 TO C.

is timeless.  It could exist nicely in any Cobol program since the
early 60's.  The thing that will tell if the program is traditional or
modern is if its source contains "EXAMINE" or "INSPECT"?  One is
'traditional' and will cause problems with the new compilers.  One is
'modern' and will cause problems for the old compiler.  They cause you
to make a choice in updating the module.
 
> > Your K&R source will fail if you turn on strict checking of the ANSI C
> > standards, specifically function signatures, that most C compilers
> > allow by default, some with a warning.
> 
> So, you do agree then that K&R C can still be compiled by recent compilers.

Only with extreme measures.  I'm sure there are Cobol compilers that
will accept Cobol-66 source if the right flags are set.  Does that
mean you should set them?

This is something like the "EXAMINE"/"INSPECT" problem.  If you write
all of your functions in K&R style and without prototypes your modern
compiler will puke unless the various flags are turned on (or off). 
If you turn those flags to non-ANSI then some other things will fail.

There really isn't a nice way to compile something like:

        greet(x$)
        int x$;
        {
         for(;x$>0;x$=x$-1) printf("hello world");
        }

with an ANSI C compiler unless you use the hacks to ignore the missing
prototype, bad variable name, and old style function signature.  If
you use those hacks then you will lose some of the features and
function that ANSI C brought you.  It's like trying to mix objects and
methods with your BLL Cells and 'TRANSFORM' verbs.

You will lose compatiblilty with the old compiler, yet not have the
features of the new compiler.  Worst of both worlds.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T12:22:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0s6sa$4c6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301220853.15a4843c@posting.google.com> <b0mpkr$sc6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <dcedb8d0.0301231359.37d2a23f@posting.google.com> <b0ppi8$fpo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <dcedb8d0.0301240632.6ac864eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Joe,
  While I don't recommend "mixing old & new" coding features, you are
showing your IBM mainframe "bias".

With Micro Focus COBOL (for example), it is ENTIRELY possible to mix

   EXAMINE & TRANSFORM & OTHERWISE & OO & Intrinsic-Functions

all in the same source code.

Each compiler-vendor decides when (if ever) to drop support for "older"
coding techniques and syntax.  IBM (when they introduced VS COBOL II, R1 -
in the mid '80s) decided to DROP syntax support for some - but not all -  of
their older extensions. (Even then, they weren't part of the "current"
standard language definition.)  This is why VS COBOL II, R1.0 - although
*basically* only a '74 Standard compiler didn't support some of the old
language such as EXAMINE, TRANSFORM, READY TRACE, etc.

Micro Focus (on the other extreme) *never* drops support for previously
supported language constructs - even "bugs" in earlier compilers (cf. their
"OLD-NEXT-SENTENCE" directive).

Most compiler vendors don't go to the extreme that Micro Focus does - but
almost all continue support for "older" language - if they think their
customers still want/need it.  (See IBM's support for the CMPR2 compiler
option - supporting the '74 Standard all the way thru IBM COBOL for OS/390 &
VM - but finally dropped in Enterprise COBOL).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trend of Cobol programming?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T19:40:09-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0s1ni$m5a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<v2maeml8id5k3c@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0301231359.37d2a23f@posting.google.com> <b0ppi8$fpo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <dcedb8d0.0301240632.6ac864eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

>> If it compiles with a 20 year old compiler too then it is by definition a
>> 'traditional-Cobol' source program.
> 
> There are some things that can't be mixed.  BLL cells come to mind,

Well _of_course_ if you have 'modern' features in a source program then it 
is not a 'traditional-Cobol' program.

> also some obsolete keywords.  

'Obsolete' does not mean that the compiler won't compile the code correctly.

> It could exist nicely in any Cobol program since the
> early 60's.  The thing that will tell if the program is traditional or
> modern is if its source contains "EXAMINE" or "INSPECT"?  One is
> 'traditional' and will cause problems with the new compilers.  

Not necessarily, many compilers still compile EXAMINE perfectly.

> One is 'modern' and will cause problems for the old compiler.  

_Of_course_ if it no longer compiles with a 20 year old compiler then it is 
no longer 'traditional-cobol'.

> They cause you to make a choice in updating the module.

No.  My compiler still has EXAMINE (though I don't use it).

>> So, you do agree then that K&R C can still be compiled by recent
>> compilers.
> 
> Only with extreme measures.  I'm sure there are Cobol compilers that
> will accept Cobol-66 source if the right flags are set.  

Which particular flags were you thinking of ?  It is entirely possible that 
a particular source program can be compiled with a Cobol 66 compiler and 
with a modern compiler without any odd flag settings at all (it just may 
avoid all the issues that the flags change).

> If you write
> all of your functions in K&R style and without prototypes your modern
> compiler will puke unless the various flags are turned on (or off).

No. That may depend on the compiler.  Ones that I have used just give a 
warning and carry on correctly.

> If you turn those flags to non-ANSI then some other things will fail.

_Of_course_ if it uses modern features then it is not a K&R C program.

> There really isn't a nice way to compile something like:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>         }

It compiles fine with 'gcc greet.c', no flags, no hacks, with no warning or 
errors (when a main() is added) and works exactly as expected.  If you 
write K&R C then you should get exactly what is written.

> with an ANSI C compiler unless you use the hacks to ignore the missing
> prototype, bad variable name, and old style function signature.  

No hacks required, it works straight out of the box.

> If
> you use those hacks then you will lose some of the features and
> function that ANSI C brought you.  

_Of_course_ if you want modern features then you won't be using 
'Traditional K&R C'.
```

Another message whichI can't see why I should be getting....

Main program scrnmgr.cbl calls filemgr.cbl with a required-function
parameter (it's there 'cos I've checked in animator)....

call part of scrnmgr.cbl:

file-manager section.
         if fn-reqd not = quit-program
            call "filemgr" using fn-reqd.

fn-reqd contains a valid parameter. Program halts at first line of
'display-message section' with two messages:

(a) 'Execution point does not exist'. WhenI click OK I then get
(b) '203 CALL Parameter not supplied <path>\debug\filemgr.int'


filemgr.cbl (this is just a skeleton program at the moment):

*--- Generic File Management program. 30th December 2002
       program-id. FileMgr.
       file-control.
         <snip>
       working-storage section.
       01 char                        pic x.
       01 message-line                pic x(60).
       linkage section.
       01 reqd-function               pic 9.
          78  record-add              value 1.
          78  record-update           value 2.
          78  record-delete           value 3.
          78  record-search           value 4.
          78  quit-program            value 5.
    
           <snip>
      *--- standard screen colours
       copy "colsfg.wks".

      *--- screen definition
       screen section.
       copy "msgscrn.ss".

       procedure division using
                 reqd-function.  <<<< contains valid call parameter
 
        evaluate reqd-function
          when record-add
            perform add-recd
          when record-update
            perform update-recd
          when record-delete
            perform delete-recd
          when record-search
            perform search-recd
          when other
            move "Data Key entered" to message-line
            perform display-message
        end-evaluate.

        exit program.

       add-recd section.
         move "Add record" to message-line.
         perform display-message.
       update-recd section.
         move "Update record" to message-line.
         perform display-message.
       delete-recd section.
         move "Delete record" to message-line. 
         perform display-message.
       search-recd section.
         move "Search record" to message-line. 
         perform display-message.

       open-files section.

       close-files section.

       display-message section.
         display message-scrn  <<<< error messages
         display message-line at 2503.

 message-scrn is a single line , reverse video. mesage-line displays
over it.
