##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - ANOTHER Strange error message.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T15:29:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2omg39rjnhl88@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301200033.6c03dbfd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301201125.1e59ae1b@posting.google.com>`

```
I believe that placing the linkage section after the screen section
is not correct sequencing of the data division. Screen section
items may refer to linkage section items.

The syntax summary shows:

data division.
file section.
working-storage section.
local-storage section.
linkage section.
communication section.
report section.
screen section.

I believe this, with the exception of 'communication section.' is
the order of presentation specified for the 2002 standard.

What you suggest may work but could fail when the program
is changed or the compiler replaced.

I probably wouldn't have mentioned this were it not for your
reminding me to think about 'the next programmer'. <g>


Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0301201125.1e59ae1b@posting.google.com...
> paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards) wrote
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                  reqd-function.  <<<< contains valid call parameter
```

- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301200033.6c03dbfd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301201125.1e59ae1b@posting.google.com>`

```

```
Others have talked about getting your data division sections in the right
order, but may I point out that you have:

 call "filemgr"
   but
program-id. FileMgr.

    ***

Depending upon various Micro Focus compiler directives
   "filemgr" may or may not match FileMgr

that is case may or may NOT be important.
```

<a id="t-c0ee75cc1f"></a>
## Thread: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data — 8 msgs

### UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer)
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T06:37:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone who runs COBOL on UNIX/LINUX but is connected to WINDOWS 
machines through a network help with the following 2 questions?

1. Is it possible to access data files from WINDOWS programs such as 
AUTOCAD, EXCELL, MS SQL? 
ie both reading the data into a COBOL program and writing data out to 
a new file.

2. Can you ever call WINDOWS programs from UNIX/LINUX?
Am I right in thinking that you can't call big programs like 
Outlook/Excell and that the only way would be to use a COBOL compiler 
on WINDOWS.

David
```

#### ↳ Re: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T11:33:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301201133.266840e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com>`

```
david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer) wrote 

> Can anyone who runs COBOL on UNIX/LINUX but is connected to WINDOWS 
> machines through a network help with the following 2 questions?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> a new file.

Unix and Linux can mount Windows shares into the file system and thus
gain read/write access to the files on that machine(s).

 mount -t smbfs -o username=name,password=pw //winmachine/windir
/mnt/windir

smbfs = SMB file system (Windows/Samba).  Files in that share will be
accessed in the /mnt/windir directory.
 
> 2. Can you ever call WINDOWS programs from UNIX/LINUX?
> Am I right in thinking that you can't call big programs like 
> Outlook/Excell and that the only way would be to use a COBOL compiler 
> on WINDOWS.

Well you can't 'call' a program to cause it to run on another machine,
but you may talk to other programs using sockets.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T09:24:39
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0j36o$8iv$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301201133.266840e@posting.google.com>`

```
>
> Well you can't 'call' a program to cause it to run on another machine,
> but you may talk to other programs using sockets.
If you are using Micro Focus COBOL it's possible to use CCITCP2 to do remote
procedure calls to the other computer.

www.microfocus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T08:08:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9026f4e.0301210808.d5b6cbd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301201133.266840e@posting.google.com>`

```

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

Thanks for that. Samba looks very interesting.

>  
> > 2. Can you ever call WINDOWS programs from UNIX/LINUX?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T20:26:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301222026.7da95d1f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301201133.266840e@posting.google.com> <a9026f4e.0301210808.d5b6cbd@posting.google.com>`

```
david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer) wrote 

> > smbfs = SMB file system (Windows/Samba).  Files in that share will be
> > accessed in the /mnt/windir directory.
> 
> Thanks for that. Samba looks very interesting.

You don't need to be running Samba in order to mount Windows shares. 
Samba allows Unix/Linux directories to be seen by Windows (or smbfs)
as shares.

That is: On Linux Samba is the server end of an SMB share, 'mount -t
smbfs ..' is the client end.

> > Well you can't 'call' a program to cause it to run on another machine,
> > but you may talk to other programs using sockets.
> 
> Can you NEVER launch a Windows program (eg Excel on users local PC) from 
> a COBOL program running on a server (which could be ANY of UNIX/LINUX/WINDOWS)?

You can launch a Windows program, but only by talking to a program on
the Windows machine in some way and asking that to launch it.


I sometimes use a very simple batch file that is in the startup of a
Windows machine.  This checks a specific directory on the server for
batch files:

       :DoAgain
       CD N:\REOMOTE\NODEnn
       FOR %%F IN ( *.BAT ) DO CALL RUNBAT %%F
       DOSSleep 60
       GOTO DoAgain

Where N: is a share on the local network server, nn=node numer for
this machine, RUNBAT.BAT will execute the .BAT file and will then
delete it.  Then all it takes is to dump a batch file into the server
directory for the appropriate machine.
This is used where I need to run a specifically Windows or DOS
program, the application just writes/copies the appropriate file.
Not sophisticated but it does the job.
```

#### ↳ Re: UNIX COBOL interacting with WINDOWS programs/data

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T18:54:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<965r2vovf0cir7cpl48rjn3daufvvdiu77@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0301200637.1e97f67d@posting.google.com>`

```
david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer) wrote:

>Can anyone who runs COBOL on UNIX/LINUX but is connected to WINDOWS 
>machines through a network help with the following 2 questions?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>a new file.

David:

With the Flexus Thin Client, data files resident on the Windows client
machine can be copied to the server platform and made available to the
COBOL program running on the UNIX server.

The file can then be copied back down to the client machine.

>2. Can you ever call WINDOWS programs from UNIX/LINUX?

Thin Client allows you to execute remote programs.....either executing
client-side programs from the server or executing server-side programs
from the client.

>Am I right in thinking that you can't call big programs like 
>Outlook/Excell and that the only way would be to use a COBOL compiler 
>on WINDOWS.

Not true.  I'm quite sure that you could execute these programs
remotely.  With Thin Client and our COBOL sp2 product, you could even
embed Active X e-mail and spreadsheet objects into the client-side GUI
screens and control them from the UNIX server-based COBOL program.
This would allow you to have the e-mail and spreadsheet capabilities
directly in your COBOL program and avoid the necessity of calling the
external programs.......besides.....what if a particular client uses
Eudora or Quattro Pro?  That would create problems.  An embedded OCX
would be the same for all clients.

Thin Client and COBOL sp2 is a very powerful and easy-to-use method of
"Internet-enabling" your COBOL programs.  

The software is also COBOL compiler indpendent and operating system
independent.  Your choice of a UNIX server combined with Windows
clients is an outstanding choice.......in my opinion, an extremely
reliable and powerful client/server platform.  Our experience is that
it provides you with the best of both worlds.......a superior server
platform combined with the world's most popular desktop environment.

Send a private e-mail message to me at rtwolfe@flexus.com if you have
additional questions or would like to evaluate the software.





Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-944883f26a"></a>
## Thread: Power Cobol & API — 2 msgs

### Power Cobol & API

- **From:** "pty0" <xxx@clix.pt>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T17:06:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0WW9.1769$RN1.903237@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
I'm looking for the API function or another method that allows me to
identify who's using an EXE file in a network. This is, I have an EXE file,
for example MENU.EXE, that is shared by other users in a network (Windows
2000), and I want to know if this EXE is active (being used by someone) and
if possible to know who's using it.

Thanks
Joe
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol & API

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T20:17:57-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0hid8$lfm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<e0WW9.1769$RN1.903237@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
pty0 wrote:

> I'm looking for the API function or another method that allows me to
> identify who's using an EXE file in a network. This is, I have an EXE
> file, for example MENU.EXE, that is shared by other users in a network
> (Windows 2000), and I want to know if this EXE is active (being used by
> someone) and if possible to know who's using it.

Have the program write a record into a shared file on the server and delete 
that record when the program exits.  Use the machine name and process-id 
(or window handle) as record key as there may be more than one copy running 
(especially on TSE).

Of course spurious records may become lodged in the file if client machines 
crash or reboot or ...  Have the server startup delete the file or use some 
other clean-up.
```

<a id="t-cd4f6a5d06"></a>
## Thread: PowerCobol for free. — 9 msgs

### PowerCobol for free.

- **From:** "John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T12:13:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2obeaadq7a0cc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I had recently posted that fujitsu made a visual cobol which was free.  What
I meant by this is that the product itself is 'visual' as in 'visual' c++ or
'visual' basic.
  Fujitsu actually called their version power cobol but it is a 'visual'
product.  This caused some of the older programmers to become upset with me,
because they used a product years ago called 'visual cobol' which is no
longer sold on the market.
  Power Cobol is graphical, just like visual c++, or visual basic. You
simply click and drag buttons, edit boxes, etc, and then write the code
(procedure) underneath.
  Since the product does develop applications for the pc, hpux, solaris, (
and the copy i had also said AIX ), and the product claimed you could write
a visual product on the pc and transfer it to a unix variant as a gui app.
  I have not tried that, I have only made executable programs on the pc, and
since it is a free compiler with a lot of functionality and ease of use, I
thought a lot of other people might enjoy using it.
  PowerCobol was actually given away on a cd in the back of the  book 'cobol
for dummies', and several other good books on cobol.
  I know that there are some gui enabled forms of unix, by which i mean,
rather than the command line type prompt, you can actually run a 'windows
like' environment on your unix mainframe and run gui apps.
  At any rate I apologize for the confusion to all the old-timers who were
rattled at my inappropriate misnaming of an old no longer marketed product.
```

product.
>
>

Danny.

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol for free.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T00:01:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net> wrote

> I had recently posted that fujitsu made a visual cobol which was free.  What
> I meant by this is that the product itself is 'visual' as in 'visual' c++ or
> 'visual' basic.

Well actually 'Visual C++' and 'Visual BASIC' are trade marks for
specific products and these are not generic terms.

>   Fujitsu actually called their version power cobol but it is a 'visual'
> product.  This caused some of the older programmers to become upset with me,
> because they used a product years ago called 'visual cobol' which is no
> longer sold on the market.

'Visual Cobol' is also a trade name and 'visual' is not a generic
term.

>   Power Cobol is graphical, just like visual c++, or visual basic. You
> simply click and drag buttons, edit boxes, etc, and then write the code
> (procedure) underneath.

>   Since the product does develop applications for the pc, hpux, solaris, (
> and the copy i had also said AIX ), and the product claimed you could write
> a visual product on the pc and transfer it to a unix variant as a gui app.

I think that you are very confused.  Please supply a reference to
where 'PowerCobol' can build a GUI app that can be run on HPUX or
Solaris (forget AIX). Now it is true that Fujitsu does say that
_PowerForm_ can build print forms on Windows and these can be used
with the HPUX and Solaris Unix versions, but 'PowerCobol' ? I don't
think so.

>   I have not tried that, 

Obviously not, nor have you even read of it.  PowerForm is not
PowerCobol and is not a GUI builder.

> I have only made executable programs on the pc, and
> since it is a free compiler with a lot of functionality and ease of use, I
> thought a lot of other people might enjoy using it.

Note that being an academic licence there is no grant of rights to
distribute applications created with the free versions.

>   I know that there are some gui enabled forms of unix, by which i mean,
> rather than the command line type prompt, you can actually run a 'windows
> like' environment on your unix mainframe and run gui apps.

Well Duh. X Windows system, as used on Linux predates Microsoft's
Windows:

"Today, when most desktop computer users see the word windows, they
think of Microsoft's operating system. That's too bad, because long
before Microsoft Windows was a twinkle in the eye of Bill Gates, Unix
had its own windowing system -- the X Window System."

>   At any rate I apologize for the confusion to all the old-timers who were
> rattled at my inappropriate misnaming of an old no longer marketed product.

You are confused again.  PowerCobol is still part of NetCobol for
Windows version 6 which is listed as a current product.  Or were you
'misnaming' Visual Cobol as 'visual cobol' ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCobol for free.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T20:51:18-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0mt4c$tr6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<v2obeaadq7a0cc@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0301210001.5493f025@posting.google.com> <v2ruapahoaq16e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
John Hastings wrote:

> yes Richard, I use 'visual' interchangeably with gui like development
> environments to indicate a sort of continuity and similarity between those
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> on a form, with pretty click buttons, etc.  

To you it may be, but then there is so much that you seem to get wrong, 
probably from a lack of experience and understanding. 

There are is fact _actual_ 'visual languages' where the 'programming' is 
done using graphical representations of building blocks and the structure 
and logic of the program is created by drawing links between them.  That 
is, the 'source code' is only seen visually and not texturally.

With 'Visual C++' and 'PowerCobol' there is still textuaral source code to 
be written and it is only the user interface that is designed 'visually' 
(actually graphically).  These may be 'Visual development environments', 
but they are far from being 'visual languages'.  

In fact the 'visual' part has little to do with the language itself.  With 
Windows 2.x and 3.x the primary way of developing graphical user interfaces 
was 'visually' using the Dialog Editor. This is completely independent of 
the lanuage used.  Later some code generators would take the resource file 
and would generate various source code for different languages to interact 
with that dialog box.  The Dialog editors were enhanced and could accept 
more details about each element which was then used in the code generation.

'Visual C++' is just a visual deveopment environment (ie Dialog Editor) 
that has a code generator, code management and compiler for C++.  In fact 
the code tools could equally be for other languages (and are also) without 
changing the 'visual' parts.  It doesn't make C++ into a 'visual language'.

At least IBM got the naming of their products correct with 'Visual Age 
_FOR_ Cobol' and 'Visual Age _FOR_ Smalltalk' (though they are often 
written wrongly - without the for).

Now 'Visual C++' and 'Visual Cobol' are trade names referring to specific 
products.  A generic term would be 'visual development for <language>'. 
PowerCobol is not 'visual Cobol' but is 'Visual development for Cobol'.

Of course, clueless newbies wouldn't know the difference.

> Not the old way of designing console applications.  

I have done 'console applications' 'visually' for more than two decades. 
MF's Forms2 was out in the late 70s, as was SourceWriter and many others. 
Draw on the screen what you want the user interface to look like and add in 
the code to do the processing.  Currently I do my Cobol console apps with 
SP2 designer console version.  Of course this will also run as graphical in 
suitable environments.

Even 'Visual BASIC' (trade name) was a 'console application' originally.  
VB for DOS was used to draw the user interface visually and the user added 
source code.

So, contrary to your assertion, it _is_ the old way of designing console 
applications, just enhanced and extended.

I noticed that you didn't address any of the other issues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCobol for free.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T23:29:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301222329.59e96cdc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2obeaadq7a0cc@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0301210001.5493f025@posting.google.com> <v2ruapahoaq16e@corp.supernews.com> <b0mt4c$tr6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E2F1715.8090803@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert: I think what you are referring to is modelling language-based
software tools which, from model diagrams, can generate code. For
example UML (Universal Modelling Language) tools particularly (but not
solely) suited for OO-based design - e.g. the toolset from Rational
(now being taken over by IBM I think), Popkin. Most of these tools can
generate C++ and/or Java. The only one I know of which could generate
COBOL is a tool from MicroGold software (can't remember the name of
the tool.)

There is a free UML-tool at http://argouml.tigris.org written in Java
although I don't think it can yet generate code of any kind.

HTH

Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3E2F1715.8090803@nycap.rr.com>...
> Richard Plinston wrote:
> > John Hastings wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> purchase/download one of these 'visual languages'.  Thanks.
```

## Thread: Types of Cobol ? — 5 msgs

### Types of Cobol ?

- **From:** "John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T12:01:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com>`

```
  I would like to know what are the major types of cobol, and what operating
systems they run on.  What kinds of cobol run on the pc?  I know that
'power' cobol by fujitsu will run on the pc, unix, hpux, and solaris.  Are
there versions uniquely designed for the pc?  what about the IBM mainframes?
what about other types of mainframes?  Thank you for your time, and your
polite answer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Types of Cobol ?

- **From:** elendil <member23120@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T20:57:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2413896.1043096224@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Another type of cobol available to you is acucobol.  It offers many
advantages.  Outside of a mainframe OS, and an Apple OS it runs on every
other platform.  Like java, it's portable - compile once and deploy
anywhere.  The makers of it have also built in a lot of extensions, such
as the ability to build graphical interfaces, make calls to databases,
run a cobol program on web server as a cgi program or run in a web
browser as a plugin, etc.  It's also very reliable.
```

#### ↳ Re: Types of Cobol ?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-01-20T20:03:23-12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0hhhv$l4f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com>`

```
John Hastings wrote:

> systems they run on.  What kinds of cobol run on the pc?  

Several and on many operating systems such as Unixware, Open Server, Linux, 
MS-DOS, System Manager, and something called Windows too.

> I know that
> 'power' cobol by fujitsu will run on the pc, unix, hpux, and solaris.  

No.  Fujitsu Cobol is available for Windows, Linux (RedHat), Solaris and 
HPUX.  'PowerCobol' is only for Windows.

> Are there versions uniquely designed for the pc?  

What exactly are you referring to when you say 'pc'.  The first 'pc' was 
the Apple ][, since then it has been used for many different machines but 
mainly the 'IBM PC' (or clone).  However these can run a variety of 
operating systems including Unix, Linux and Windows.

If you mean 'Windows' then say so.

'PowerCobol' is uniquely for Windows, though it probably shares compiler 
code with the other versions.  Some other compilers have been MS-DOS only.

> what about the IBM mainframes? what about other types of mainframes?  

I can't think of a mainframe that wouldn't run Cobol, but there may be some.
```

#### ↳ Re: Types of Cobol ?

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T09:37:13
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0j3ub$8kn$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com>`

```
see www.microfocus.com

Micro Focus has various COBOL compiler products that run on Windows, Linux
(RedHat & SuSE), Unix (Solaris, HP-UX, AIX, Tru64, Dynix, SCO[Caldera]
OpenServer, OpenUnix and UnixWare and others).

The Windows product is NetExpress which is as you describe in another post
as "Best of Both Worlds" allowing development of character, gui, html, com,
mts, java-wrapped etc etc applications and programs in cobol. It allows
interoperability with .NET and a full integrate .NET product is under
development.

For the major Unix platforms there is Server Express which allows
development of cobol applications allowing interoperability with java.
Server Express also allows the deployment of HTML applications developed
using Net Express.

For the Minor Unix platforms and Linux there is have Object COBOL Developer
suite. This is as Server Express but does not support multi threading and
Java interoperability.

From late February there will be a Server Express for the zSeries
implementation of Linux.

For developing mainframe applications on the PC there is Mainframe Express.
This allows you to use the power of your PC to edit test and debug your
mainframe COBOL application before deploying it to the mainframe. This can
save you lots in terms of connection costs and CPU usage costs.

Micro Focus also have product set called Revolve which is a very powerful
application code analysis tool.

"John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net> wrote in message
news:v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com...
>   I would like to know what are the major types of cobol, and what
operating
> systems they run on.  What kinds of cobol run on the pc?  I know that
> 'power' cobol by fujitsu will run on the pc, unix, hpux, and solaris.  Are
> there versions uniquely designed for the pc?  what about the IBM
mainframes?
> what about other types of mainframes?  Thank you for your time, and your
> polite answer.
>
>
```

- **From:** bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs)
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T18:23:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ca5032.0301251823.7b78ecb6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v2oann566vcg58@corp.supernews.com>`

```

> I would like to know what are the major types of cobol, and what operating
> systems they run on.

There's Compaq COBOL, formerly called DEC COBOL, DIGITAL COBOL, VAX
COBOL, DIGITAL VAX COBOL, and probably to be renamed HP COBOL shortly.
 Runs on OpenVMS Alpha, OpenVMS VAX, and Tru64 UNIX.

http://www.openvms.compaq.com/doc/cobol.html

<a id="t-f2812000f3"></a>
## Thread: COMP field — 5 this month / 16 total

_Thread started [2002-12](2002-12.md#t-f2812000f3)._

#### ↳ Re: COMP field

- **From:** Jess��� Goncalves <member4962@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T14:04:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2416870.1043157899@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2134416.1039204895@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi there,

sorry to post a new question, but my doubt is almost equal yours. I'm
not a Cobol Programmer.

I have some microbase tables, and within there are some fields defined
like comp-3. I need to "translate" it, but I don't know how it's work.
Somebody know the compressed format, that I could do the reverse to
uncompressed.

Thanks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP field

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T12:36:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E2D851D.1A7C8C29@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<2134416.1039204895@dbforums.com> <2416870.1043157899@dbforums.com>`

```
"Jessï¿½ Goncalves" wrote:
<snip>
> I have some microbase tables, and within there are some fields defined
> like comp-3. I need to "translate" it, but I don't know how it's work.
> Somebody know the compressed format, that I could do the reverse to
> uncompressed.
<snip>

First we need to know which COBOL you are using and on what
platform.

This is needed as in the COBOL that I use, COMP-3 is a
packed decimal field that is not used on too many platforms
other than an IBM mainframe (e.g., WANG VS systems use it,
but Intel does not).

But let me assume that you are talking about an IBM
mainframe system.

Packed decimal (COMP-3) is represented as follows:

Assume that you have an area in your record that looks like
this: nnnnns  

where n= a digit 0-9 and "s" is the sign. The sign in this
case is x'F' for positive (actually absolute), x'C' is
positive, x'D' is negative.

Now the field above could have been defined in a COBOL
program as:

 PIC 9(5) COMP-3 
 PIC 9V9999 COMP-3
 PIC S999V99 COMP-3

etc.

So, knowing what platform (architecture) you are starting
with and the COBOL you are using would make showing you
exactly what is going on much easier.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP field

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T13:40:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0301211340.79e13c30@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2134416.1039204895@dbforums.com> <2416870.1043157899@dbforums.com>`

```
Jess  Goncalves <member4962@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<2416870.1043157899@dbforums.com>...
> Hi there,
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

You don't specify the platform on which the files/table are held, but
presuming that COMP-3 is IBM's packed-decimal format, then the
following may help.

COMP-3 numbers are held in a compressed format such that there are two
digits per byte with the exception of the rightmost half byte, which
holds a sign instead.  In hexadecimal notation an example such as +123
in a two byte filed would be seen as follows '123F' or '123C', where
the 'F' and 'C' both represent the positive sign.  -789 would be
represented as '789D', where the 'D' represents the negative sign. 
The other hexadecimal letters A, B and E are rarely encountered as
signs, since they aren't automatically generated by any of IBM's
instructions, but would translate as either positive or negative
signs, though I can't remember which.  The easiest way to do the
conversion is to use a utility or write a program and change them all
to display format with separate signs.

Good luck

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP field

- **From:** sanghabum@aol.comjunkless (Sanghabum)
- **Date:** 2003-01-21T22:39:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030121173907.25866.00000108@mb-me.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<fcd09c56.0301211340.79e13c30@posting.google.com>`

```
>The other hexadecimal letters A, B and E are rarely encountered as
>signs, since they aren't automatically generated by any of IBM's
>instructions, but would translate as either positive or negative
>signs, though I can't remember which.

FACE -- positive
DB -- negative

I can't remember if -0 is allowed (0B or 0D), so assume it is.

As Robert says, this is IBM packed-decimal format. You may have something else
called COMP-3.

Colin.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP field

- **From:** Jess��� Goncalves <member4962@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-22T13:38:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2422168.1043242705@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2134416.1039204895@dbforums.com>`

```

Thanks.

I'll try these way. Just something else: There's some driver to read
this files? Remember that the database is Microbase. Could I read it
with Micro Focus?

Thaks again.
```

Hello,

I need to convert a PIC 9(8) date field YYYYMMDD into something that
I can use in an SQL-statement, like DD.MM.YYYY. I know that I can redefine 
this field and move the day, month and year to a new field seperately. But 
I thought that one of the "new" functions on COBOL (z/os) could do that 
more elegantly (like INTEGER-OF-DATE etc.).

But none of the functions I looked at seem to work for this.

Is there a function to do this?

A.

> IMO we have a legitimate case for using MOVE CORRESPONDING here

The new FUNCTION LOCALE-DATE requires only one line
in the procedure division. No redefinitions in the data division.
One entry in SPECIAL-NAMES, I think. I don't know if it is available.

Why? International portablility.

#### ↳ Re: Actual comments on SORT

- **From:** "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T02:51:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9udnbUC0LVb3a-jXTWcow@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<b0mq2g$12u$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I also lost interest in the original thread, so don't know if anyone
presented the following.

The original posting asked for comments about internal vs. external sorts.
It was pointed out to me several years ago that internal sorts, where Input
and Output Procedures are used, as opposed to Using/Giving, were frequently
more efficient than using an external sort.  Obviously, if the required
processing can be accomplished via sort parameters, then that's the way to
go.  But, assuming that those conditions don't apply, consider the case
where you have a data source (data base or flat file) from which you need to
extract data, sort it, and produce a report.

Using an external sort, you would have the following steps:

1) extract program:
reads the data source
writes extracted data to an output file

2) sort step:
reads file from step 1
writes to intermediate sort storage
reads from intermediate sort storage
writes to output file

3) report program:
reads output from sort step
writes report file

If those steps were replaced with a single program that included an internal
sort with Input and Output procedures, you would have the following:

1) Input Procedure:
reads the data source
writes extracted data to intermediate sort storage
2) Output Procedure:
reads from intermediate sort storage
writes report file

So the use of an internal sort eliminates two entire read/write passes of
the extracted data.  It's frequently pointed out that the sort utility's I/O
is much faster than that of an application program.  But not having to do it
at all is even faster.

Tom McFarland


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b0mq2g$12u$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...

> entire thread with the subject related to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

This will get mentioned time and again... but...

SORT FIELDS=(startpos1,len1,type1,A/D,startpos2,len2,type2,A/D...)
INCLUDE/OMIT COND=(startpos3,len3,operand1,value1,startpos4,len4,operand2,value2...)

... or something like that.

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T19:10:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
The problem with the type of sort below is that the statement is not
self documenting.  For those in the IBM mainframe world, you are in for
a big surprise if you go to a UNIX shop that has SYNCSORT.  That sort
will allow you to set up inline records definitions or point to COBOL
copybooks.  You can set up sorts that tell someone what fields are being
tested and/or sorted on.  It was like moving from the dark ages.  With
that sort of capability I would be more than willing to have the
SYNCSORT report writer facility in production use.

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> In article <u9udnbUC0LVb3a-jXTWcow@comcast.com>,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

On 27-Jan-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >
> >Which works for simple cases.  But sometimes the conditions will require
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> sort, process) is not universally applicable.

Which I pointed out as well (in a different message).   But we should keep
reminding people that there should not be an automatic, non-thinking best way of
doing things.  That each choice comes with a cost.   In this case, a company
policy that requires internal sorts only or a policy that requires external
sorts only, both have costs.  There is on replacement for thinking and analysis.
```

"Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com> wrote in message
news:u9udnbUC0LVb3a-jXTWcow@comcast.com...
> I also lost interest in the original thread, so don't know if anyone
> presented the following.
>
> The original posting asked for comments about internal vs. external sorts.
> It was pointed out to me several years ago that internal sorts, where
Input
> and Output Procedures are used, as opposed to Using/Giving, were
frequently
> more efficient than using an external sort.  Obviously, if the required
> processing can be accomplished via sort parameters, then that's the way to
> go.  But, assuming that those conditions don't apply, consider the case
> where you have a data source (data base or flat file) from which you need
to
> extract data, sort it, and produce a report.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> If those steps were replaced with a single program that included an
internal
> sort with Input and Output procedures, you would have the following:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the extracted data.  It's frequently pointed out that the sort utility's
I/O
> is much faster than that of an application program.  But not having to do
it
> at all is even faster.
>
> Tom McFarland
>

        This assumes that the amount of data is relatively small.
When there is too much data to hold in memory, the internal sort's
advantages shrink.  Microfocus even has a switch to allow you
to choose from 2 different sort methods - the default tries to
hold the data in memory, the "+B2" switch uses intermediate files.

        I can see where an external sort would have been superior
back when the program's business logic took up a large percentage
of the computer's real memory, but today, the ability to remove
the business logic from memory should not normally make that
much difference, memory wise.
```

extract program, or an extract program isn't needed.

## Thread: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T00:26:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com>`

```
Is the interrogation of file status codes meant to be a complement to,
or a substitute of, the use of 'normal' COBOL verbs? For example, I
can check file status code for End of File, Invalid Key conditions
just as I can READ...END OF FILE, or WRITE ... INVALID KEY.

I realise that file status codes can give more information about the
causes of unsuccessful file operations - the question is it 'either -
or' or 'both'?
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T09:13:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gnOX9.9035$zF6.731883@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com>`

```

Paul Richards <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message news:fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com...
> Is the interrogation of file status codes meant to be a complement to,
> or a substitute of, the use of 'normal' COBOL verbs? For example, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or' or 'both'?

Both.

The code required to test each possible status code
and execute the necessary logic can be extensive.

You normally would not want all those statements
cluttering up your Read / Write statements.

So, in the interest of programming elegance,
you use the AT END or INVALID KEY clauses
to direct program execution out-of-line to your dedicated
status code test procedures, where you can wax complex
to your hearts content, by testing for each and every possible
status code and executing the appropriate response logic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T07:12:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230712.24a271f1@posting.google.com>`

```

AND thoroughness.

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T14:10:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YISX9.943$eZ2.537540@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com>`

```
"Paul Richards" <paul.richards@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com...
> Is the interrogation of file status codes meant to be a complement to,
> or a substitute of, the use of 'normal' COBOL verbs? For example, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or' or 'both'?

You may use both at one time if you want. You can even use AT END .. INVALID
KEY and DECLARATIVES at the same time. That is, if a FILE STATUS IS is in
effect, the explicit verbs work as well, and the order of what gets control
is documented somewhere.

The main advantage of FILE STATUS is there is no method other than
file-status or declaratives to check the success of any OPEN, or to check a
WRITE of a non-keyed file.

Just as a matter of style, I do not 'mix and match.'   For me it's just as
easy to set up a FILE STATUS to query AT END or INVALID KEY conditions...

FILE STATUS is INPUT-FILE-STATUS....

05  INPUT-FILE-STATUS    PIC X(02).
       88   INPUT-AT-END      VALUE  "10".   << or whatever those codes are
      88    INPUT-NOTFOUND VALUE "23".  << sequential
     88     INPUT-DUP-KEY    VALUE "22"   << Indexed or keyed
     88    INPUT-OK              VALUE "00".

      READ INPUT-FILE
      EVALUATE TRUE
         WHEN INPUT-OK
           yadda yadda
         WHEN INPUT-AT-END
          yabba dabba
         WHEN INPUT-NOTFOUND
           tum-de-dum


Works good for me.


MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T08:32:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E30190C.9020506@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com>`

```
The status code is a suppliment for additional information, as well as a 
way of interpeting abnormal conditions (File does not exist, for 
example).  You can get along without it for simple programs, but at the 
systems level, you need it if your code is going to correct error 
conditions.  A common usage, for example, is to create a new file on a 
status 35 (File does not exist).

Donald

Paul Richards wrote:
> Is the interrogation of file status codes meant to be a complement to,
> or a substitute of, the use of 'normal' COBOL verbs? For example, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or' or 'both'?
```

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T23:53:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301232353.162c94f@posting.google.com>`

```

Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3E30190C.9020506@Sympatico.ca>...

> way of interpeting abnormal conditions (File does not exist, for 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T09:05:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E317247.2060304@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com> <3E30190C.9020506@Sympatico.ca> <fb10cc3a.0301232353.162c94f@posting.google.com>`

```
While I seldom code for all types of status codes, there are a couple of 
instances where I do usually code it.  The three that come to mind off 
the top of my head are newly installed systems (where you want to create 
the file), malformed Isam files (where you want to do a rebuild), and 
sharing errors (where either file or record is locked, and you want to 
either wait with a timeout then retry, or give a message that the file 
is in use and stop nicely).

There is really no way to handle any of those situations without looking 
at the file status codes, particularly sharing of files.

On the other hand, there are lots of simple programs that can get along 
without any of the above.  I'd probably not bother, for example, if I 
was writing a program to dump an Isam file to a sequential for backup or 
for conversion to a larger file.  There are default error handling 
routines that will give an error and abort for the weird stuff in a case 
like that, and since the program is being run by a pro, they are 
qualified to read the message and take appropriate action. (like trying 
to dump a file when it is in use by another process).

It is, as you say, not black and white. It is more a matter of how 
sophistocated the program is in handling weird stuff.

Donald

Paul Richards wrote:
> Donald: thanks. As ever, it's  not just a 'black and white' decision.
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T12:15:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0301231215.b88a2e8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com>`

```
paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards) wrote 

> Is the interrogation of file status codes meant to be a complement to,
> or a substitute of, the use of 'normal' COBOL verbs? For example, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or' or 'both'?

Note that AT END / INVALID KEY will not be activated just because a
READ/WRITE is unsuccessful but only for certain conditions.  For
example if you do a START or READ on a file that is not open then you
will not get INVALID KEY branch but there will an unsucessful
file-status.

If you want to test for AT END using the file-status only then you
should check the first byte for '1' and not the two bytes for '10'. 
If you want to test for success then also only check first byte for
'0' as any '0x' file status indicates success, such as '02' on a read.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL - File status codes

- **From:** paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T23:55:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb10cc3a.0301232355.2658c887@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb10cc3a.0301230026.6d4366a7@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0301231215.b88a2e8@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard: thanks. So 'mix and match' is fine - but for comprehensive
status checking it seems file ststuas codes are the way to go.

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0301231215.b88a2e8@posting.google.com>...
> paul.richards@virgin.net (Paul Richards) wrote 
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> '0' as any '0x' file status indicates success, such as '02' on a read.
```

## Thread: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program — 11 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** mircea_tamas@yahoo.com (mircea)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T02:04:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com>`

```
I have to develop a Cobol/MVS program which writes different sets of
data  on different tapes, as distinct files, but with the same DD and
DSN.
Let's say the program writes data related to January on file 'ACCOUNT'
on tape 170001, data related to February on file 'ACCOUNT' on tape
170002, and so on.
The number of required tapes is not known at the start of the program.

Question:
Is there a way to write and close a tape file and after that have it
unload so that a subsequent tape can be loaded and the same program
begin to write on the new tape?

I tried many solutions, but it didn't work.
My last chance was to use:
	CLOSE filename REEL FOR REMOVAL
	CLOSE filename
	OPEN OUTPUT filename.....
as I read in: 
http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&newwindow=1&threadm=B0000107577%40apps.escape.net.au&rnum=1&prev=/groups%3Fhl%3Den%26lr%3D%26ie%3DUTF-8%26newwindow%3D1%26selm%3DB0000107577%2540apps.escape.net.au

When my program tries to open the second file, I get in JES2 LOG the
messages "IEC502E K" and "IEC501A M". That means the second tape is to
be demounted and the first tape to be remounted. Maybe operator can
force here someway to keep the second tape, but I need a clean
solution.

Regards

Mircea Tamas
```

#### ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T11:11:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E301422.AB27FB3F@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com>`

```
mircea wrote:
> 
> I have to develop a Cobol/MVS program which writes different sets of
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> as I read in:
<snip>

One might try using the FREE=CLOSE parameter on the DD
statement. However, I think you will find that this will not
get you the results that you are after. Particularly if you
are running an SMS environment and the tapes are CATALOGED,
or you are running a Tape Librarian product.

You need to have a routine that will dynamically allocate
and deallocate your tape DD name. This way you can "change"
the DSN and VOLSER information (Yes, I know you said the DSN
is the same). Now as to COBOL and LE having standard
routines for doing this, I don't know.

I've written routines like this in ALC using SVC 99 (DYNAMIC
ALLOCATE/DEALLOCATE SVC) where that routine gets called as
many times as is needed. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T19:44:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E308C74.D580A3D@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3E301422.AB27FB3F@ix.netcom.com> <20030123193109.20325.00000334@mb-fo.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
<snip>
> I don't suppose that there is a way to prevent the file from being CATALOGED
> when the file is closed.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for output.
<snip>

If running under SMS, SMS can control the cataloging of the
data set regardless of what is specified in the DISP=! 

The VOL=SER idea that you have is a bit confused. The # of
tapes is per an allocation. If you dynamically release and
reallocate the DDNAME, then the # should be reset. Even if
it is not, during testing one can verify if they will need
to code VOL=(,,,n...) (where n is the "max" number of
volumes to mount).
```

#### ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** Terry Sambrooks <terry@kmsitltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T13:28:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e303446$1_7@news.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

As Steve correctly points out the issue is OS/390 or z/OS Allocation and
the tie with the DDNAME. By the time the program starts the Operating
System has allocated the DDNAME and Volume combination ready for OPEN,
unless the DEFER option is specified on the UNIT parameter, which delays
allocation until OPEN time.

Before going down the Dynamic Allocation route, it might be worth trying
the DEFER option with the existing CLOSE REEL options in the program.

I have a concern about dynamic allocation and retaining the same DDNAME,
as in standard JCL if two DD statements have the same name one is
ignored. I know the requirement was to have the same DDNAME, but to
ensure dynamic allocation works it might be better to change it for each
file. (Being dynamic the DDNAME will be transparent to the JCL.)

The topic of dynamic allocation from COBOL programs has been the subject
of recent discussion in the IBM-MAIN list, so if you have access a trawl
of the archives should provide some help. The alternative to call an
assembler Rouitine.

Hope all the above helps in some way - Terry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T17:04:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0psea$4no$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com> <3e303446$1_7@news.onlynews.com>`

```
One other concern that I would have with this entire "design" is if you ever
need to do a RESTART of the job.  I don't know what application requirements
there are, but often (not always) jobs that require multiple tapes for
output are "long running" jobs that you may not want to have to restart from
the very beginning  - if a "problem" occurs during processing.  With the
design originally described, I don't know how you could ever be prepared for
a restart OTHER than at the very beginning.  If you are dealing with a
production "nightly batch job" with a "close window" - I think you are
building in problems for the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T19:38:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E308B1C.A46B2705@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com> <3e303446$1_7@news.onlynews.com> <b0psea$4no$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> One other concern that I would have with this entire "design" is if you ever
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

Unless there is random processing of files/data base going
on, one likely could make use of the JCL controlled
CHECKPOINT/RESTART facility...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T19:46:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E308CE7.B98E2CA9@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com> <3e303446$1_7@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Terry Sambrooks wrote:
<snip>
> I have a concern about dynamic allocation and retaining the same DDNAME,
> as in standard JCL if two DD statements have the same name one is
> ignored. I know the requirement was to have the same DDNAME, but to
> ensure dynamic allocation works it might be better to change it for each
> file. (Being dynamic the DDNAME will be transparent to the JCL.)
<snip>

Because of dynamic allocation, the two simultaneous DDNAMEs
in a step does not apply. It is because you deallocate and
then allocate that DDNAME that things get changed, the TIOT
gets updated, etc. etc.
```

I have to thank to all posters. I am a kind of newbie on these scene of

Let's summarize:
- I already rejected the solution with FREE=CLOSE , I got some
  sort of error
- I tried UNIT=(CASS,,DEFER) - the jobstep took the first tape and wrote
  the first file, took the second tape (maybe wrote on it), afterwards
  asked again the first tape and again the second, here operator
  canceled the job - it was so awfull, I think it deserve not to dig in
  this direction
- DYNAMIC ALLOCATION can be a solution, I've read previously about that,
  but I wanted to avoid it because it sounds complicated. Perhaps I'll
  try it and I'll post here the results, but after all this adventure,
  I've received a new design of the program. :))

Regards

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tape load/unload from within a Cobol/MVS program

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T17:14:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0301241714.23039d7d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5f25b5cf.0301230204.71170b4c@posting.google.com> <3e31520b_7@news.onlynews.com>`

```
Hi Mircea,

Interesting problem.  I wouldn't call this a solution. It's more a
path to a possible solution.
Since you're using the same DSN and DD, matching the data with the
tape for logging is problematic. You may resolve that by reading the
tape label after (or before) the data is written, then creating a
report listing the tape serial #s and the associated data description.

You may be able to use RETPD=2 (or whatever) to keep the operator from
mistakenly remounting the previous tape

The JCL for the data and the label would look something like this:

//DATADD    DD  DSN=TEST.DATA,UNIT=(TAPE,,DEFER),DISP=(,KEEP,KEEP),
//                    VOL=(,,RETAIN),DCB=(.....),LABEL=(1,SL,RETPD=n)
//LABELDD  DD  DSN=TEST.LABEL,UNIT=AFF,DISP=(OLD,KEEP,KEEP),
//                    VOL=REF=DATADD,DCB=(LRECL=80.....),LABEL=(1,BLP)

You'd need  an FD in your pgm for each of the DDs above. The way I've
written the JCL, you'd have to process the DATADD 1st, CLOSE it, then
OPEN and READ the LABELDD looking for the serial# in the label recs,
then CLOSE that and write a report line. Then loop back and do it all
again until you've processed all your input.

If you want to process the label before the data, some mods to the JCL
are required.
    
You should check with your system guys to verify  what you need to do
to process an SL tape as unlabelled and to verify whether or not
they're using 3rd party tape mgmt software and what impact that may
have on your plans.

It's a little kludgey, but it might work.

Regards, Jack.  
P.S. JIC, check the JCL syntax. I coded it from memory. :-)  


mirceat <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message news:<3e31520b_7@news.onlynews.com>...
> I have to thank to all posters. I am a kind of newbie on these scene of
> COBOL/MVS and I didn't expected so many interventions.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards
```

## Thread: Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico

- **From:** i72gumaj <member23445@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T18:24:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2428909.1043346277@dbforums.com>`

```

Buenas, no tengo mucha idea de COBOL y me gustaria que me
ayudaseis en algo.

Me gustaria saber si es posible convertir caracteres que representan
datos en Hexadecimal a caracteres alfanumericos. Me explico mejor con
un ejemplo:

Si tengo el cadena "3031" (Codigo ASCII) que representa un valor
hexadecimal, quiero obtener su representacion alfanumerica, y por tanto
convertir:
       - el 30 en el 0.
       - en 31 en el 1.

Todo junto devolveria 01.

3031(HEX) = 01(CHR)

El mismo ejemplo para codigo EBCDIC seria F0F1 como dato de entrada que
devolveria justamente la cadena 01.

Muchas gracias de antemano.
```

#### ↳ Re: Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T17:02:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E30909B.5D08F98E@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<2428909.1043346277@dbforums.com>`

```
Lo siento que no puedo contestarle en Espanol, pero si Usted puede entender
Ingles, aqui es un possibilidad:

I am assuming you have an input string with two characters for each
hexadecimal digit, and want to produce a string of the ASCII or EBCDIC
equivalent.  So, the character string '3031' ASCII should result in an
output string of '01', and 'F0F1' EBCDIC should result in '01'.

You could construct a character string of all possible hex digits,
'0001020304....', and REDEFINE the string as an array OCCURS 256 INDEXED
I-X PIC X(02).  Then, use SEARCH ALL to locate the entry that matches your
first two input characters.  Define another array that contains the
Character equivalent and REDEFINE it as OCCURS 256 INDEXED O-X PIC X(01).
SET O-X to I-X.  Then, MOVE the output character from the second array to
your output string.

You could use Reference Modification to access each pair of input
characters and to access each output character.

Another variation would be to define a single array that has the result
character immediately after the two hex characters.  SEARCH ALL would still
work, and you would not need to SET one index to the other.  For example:
'...300311322333344...', and REDEFINE it as
05  ARRAY-ENTRY OCCURS 256 INDEXED I-X.
10  ARRAY-IN        PIC X(02).
10  ARRAY-OUT    PIC X(01).

Estoy seguro que otras soluciones existen.  !Buena Suerte!
=====

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T11:13:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E31823B.6020605@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>`
- **References:** `<2428909.1043346277@dbforums.com> <2431985.1043394173@dbforums.com>`

```
i72gumaj wrote:

> Habia pensado en algunas de esas soluciones, pero creo k el tiempo de
> computo va a ser muy elevado. Fijate que voy a necesitar muchas
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> que hago son un poco delicados en ct a tiempo de computo.

Otra solucion, puede ser un poco mas rapido:

Using reference modification, move two characters at a time into a
PIC X(2) field which is redefined as a 16-bit binary integer.  Use
that integer as a subscript into an array of 4096 (= 256 * 256)
characters.

For ASCII conversion, initialize that array so that:

    A[hex 3330] = '0'  ('30')
    A[hex 3331] = '1'  ('31')
    ...

For EBCDIC conversion, it depends on whether you're running on an
ASCII machine or an EBCDIC machine.  If you're running on an ASCII
machine:

    A[hex 4f30] = '0'  ('F0')
    A[hex 6f30] = '0'  ('f0')
    ...

On an EBCDIC machine:

    A[hex c6f0] = '0'  ('F0')
    A[hex 86f0] = '0'  ('f0')
    ...

This would use more memory, but it might be faster.

You could have your translation program initialize the arrays, or
you could have a separate program create a copybook.

If your input is clean, you might be able to use one array for
ASCII and EBCDIC, since the codes don't overlap.  I'd recommend
having two arrays and initializing unused cells to low values (or
whatever) to catch bad data.

Que le vaya bien...

Louis Krupp
```

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T20:34:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E335748.80404@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>`
- **References:** `<2428909.1043346277@dbforums.com> <2431985.1043394173@dbforums.com> <3E31823B.6020605@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid> <01HW.BA58A57E014C2D1C1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Edward Reid wrote:

> On Fri, 24 Jan 2003 13:13:15 -0500, Louis Krupp wrote
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Um, Louis? 65536? ;-)

OK.  Bad math day.  One whole order of magnitude off.

65536 bytes might be too big for an 01 level on a lot of systems.
Even if it isn't, there might be some caching considerations.

It seemed like a good idea at the time.

Thanks for the wake-up call.

Louis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgente: Convertir Hexadecimal a Alfanumerico

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T12:57:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA5ADD1F01D1692E1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<2428909.1043346277@dbforums.com> <2431985.1043394173@dbforums.com> <3E31823B.6020605@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid> <01HW.BA58A57E014C2D1C1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com> <3E335748.80404@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>`

```
On Sat, 25 Jan 2003 22:34:32 -0500, Louis Krupp wrote
> 65536 bytes might be too big for an 01 level on a lot of systems.
> Even if it isn't, there might be some caching considerations.

OTOH, there's hardly a system around today for which this is simply too 
much memory. If the compiler or some other arbitrary factor doesn't 
limit you, then it's probably the best way to go.

Louis, you've been gone from A-Series a long time IIRC ... the 64K 
limits there disappeared long ago. I just finished implementing a table 
with 80,000 occurrences, totalling about 35MB, for a Unisys NX6800 ... 
and it's not even a stretch.

Are there any compilers in wide use today for which a 64KB table (PIC X 
OCCURS 65536) would be a problem?

Edward Reid
```

<a id="t-186ba6dc31"></a>
## Thread: Signed Numeric ASCII-EBCDIC conversion question — 4 msgs

### Signed Numeric ASCII-EBCDIC conversion question

- **From:** ajwerden@yahoo.com (Andrew Werden)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T14:19:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b77463a5.0301231419.256c93d2@posting.google.com>`

```
How do PIC S9(6)V99 fields with negative values get translated between
EBCDIC and ASCII? As I understand it, value 123.45 will be represented
in EBCDIC as F0 F0 F0 F0 F1 F2 F3 F4 F5, and -123.45 is F0 F0 F0 F1 F2
F3 F4 D5. How does the negative get turned to ASCII? Does D5 (ebcdic
N) get turned into 4E?

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numeric ASCII-EBCDIC conversion question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T16:59:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0ps52$btp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b77463a5.0301231419.256c93d2@posting.google.com>`

```
Neither ASCII nor EBCDIC actually "define" how a sign is stored *unless*
"SIGN IS SEPARATE" is specified.  IBM's "conventions" for internal signs for
EBCDIC data is pretty common in even non-IBM environments that use EBCDIC
(for character data).  When it comes to ASCII, there are several different
"conventions".

Tell us which compiler and on what operating system you are working - and we
can tell you what values are used for "internal" signs (assuming you are
talking about USAGE DISPLAY numeric data - not COMP-????).

Note: Even knowing the compiler and the operating system MAY not be enough.
Some compilers (notably Micro Focus) have a VARIETY of ways of storing
internal signs - depending upon the compiler directives selected.

Bottom-Line: If you want to "know for sure" what is happening when
converting DISPLAY numeric data from one convention to another - use the
SIGN IS SEPARATE clause.
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numeric ASCII-EBCDIC conversion question

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T00:28:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZM%X9.1038$eZ2.609365@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b77463a5.0301231419.256c93d2@posting.google.com>`

```
"Andrew Werden" <ajwerden@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:b77463a5.0301231419.256c93d2@posting.google.com...
> How do PIC S9(6)V99 fields with negative values get translated between
> EBCDIC and ASCII?

Funny you should ask... because I just ran into this this week (again).

The answer is, the overpunched digit gets converted to a different
character, and the resulting numeric value comes out in what is called
"CHARSET ASCII SIGN EBCDIC" -at least that's what Microfocus called it.

How about a really nice FREE tutorial on the topic?

http://www.providerpaymentpartner.com/tsihome_html/downloads/C2IEEE.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed Numeric ASCII-EBCDIC conversion question

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T17:11:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E3092AB.46B04A81@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<b77463a5.0301231419.256c93d2@posting.google.com>`

```
As Bill's reply says, the embedded sign can be problematic.  So, get it
out of the way.

The sensible way to convert is to first, working in either EBCDIC or
ASCII, convert to SIGN SEPARATE (either leading or trailing), or even a
floating sign.  Then, convert to the other representation,  ASCII to
EBCDIC or EBCDIC to ASCII.  Finally, convert back in that target
representation to the original picture.  1985 ANSI COBOL allows MOVE of a
Numeric Edited input to a "normal" Numeric item, so there are plenty of
choices.

This advice goes even more strongly if the input is not Numeric Character
(Packed-Decimal or BINARY)!

Andrew Werden wrote:

> How do PIC S9(6)V99 fields with negative values get translated between
> EBCDIC and ASCII? As I understand it, value 123.45 will be represented
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks
```

---

## Thread: numerical left justified — 28 msgs

### numerical left justified

- **From:** "Christer Olsson" <anybody@telia.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T22:42:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net>`

```
hi,
I want to MOVE a numerical item, 9(10), to an alpabetic
item, X(10), get rid of initial zeroes and have it left justified
(standard procedure I think).

Is there a way of accomplish this without a lot of UNSTRING
and STRING operations?

thanks / Christer
```

#### ↳ Re: numerical left justified

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T16:55:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0prtk$mn7$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net>`

```
> hi,
> I want to MOVE a numerical item, 9(10), to an alpabetic
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and STRING operations?

05  A  Pic 9(10).
05  B  Pic x(10).
05  tally-count pic 99 value zero.

inspect A tallying tally-count for leading zeros.
move A (tally-count + 1 : length of A - tally-count) to B.
```

#### ↳ Re: numerical left justified

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T18:14:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0psi4$eas$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net>`

```
Here's a thought -

03  WS-SUB PIC S9(04) BINARY.
03  WS-HWORD PIC S9(04) BINARY.
03  WS-NUMERIC PIC  9(10).
03  WS-ALPHABETIC PIC  X(10).

MOVE +1 TO WS-SUB.
MOVE ZERO TO WS-HWORD.
MOVE 123456 TO WS-NUMERIC.
*
PERFORM UNTIL WS-SUB > LENGTH OF WS-NUMERIC
        IF  WS-NUMERIC (WS-SUB:1) > ZERO
                MOVE WS-SUB TO WS-HWORD
                MOVE LENGTH OF WS-NUMERIC TO WS-SUB
                END-IF
        ADD+1 TO WS-SUB
        END-PERFORM.
*
IF  WS-HWORD > ZERO
        MOVE WS-NUMERIC (WS-HWORD:) TO WS-ALPHABETIC
ELSE
        MOVE SPACES TO WS-ALPHABETIC
        END-IF.

At this point, WS-ALPHABETIC will contain C'123456bbbb' (the 'b' means a
Blank/Space).

HTH....

Bill

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>

> 77 MY-SAMPLE-NUMBER PICTURE 9(10) VALUE 1234.
> 77 SIMPLE-EDIT-FIELD PICTURE Z(10).
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> are going to write BASIC code?

This does not compile. 'Justified Left' is not valid
on Microfocus nor OS/390. If fixed so it does compile
it doesn't do what the original poster requested.
Simple-alphabetic-field contains '      1234' after the
move. The poster wanted '1234      '.

Again I suggest:

05  A  Pic 9(10).
05  B  Pic x(10).
05  tally-count pic 99 value zero.

inspect A tallying tally-count for leading zeros.
move A (tally-count + 1 : length of A - tally-count) to B.
```

Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3E30E376.1060809@Sympatico.ca>...
> 77 MY-SAMPLE-NUMBER			PICTURE 9(10) VALUE 1234.
> 77 SIMPLE-EDIT-FIELD			PICTURE Z(10).

<Rest Snipped>

Donald,

        I'm not too sure about what you've suggested. Won't the second
statement result in a straight MOVE of 10-bytes to 10-bytes with the
result being "bbbbbb1234" in SIMPLE-ALPHABETIC-FIELD? AFAIK,
left-justification is the default for PIC X fields anyway.

        Perhaps my posting is a bit elaborate (an understatement),
although unlike the "INSPECT" that Ron posted (which is probably the
most elegant and maintainable solution but, requires a Call to a
run-time module), the "Italian food mess" does get resolved quicker
because the code is in-line, although many would claim that "quicker"
is a relative term.

        My point was to show the Lad how to manually perform this
without using the "INSPECT". It's too bad the "INSPECT" didn't resolve
in-line using a TRT instruction, because THAT would be the ticket.
Note that there are formats of "INSPECT" that resolve in-line, using
single a TR instruction and a 256-byte translate-table built by the
compiler in the TGT.

        Sadly, in this day and age, programming and language rudiments
are a thing of the past and there's no time for free thinking.

        And yes, the original post does have the look and feel of
homework but again (IMHO), giving someone the easy answer doesn't
allow them to think about alternatives.

        Just my .02 cents....

Cheers,

Bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T11:13:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0rs6u$8i8$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <b0psi4$eas$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b0q33o$ano$1@reader10.wxs.nl> <3E30E376.1060809@Sympatico.ca> <173f80ae.0301240305.b51fd74@posting.google.com>`

```
How about this to avoid the INSPECT and run-time
call? I think this perform is a little more
straightforward and efficient.

05  A  Pic 9(10).
05  B  Pic x(10).
05  tally-count pic 99 comp.

perform varying tally-count from 1 by 1
  until A (tally-count:1) not = zero
     or tally-count > length of A
  continue
end-perform.
move A (tally-count : length of A - tally-count + 1) to B.

Ron.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T13:35:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3317m46if8sc4@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <b0psi4$eas$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b0q33o$ano$1@reader10.wxs.nl> <3E30E376.1060809@Sympatico.ca> <173f80ae.0301240305.b51fd74@posting.google.com> <b0rs6u$8i8$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```

Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:b0rs6u$8i8$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com...
> How about this to avoid the INSPECT and run-time
> call? I think this perform is a little more
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> move A (tally-count : length of A - tally-count + 1) to B.

This is better; but the conditions for ending the 'perform'
need to be reversed to prevent the test 'A(11:1) not = zero',
when A is zero.

Also, prior to the move, a test is needed to prevent an
out of range condition, as in 'move A(11:0)' and to ensure
that spaces are moved to B if tally-count is greater than 10.

Also, 'length of A - tally-count + 1' is not necessary, the
compiler will take care of that.

Also, 'length of' is a common extension; but is non-standard.
Use 'function length (A)' for standard COBOL conformance.

With the noted changes:

compute length-of-A = function length (A).
perform varying tally-count from 1 by 1
  until tally-count > length-of-A
     or A (tally-count:1) not = zero
  continue
end-perform.
if tally-count > length-of-A
  move spaces to B
else
  move A (tally-count : ) to B.
end-if.

Efficient, bug free(?), and conforms to standard COBOL.

However, it may not be correct because it is not clear
whether 'initial zeroes' means all leading zeros or those
leading zeros which will result in B having at least one
digit, even it that digit is a zero.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
Ron wrote:

> move A (tally-count : length of A - tally-count + 1) to B.

You don't need the complicated length specifier:

       MOVE A(Tally-Count:)    TO B
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b0rvo6$kp1$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Ron wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>        MOVE A(Tally-Count:)    TO B


I gots to know... why on Earth would anyone ever design something using LEFT
justified NUMERIC values?

The only "possible" application I can think of is someone constructing a
numeric value to insert into a line of text, like..

"Please immediately pay past due amount of         1234.56 or we will send
Bruno to nail your kneecaps to the floor."

.. and they do not like the gap between "of" and "1234."  But the OP never
said what the application was.

(In this application, of course, the reader probably never notices the gap:
all he sees is "Bruno," "nail" and "kneecaps").

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T17:04:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T21:08:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA575BD000FECBDD1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <b0psi4$eas$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b0q33o$ano$1@reader10.wxs.nl> <3E30E376.1060809@Sympatico.ca> <173f80ae.0301240305.b51fd74@posting.google.com>`

```
On Fri, 24 Jan 2003 6:05:35 -0500, WOB Consulting wrote
> Perhaps my posting is a bit elaborate (an understatement),
> although unlike the "INSPECT" that Ron posted (which is probably the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is a relative term.

Not all compilers generate calls on general-purpose routines for 
INSPECT. The Unisys NX COBOL85 compiler generates in-line code for 
INSPECT -- AFAIK for all options, certainly for all for which I've 
glanced at the code. That doesn't guarantee it's efficient, of course 
-- if you can do it just as easily without INSPECT, then by all means 
do so. But if doing it in COBOL requires writing a 
character-by-character loop in COBOL, then INSPECT is more efficient on 
these systems, usually quite a bit more efficient.

(But as always, when looking for efficiencies, the greatest gains 
result from changing the algorithm, not from tuning the code.)

Edward
```

- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <b0psi4$eas$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b0q33o$ano$1@reader10.wxs.nl> <3E30E376.1060809@Sympatico.ca> <173f80ae.0301240305.b51fd74@posting.google.com> <01HW.BA575BD000FECBDD1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Edward Reid" <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:01HW.BA575BD000FECBDD1831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com...
> On Fri, 24 Jan 2003 6:05:35 -0500, WOB Consulting wrote
> > Perhaps my posting is a bit elaborate (an understatement),
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> result from changing the algorithm, not from tuning the code.)

Once again we have the specter of micro-efficiency. In my view, striving for
micro-efficiency is inefficient.

I coded the two examples: INSPECT vs LOOP and found that, indeed, the loop
method was more efficient: 1.75 seconds for the loop vs 5.45 seconds for the
INSPECT.

This was for TEN MILLION ITERATIONS!

Assuming you have to "fix" the address line in the Reader's Digest mailing
list of 30 million records, you would save (mumble, mumble, carry the
two...), ah ha! Over ELEVEN SECONDS on my pissant PC each month. But that's
not all!

Assume you had to pretty-print 100,000,000 government checks each month. You
would save (punch, punch on calculator - no, damnit, where's the clear
button...). Yes, I have it. Wait for it now: a whopping THIRTY SEVEN seconds
of CPU time. To print checks made from an entire forest. That's each and
every month!

You can take it to the bank.

We've now visited (as I recall) - by actual example, not legend:

Indexing vs. subscripting
Display vs. binary (as subscripts)
and now
Inspect vs looping

with no practical difference between the left and the right.
```
