hi again, for the record .. I'm designing a program that generates a capital
report file for the tax authority in Sweden. I'm a little bit rusty with
COBOL
since some years has passed since I did my latest programming effort
on our grand old lady, a Data General MV/2500.

I need the left justification to comply to the requirement on the report
file
structure.

Thanks all for your advice. I was interested in knowing if I have missed a
SIMPLE way of doing the MOVE and left justification. It seems there is no.

My own implementation is like:

05  WS-NUMBER    PIC 9(10)
05  WS-ALPHA         PIC X(10)
05  INSP-POINTER PIC 99.

MOVE 1 TO INSP-POINTER.
INSPECT WS-NUMBER TALLYING WS-POINTER FOR LEADING ZEROES.
MOVE SPACES TO WS-ALPHA.
UNSTRING WS-NUMBER INTO WS-ALPHA WITH POINTER INSP-POINTER.

It works, and it compiles on our old AOS/VS COBOL wich I believe is old
fashion
ANSI COBOL, not COBOL85 compliant. (I do not recognize some string handling
that some of you propose)

Thanks / Christer

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T20:07:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-AKdndpnF8FUbKyjXTWckQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <Y0kY9.7821$LY2.466950@newsc.telia.net> <b0siks$4ao$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <xKkY9.7833$LY2.467529@newsc.telia.net>`

```

"Christer Olsson" <anybody@telia.com> wrote in message
news:xKkY9.7833$LY2.467529@newsc.telia.net...
> I tried it, here's the transcript:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes the compiler supplies default values as in   MOVE "A" TO X(3:)

Sometimes the compiler can trap for runaway indices as in   MOVE "A" TO
X(INDX)

Sometimes the compiler can generate code to trap for things like divide by
zero.

In general, program failure caused by this sort of stuff or by an attempt to
reach outside the program is very rare in COBOL. COBOL does not often need
(C-type) pointers - I've actually never seen a program in the wild that used
them.

I think it's more of a programmer attitude. COBOL programmers tend to
manipulate the representations of physical things (widgets in the warehouse,
salary on a paycheck, etc.), and it's easy to imagine how these physical
things interact ("take one down, pass it around..."). C programmers tend to
manipulate more abstract, ephemeral, evanescent things. Sometimes these
gossamer constructs really ARE fictitious.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: numerical left justified

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T20:51:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA58A93F014D0EC21831E100@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<kd_X9.7681$LY2.456687@newsc.telia.net> <Y0kY9.7821$LY2.466950@newsc.telia.net> <b0siks$4ao$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <xKkY9.7833$LY2.467529@newsc.telia.net>`

```
On Fri, 24 Jan 2003 19:19:41 -0500, Christer Olsson wrote
> it seems that at least my run-time system delas with it (well, at 
> least I didn't got a run-time error this time). But I agree, the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> run-time system really do these consistency and range checks for you? 

The ANSI standard specifies that if the POINTER is out of range at the 
beginning of the UNSTRING statement, then the OVERFLOW condition 
exists. If you haven't written an ON OVERFLOW clause in the UNSTRING 
statement, this results in execution passing to the next statement.

In other words, yes, you are correct: the implementation makes the 
checks, and is required to do so by the standard. (Depending on the 
system, this may be the run-time support or may be code generated by 
the compiler.)

However, note that your example shows a blank field for the value 0. 
This probably is not what you want. I don't remember if your example 
was identical to the "real" code you quoted earlier.

Edward Reid
```

### How to optimize a JCL

- **From:** arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath)
- **Date:** 2003-01-23T23:16:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

I am trying to optimize a JCL and cut down its running time (the job
runs for more than an hour now). Are there any specific points I need
to keep in mind while trying to look for bottle-necks in the JCL? Is
there any clue I should be looking for in the job's output spool so as
to figure out whether any optimization is required?... like..

1) should I follow any rules while allocating a dataset. If it is a
tape, can I do something to speed up the loading.
2) Are there any rules for sorting files? ie; if the file is huge,
then will it do any good by splitting them and sorting.

If any of you guys follow certain basic rules while writing a JCL, pls
let me in on your secret.

I have so far aimed at writing jcls that work. Perhaps your thuoghts
on this should be an eye-opener for me to writing more efficient ones.

Thanks in advance,
Arun
```

#### ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T12:08:24+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o0423v4a30rk80ppeltovo3ndnbj6rg04a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 Jan 2003 23:16:44 -0800 arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath) wrote:

:>I am trying to optimize a JCL and cut down its running time (the job
:>runs for more than an hour now). Are there any specific points I need
:>to keep in mind while trying to look for bottle-necks in the JCL? Is
:>there any clue I should be looking for in the job's output spool so as
:>to figure out whether any optimization is required?... like..

You are trying to optimize the job, not the JCL.

:>1) should I follow any rules while allocating a dataset. If it is a
:>tape, can I do something to speed up the loading.

Depends how your operations group is set up.

:>2) Are there any rules for sorting files? ie; if the file is huge,
:>then will it do any good by splitting them and sorting.

SORT does that automagically.

:>If any of you guys follow certain basic rules while writing a JCL, pls
:>let me in on your secret.

:>I have so far aimed at writing jcls that work. Perhaps your thuoghts
:>on this should be an eye-opener for me to writing more efficient ones.

What are the issues?

Too short batch window?

Too much CPU or too much elapsed?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

- **From:** Terry Sambrooks <terry@kmsitltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T06:05:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e311ddc$1_4@news.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com>`

```
Arun,

As Binyamin correctly points out it is the job which you wish to
optimise, changes to the JCL may or may not help depending upon what the
issues are.

Without knowing the precise situation the only thing I can comment on is
what typically causes delay, and probably the biggest culprit is file
BLKSIZE in respect of non-VSAM files, and CI-size for VSAM. Changing
these should only be done however with due regard to other possible data
set uses there are fundamental differences between online and batch
requirements.

Any change to BLKSIZE, CI-Size or the number of buffers will increase
Virtual Storage usage, which may increase Paging Rates so if Storage is
a problem these changes could make that worse.

There may be conflict with other jobs within the system.

Having said all of the above reviewing BLKSIZE, or equivalent and the
number of Buffers would be a good starting point the less I/O performed
the faster the job will run, but be aware of the other issues mentioned.

Monitor any change not just in respect of this job, but for any impact
elsewhere.

Regards - Terry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T10:21:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E314C04.29B27561@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com> <3e311ddc$1_4@news.onlynews.com>`

```
In the IBM environment or any other, be friends with your systems
people.  Get all of the performance statistics you can from them for the
jobs that are taking too much time (this includes finding the jobs). 
Look for logical to physical I-O ratios.  If the jobs are using
databases, then the DBA's should be brought into the loop.  Know the
level of your system and whether the shop standard is to take advantage
of newer (sometimes 10 - 15 year old) functions such as systems managed
volumes.  If your shop is in Canada, I would be willing to consult.

Terry Sambrooks wrote:
> 
> Arun,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> posted via MFF :  http://www.MainFrameForum.com - USENET Gateway
```

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T20:14:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0s6rp$krs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com> <3e311ddc$1_4@news.onlynews.com> <3E314C04.29B27561@istar.ca> <a3ef804e.0301241202.76ce0ba0@posting.google.com>`

```

On 24-Jan-2003, arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath) wrote:

> I was given a job written by someone else and asked to check for
> bottle-necks it may have. This job essentially has a number of sort
> steps interspersed with PROGRAM steps. I wanted to have a sort of (no
> pun intented :-) )checklist before I plunged in. Your pointers will
> certainly help.

Analysis may help you here.  I have seen the same file sorted the same way in
different steps of a job.  I have seen sorts that don't exclude unwanted data
that have to be read in by the program.

Figure out how to get as small of files as you can for each program - this may
involve changing order of runs (if that is allowable).  Do a business analysis
of your needs.  Then test your findings.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T00:12:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NDkY9.2098$rq4.181203@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com> <3e311ddc$1_4@news.onlynews.com> <3E314C04.29B27561@istar.ca> <a3ef804e.0301241202.76ce0ba0@posting.google.com>`

```

Arun Prasath <arunprasath420@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:a3ef804e.0301241202.76ce0ba0@posting.google.com...
>
> I was given a job written by someone else and asked to check for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> certainly help.

1  Determine purpose of each job step.
2  Check the production schedule.  Determine when the job runs.
3  Check the job dependencies.  Determine when prior jobs run
    which create required input files and/or update required databases.
4  In particular, consider breaking earlier jobs up into smaller jobs,
    if that will permit the earlier release of a required database.
   That is a common bottleneck - large jobs tying up files unnecessarily.
5  In addition, consider breaking this job up into smaller jobs,
    if that will permit the earlier release of any database.
   This job me be the cause of a bottleneck, as well as the victim of one.
6  Review database access calls for efficiency, particularly Get Uniques
    being used where Get Nexts would be more efficient.
7  Consider extracting one or more databases to flat files, and possibly sorting them,
    possibly in a new job, possibly run earlier in the batch window.
    This may be more efficient, depending on structure of database,
    and whether or not there is update access or read-only access.
8  Consider creating print files instead of printouts,
    and extract those print steps to a new job/jobs.
9  The production support analysts and the on-call developers
    will all thank you if you remove as many forced abends as you can
    in favor of exception reports and/or emails to a support group.
10 THINK.

The above is by no means comprehensive.  It is just to make you aware of
and thinking about some of the possibilities.

You need to be analyzing and asking questions about !every! aspect of the process,
such as "what does this do, why is it done, and can it be eliminated or restructured ",
and "when is this done, and can it be rescheduled, earlier or later".
```

#### ↳ Re: How to optimize a JCL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T15:17:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E319F4D.63372DE8@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0301232316.565536a4@posting.google.com>`

```
Arun Prasath wrote:
> 
> Hi all,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> tape, can I do something to speed up the loading.
<snip>

If the DD is writing to two or more tape volumes, then you
can code the following:
[//ddname dd dsn=......,disp=(new,...), ] <<== start of the
DD statement
//        UNIT=(????,2,DEFER)             <<== insert in the
DD statement

Where ???? is the ESOTERIC for the tape units (e.g., TAPE,
CART, SQTP), or the GENERIC (e.g., 3490, 3480).

What this will do, assuming that you have enough tape units
available at the time, is the first tape will mount and be
ready to be written to while the second tape is being
mounted. You do not have to wait for the tape to rewind and
unload before you start writing on the second volume this
way. This alone can cut 5-10 minutes out of your *elapsed*
time for processing 2 tapes!

Now if you are READING a file, unless you know for sure that
the file is on tape, things can get ugly coding the UNIT
parameter (all depends on SMS, and the ACS rules in place,
etc.).

<snip>
> 2) Are there any rules for sorting files? ie; if the file is huge,
> then will it do any good by splitting them and sorting.
<snip>

IF and ONLY IF you are doing a MAXSORT (SYNCSORT terminology
here), get the SYNCSORT manual and read on how to optimize
this type of sort. However, in the shop I just did a lot of
tuning for, SYNCSORT for the LARGEST VSAM files was running
less than 1 minute ELAPSED time (KSDS/ESDS files > 6GB).

Otherwise, find out which sort you are using (e.g., DFSORT
from IBM), and read the manuals for it and how to tune
things for LARGE [read that, GIGANTIC] files.

<snip> 
> If any of you guys follow certain basic rules while writing a JCL, pls
> let me in on your secret.
<snip>

As some one else said, become good friends w/ your systems
programmers. They can help you with all kinds of tuning
items. Ask if you are using PAVs (Parallel Access Volumes)
or if you can (some shops can use them, but have not
installed them because of $$$, or your files are on the
wrong "DASD" device(s)). These will allow multiple
simultaneous I/O to the same volume from the same LPAR/MVS
image.

Then there are things such as HyperBuf (CA product), BLSR
(Batch LSR, which is done from the JCL for VSAM files).
REGION size and BUFNO settings, assuming that you have
sufficient REAL (C-STORE) storage, will also help you out.
Then there is how your JOBs are defined to WLM... Does SMS
have your files compressed? How about striped?

All of these things are what your systems programmers work
with on a regular basis. Make them happy and they can make
your life pleasant. Piss them off and life gets real
difficult (how do I know? Because I is one!).
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-01-24T13:56:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1043416515@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Cobol Requirements — 3 msgs

### Cobol Requirements

- **From:** "Texan" <luchesis@ticnet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-25T21:37:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v36lumk2s6gk10@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Isn't there a book that tells the latest requirements for a cobol compiler
as determined by some group or committee? Where would someone obtain this
book? TIA.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Requirements

- **From:** Alexander Meins <un.cr.281202@blue-seal.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-26T09:17:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1487572.6sKe1bMVKu@blue-seal.com>`
- **References:** `<v36lumk2s6gk10@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Texan (luchesis@ticnet.com) schrieb am Sonntag, 26. Januar 2003 04:37:

> Isn't there a book that tells the latest requirements for a cobol compiler
> as determined by some group or committee? Where would someone obtain this
> book? TIA.

Maybe this is what you are looking for:
http://www.iso.org/iso/en/CatalogueDetailPage.CatalogueDetail?CSNUMBER=28805&ICS1=35&ICS2=60&ICS3=
But it's about 260$ to buy...

A.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Requirements

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-01-26T05:32:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0301260532.1fe05eb2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v36lumk2s6gk10@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Texan" <luchesis@ticnet.com> wrote in message news:<v36lumk2s6gk10@corp.supernews.com>...
> Isn't there a book that tells the latest requirements for a cobol compiler
> as determined by some group or committee? Where would someone obtain this
> book? TIA.

Hi,

Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ is a good place to look - 

http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

It has a section on COBOL standards, currently in item 7.

A new standard, COBOL 2002, has just been issued and can be bought
from ISO or NCITS as can COBOL 85.  It looks as though NCITS will be a
lot less expensive than ISO.  While no implementor as yet has issued a
COBOL 2002 compiler, many are already part way there using some of the
facilities proposed during its development, though not always exactly
as per the finalised new standard.

Implementors don't have to implement the full standard, but you would
still find most of the new features in compilers eventually.

Robert
```

<a id="t-bc1a495d63"></a>
## Thread: SORT - E15/E35 Exits — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T09:06:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up to the various SORT threads, I am curious (and I think this
will ONLY make sense to IBM mainframe people),

Does anyone still use E15 or E35 exits?  If so, why/when do you use them
instead of COBOL Input/Output procedures?

(As far as I know, the answer does NOT depend on DFSort vs SyncSort, but if
it does, I would be interested in that too.)
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T10:49:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ycZ9.851$hw4.261372@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I rarely see them anymore because SORT capabilities have overtaken many
(most?) of the reasons that we used to use them.  For example, we used to
write E35 exits to split the results of a sort into multiple files.  That
issue went away many years ago.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...
> As a follow-up to the various SORT threads, I am curious (and I think this
> will ONLY make sense to IBM mainframe people),
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (As far as I know, the answer does NOT depend on DFSort vs SyncSort, but
if
> it does, I would be interested in that too.)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T11:58:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b13rvu$iv5$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Most of the time I don't use them. But a couple of years ago I
had requirement to record the tape volser and the first and last
account number on each output tape of a multi-volume dataset.
I used an E35 because I needed the assembler DCB exit capability.
Also, my former shop had a whole bunch of E15 and E35 exits that
were written in COBOL. Syncsort allows COBOL exits, I don't know
about DFSort but I assume so.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T14:36:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E357C21.AE515C80@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <b13rvu$iv5$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
I think that the HP-UX version of SYNCSORT also allows E15/E35 exits.

Ron wrote:
> 
> Most of the time I don't use them. But a couple of years ago I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> about DFSort but I assume so.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T14:24:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E35B1AF.3070502@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <b13rvu$iv5$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
Ron wrote:

>Most of the time I don't use them. But a couple of years ago I
>had requirement to record the tape volser and the first and last
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, DFSORT allows both Assembler and COBOL E15 and E35 exits.  For 
details, see Chapter 4 of "DFSORT Application Programming Guide" which 
you can access online at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ICECA109/4.0?DT=20020722140254 
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T13:33:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E357B6E.F513FACC@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> As a follow-up to the various SORT threads, I am curious (and I think this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> instead of COBOL Input/Output procedures?
<snip>

I don't know about DFSORT, but for SYNCSORT, one needs the
input exit (15?) to process multiple VSAM input files. You
can't concatenate VSAM for input as you can SAM (QSAM
typically).

The only other reason for the exits is where you need to
do/handle things that SORT can't necessarily deal with, such
as short records where the selection criteria occurs after
the record ends -- I typically run into this with SMF data.
Some records happen to have special cases where a normal
item you need to check is beyond the length of a special
"short" record. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T16:13:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E35CB39.1060303@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3E357B6E.F513FACC@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:

>I don't know about DFSORT, but for SYNCSORT, one needs the
>input exit (15?) to process multiple VSAM input files. You
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that's true for DFSORT as well  - you cannot concatenate VSAM input 
files.  Actually, I believe it's a "system" restriction that you can't 
OPEN concatenated VSAM files.
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T02:56:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030127215611.20883.00000163@mb-fq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I believe we have one or two jobs left that use the exits (not sure which one
at the moment) but most were removed during the Y2K frenzy.  Removing them from
a few jobs caused some abends because the output wasn't the same as the
original format but that was corrected by some concantinations etc.

I think that it was used mostly when a VSAM files was the input but that isn't
required any more, and I believe it was used for merging as well.

Most of it was left over from the old DOS days of the shop.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T14:49:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MLwZ9.104$DY3.94842@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <20030127215611.20883.00000163@mb-fq.aol.com>`

```
"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20030127215611.20883.00000163@mb-fq.aol.com...
> I believe we have one or two jobs left that use the exits (not sure which
one
> at the moment) .....I think that it was used mostly when a VSAM files was
the input but that isn't
> required any more, and I believe it was used for merging as well....
> ...Most of it was left over from the old DOS days of the shop.

Sometimes I am amazed at just how much "because that's the way we have
always done it" code is maintained and upgraded, or just blindly "ported"
job-step-by-job-step or verb-by-verb to the next generation of software or
hardware.

 Wouldn't you think that when making major upgrades to old code developers
would at least check what year it is?

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T09:35:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b164g2$clj$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <20030127215611.20883.00000163@mb-fq.aol.com> <MLwZ9.104$DY3.94842@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <MLwZ9.104$DY3.94842@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Sometimes I am amazed at just how much "because that's the way we have
>always done it" code is maintained and upgraded, or just blindly "ported"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>would at least check what year it is?

About as much as I would think that when upgrading to a newer version of 
the language - say, COBOL74 to COBOL85 - that old code developers would be 
given formal instruction in the changes in the language's features...

... or about as much as I would think that when upgrading to a newer 
version of the language - say, COBOL74 to COBOL85 - that old code 
developers, having their requests for formal instruction in the changes in 
the language's features refused by management, would take it upon 
themselves to research said changes...

... or about as much as I would think that when a new version of the 
language has proven itsself to be stable - say, COBOL85 in, oh, about 
1993, maybe 1995 - that it might be implemented in an organisation in a 
fashion which might preclude situations such as:

from <http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=7cucq6%24san%241%40clarknet.clark.net&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

'What is *this* stuff?  EVALUATE TRUE WHEN cond-1 imperative statement...
you call this COBOL?!?'

'Oh, please, Mr Standards-and-Practises Reviewmeister, it is exactly what
is allowed by the ANSI '85 Standard.'

'ANSI '85?  Crap, I *knew* things were goin' ta hell in a handbasket when
we allowed them fancy ANSI '74 constructs in a couple a' years back...
look, 1985 is only 14 years ago, we oughta wait until the technology is
Really Proven before we implement it.  Go back and rewrite this in *real*
COBOL, then try again.'

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T11:17:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3dajhjhdeuec1@corp.supernews.com>`

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T04:59:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E360E81.4CE9B5A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> As a follow-up to the various SORT threads, I am curious (and I think this
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

We have a weekly batch reporting job that reads in a multi-volume tape
file, typically 150 million records.  The file is sorted by several
fields with customer number being the high-order portion of the key. 
A few records required special handling where the original customer
number was replaced by a different customer number, depending on
certain business rules.

Originally, we used SYNCSORT to sort the data and split it into two
files.  A separate COBOL program modified the customer number for the
special cases (which required a separate file lookup) and both files
were merged together (again using SYNCSORT).  

Since the unmodified records were usually 99% of the file, and that
file was usually more than 15 tape volumes, we were able to process
the big file only once by using an E15 SORT exit to modify the keys
and sort all the records in one pass. 
```

#### ↳ Re: SORT - E15/E35 Exits

- **From:** "Don Higgins" <don.higgins@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T06:32:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b15pmc$8gv$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
E35/E15 assembler exits are also useful to compare field formats not
currently supported by Cobol such as 128 bit extended hex and binary
floating point.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b13hua$svc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...
> As a follow-up to the various SORT threads, I am curious (and I think this
> will ONLY make sense to IBM mainframe people),
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (As far as I know, the answer does NOT depend on DFSort vs SyncSort, but
if
> it does, I would be interested in that too.)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Index files — 5 msgs

### Index files

- **From:** tromba99@netzero.net (tromba99)
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T08:23:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89dbd7af.0301270823.51b05eb7@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone tell me how I can see the actual contents of the index of a
data file in some sort of meaningful format?  I have indexed data
files created with COBOL on a unix system.  They have two parts, the
main data file(i.e. PRDMST) and the index (i.e. PRDMST.idx).  I have
come across an issue where every so often the index for a file(i.e.
PRDMST.idx) that is usually about 1 mb suddenly balloons to 900 mb.  I
have a rebuilding process that corrects the problem, but I can't
figure out why it keeps happening.  I would like to see what the index
actually looks like to find out what is in it when it grows so large. 
I have utility programs with which I can export the data in the file,
but not the index itself.  Any help would be appreciated.
```

```

#### ↳ Re: Index files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T15:49:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7NicndjMUsgONKijXTWckA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<89dbd7af.0301270823.51b05eb7@posting.google.com>`

```

"tromba99" <tromba99@netzero.net> wrote in message
news:89dbd7af.0301270823.51b05eb7@posting.google.com...
> Can anyone tell me how I can see the actual contents of the index of a
> data file in some sort of meaningful format?  I have indexed data
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> but not the index itself.  Any help would be appreciated.

While you might be able to read the DATA portion in a meaningful way, the
INDEX portion is usually proprietary and contains pointers, pointers to
indexes, pointers to data tracks, pointers to overflow areas, pointers to
available records, and badgers.

I don't hold out much hope for deciphering the index portion of the ISAM
file.

Could you contact the compiler vendor with the symptom?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index files

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T01:47:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E35E144.6010502@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<89dbd7af.0301270823.51b05eb7@posting.google.com> <7NicndjMUsgONKijXTWckA@giganews.com>`

```
My bet would be that the index file marks the index 
references of changed or deleted records, and adds new 
pointers for the new location of the changed or added 
record.  This probably causes the increase in size - check 
update and add activity.  The rebuild process probably 
removes the now unused pointers, and reorganizes the file 
back into the correct indexed state.  At the same time it 
probably actually deletes the records from the file and 
possibly reorganizes (sorts) it back into the index order to 
increase access time.  Just a guess as I don't know what 
kind of files these are - ISAM, VSAM, etc.

JerryMouse wrote:
> "tromba99" <tromba99@netzero.net> wrote in message
> news:89dbd7af.0301270823.51b05eb7@posting.google.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

I am fairly new to COBOL and am wondering if anyone knows any good
reference sites.  I haven't had much luck finding any good online
language reference.

The problem I am having is:

I have defined an integer as such:
01 COUNTER PIC 9(3).

Now I move a 1 into the integer as such:
MOV 1 INTO COUNTER

What do I have to do if I want to DISPLAY that integer as a string
like this:
1

Not like this:
001

Thanks in advance for any help.
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP!

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-27T17:51:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b14cmf$9n$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<69ca2de9.0301270900.6d8a5c55@posting.google.com>`

```
Use reference modification -

03  WS-SUB PIC 9(04) BINARY.

EVALUATE COUNTER
        WHEN < 10
                MOVE 3 TO WS-SUB
        WHEN < 100
                MOVE 2 TO WS-SUB
        WHEN OTHER
                MOVE 1 TO WS-SUB
        END-EVALUATE.

DISPLAY COUNTER (WS-SUB:)

You don't need to specify the length as the compiler will calculate that for
you.

However, this smells like homework....

news:69ca2de9.0301270900.6d8a5c55@posting.google.com...

 EVALUATE COUNTER
         WHEN < 10

         END-EVALUATE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP!

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T03:05:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0301280305.5b94f7a1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<69ca2de9.0301270900.6d8a5c55@posting.google.com> <b14cmf$9n$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <xRkZ9.127$D07.72809672@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"WiNoJoE" <WiNoJoE@the.bar> wrote in message news:<xRkZ9.127$D07.72809672@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>...
> I'm not sure how reference modification works, but did you mean to code this?
> 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> > However, this smells like homework....

<Rest Snipped>

Yes, he was looking to display a left-justified value of his 3-byte
Display-Numeric field named COUNTER. Moving COUNTER to a receiving
field defined as ZZ9 would have worked as well, but that would have
been right-justified for values less than 100. His example
specifically illustrated his need for a left-justifield solution.

Using the DISPLAY verb with reference modification basically
translates to -

"I want to begin my data-display at the byte/position defined to
WS-SUB. The compiler will determine the length of my display so I
don't need to specify this length".

HTH....

Bill
```

through keyboard using ACCEPT in a program. Generally,we know that A
Numeric field will store data with RIGHT JUSTIFICATION, but in my case
this numeric field stores the data with LEFT JUSTIFICATION.
For Example let us assume 
       05 empno pic 9(5). is a Numeric Variable.

But in my case that value is storing like 20000 in emp.

## Thread: Serial Number Request - NOT MINE — 2 this month / 13 total

### Serial Number Request - NOT MINE

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <fjohnso5@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T13:49:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E368A70.8080904@rochester.rr.com>`

```
I received the following in today's e-mail.  I have omitted the e-mail 
because I do not want him receiving a serial number - but it did make me 
  mad.  Ironically, if I understand Fujisu's serial number scheme, the 
number is eventually tied to a single machine - it is not a general 
serial number that can be used on multiple machines.

I do not want to reply to his e-mail, so my response will be given here 
- "NO, I will not send it." [:}]

Thanks for listening, to my frustrations.

Floyd Johnson

==============================================================

Hi,



my name's Fabrizio. I'm looking for a serial number for Fujitsu NetCobol
with www.google.com and in the Groups, I found a message with your
e-mail.

If you have a serial, could you please send me?



Thanks in advance.

Fabrizio

=================================================================
```

#### ↳ Re: Serial Number Request - NOT MINE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T11:27:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b16eig$t1c$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E368A70.8080904@rochester.rr.com>`

```
Floyd (and others),
  I certainly agree that one should NOT give out their purchased product's
serial number.  There is a *slight* possibility, however, that this question
relates to a problem with a very early version of the software that came out
with the book "COBOL for Dummies" - where the disk required a serial
number - but didn't tell the purchaser what it was.  If so, anyone still
having this problem should see the COBOL FAQ section at:

 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm#sect105
```

<a id="t-3830b694ec"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe HLL Migration — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** "joseluis" <jos@generali.es>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T17:36:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software
- **Message-ID:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`

```
 Hi all,

 This year we're planning to migrate our high level languages. Now we're
running

        on a OS/390 V 2.10,
        LE runtime environment in Linklist,
        OS PL/I 2.3 and
        COBOL II V 1.3.2
        CICS Transaction Server V 1.3.0
        DB2 V 5.1.0

 Our online environment is all in AMODE 31 but in batch we're runnig mainly
in AMODE 24. This change is dificult
 for as because is a integrate and dinamyc environment and is very dificult
to establish trcability of programs calls and
 we've understand that we've to migrate in a particular order in order to
avoid AMODE31 call to AMODE24 rutines.

 we would like to migrate to OS/390 Enterprise PL/I and COBOL and some that
we discus are

    - Can we linkedite our actuals programs with LE (only linkedite) ? Is it
a good idea?
    - Is it necesary to migrate our batch environment to AMODE 31 (or ANY) ?
and if is not
      a mandatory, is it better?
    - Is there strong compatibilities within pl/I and COBOL verssions runnig
together ?
    - Someone has experienced a similar (ordered and global) migration?

  Any sugestions?

  Thank you very much

  Josep Lluis
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T14:15:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software
- **Message-ID:** `<3E36D6CA.11E4A63C@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`

```


joseluis wrote:
<snip>
>  Our online environment is all in AMODE 31 but in batch we're runnig mainly
> in AMODE 24. This change is dificult
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Any sugestions?
<snip>

This is more of a system level issue. 

In your COBOL programs that must be linked AMODE24, use a
CBL statement to force the issue. Do not use AMODE/RMODE
with the Linkage Editor (BINDER). This way, it will not
override the requirements of source that has to exist below
the line.

Now why would the code require AMODE 24? Would it be because
of subroutines that are called that are written to only
handle 24 bit addresses?

At anyrate, with regards to the COBOL and BATCH issues - you
are better off by having your programs not care if they are
RMODE 24/31, but to be RMODE ANY. This will allow them to
load above or below the line. Next is, if you are using VSAM
data sets, then not being fixed to below the line storage
(AMODE 24), allows you to make use of above the line
buffering (AMODE 31), which can improve your elapsed times
on your batch JOBS.

Other than the PL/1 question, I have just done such a
migration from VSE to z/OS 1.1 wherein some of the BATCH
programs required AMODE24/RMODE24. And this was specifically
because the COBOL code was poorly generated by an RPG to
COBOL translator. As a result, a special subroutine had to
be written that the COBOL program called (it contained ESTAE
to catch and fix S0C7 ABEND problems that RPG automatically
resolved in VSE).

More than 90% of the BATCH COBOL code did not care where it
ran, and as a result, the use of HYPERBUF (CA buffer control
product) allowed for better handling of HUGE VSAM datasets)
was enhanced as it did not have to move buffers below the
line (24 bit addressed storage).

I hope this helps you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** "joseluis" <WITHOUTTHISjos@generali.es>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T10:33:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software
- **Message-ID:** `<b1875f$haa$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`
- **References:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net> <3E36D6CA.11E4A63C@ix.netcom.com>`

```

Hi Steve and thanks for your help. I'll try to answer you each point :

    "In your COBOL programs that must be linked AMODE24, use a
    CBL statement to force the issue. Do not use AMODE/RMODE
    with the Linkage Editor (BINDER). This way, it will not
    override the requirements of source that has to exist below
    the line."

Until now, I didn't know that from a cobol source you could notice amode criteria. I knew 
that you could compile with parameter DATA 24 and link with AMODE ANY but I don't know
cobol statements that controls AMODE type. Anyway, my hope is to migrate all my code 
to AMODE ANY.

Reason by we've AMODE 24 code is HISTORY. We've old code, but valid yet, and we've 
critical subroutines (in PL/I mainly but some in COBOL) that are called from different applications.
This is a difficult in order to migrate to AMODE 31 or ANY because this subroutines may not 
understand parameters received from caller programs and is also very difficult, for repository 
low function, to establish the correct order to migrate because we can't determine the trace 
of calls.

    "At anyrate, with regards to the COBOL and BATCH issues - you
    are better off by having your programs not care if they are
    RMODE 24/31, but to be RMODE ANY. This will allow them to
    load above or below the line. Next is, if you are using VSAM
    data sets, then not being fixed to below the line storage
    (AMODE 24), allows you to make use of above the line
    buffering (AMODE 31), which can improve your elapsed times
    on your batch JOBS."
    "Other than the PL/1 question, I have just done such a
    migration from VSE to z/OS 1.1 wherein some of the BATCH
    programs required AMODE24/RMODE24. And this was specifically
    because the COBOL code was poorly generated by an RPG to
    COBOL translator. As a result, a special subroutine had to
    be written that the COBOL program called (it contained ESTAE
    to catch and fix S0C7 ABEND problems that RPG automatically
    resolved in VSE)."
    More than 90% of the BATCH COBOL code did not care where it
    ran, and as a result, the use of HYPERBUF (CA buffer control
    product) allowed for better handling of HUGE VSAM datasets)
    was enhanced as it did not have to move buffers below the
    line (24 bit addressed storage)"

This isn't relevant because our COBOL code is only for subrouitines and thay don't need file
access. We also use Hyperbuffer but VSAM access is only avalaible from PL/I and I think 
that won't be a problem run in AMODE 31 or ANY.
About RMODE I understand that is a ressidency criteria and will be always ANY but, in fact
will depend on AMODE because if a program is AMODE 24, Can you load it above the line?
How manage his "below" memory?

At end, I think (by now) that only way to make a correct migration is with steps :

    1 - Determine trace programs calls
    2 - Determine assembler subroutines with restricted AMODE source code, change them and
         compile and link with new AMODE/RMODE but be sure that these subroutines respects
         AMODE/RMODE caller's and AMODE/RMODE called, this is, use BAS and BASSM and
         catalog CSET with AMODE ANY, RMODE ANY.
    3 - Confirm that PL/I and COBOL source code are independent of AMODE criteria. I think it is.
    4 - If 3 is true, relink low level subroutines (in the trace calls) at same time and at the end of this
         batch and complete proces, change your linkage AMODE/RMODE criteria for this kind of 
         low level routines. Low level routines are these that are called by they but don't call anymore.
    5 - Same as 4 but with the intermediate routines level. They are whose that are called by main
         programs and they calls another routines.
    6 - Change AMODE/RMODE linkage criteria of our main programs. I understand isn't necessary 
         do a batch and complete migration, can be progressive at time main programs be compiled as
         a normal maintenance proces.

 I will have to determine if I can linkage with new language environment libraries because until this
 point I'm yet in HLL old verssions. 

 Also I've to find a way to detect my routines level and in our environment this is so dificult because 
 is highly dinamyc and ther is also a high integration level.

 This would get you a AMODE/RMODE independent (and migfrate to 31) scenario. Now, if there is not
 verssions restriccions, install and customiza new HLL verssions.

 Anyway, your answer are usefull and welcomed

 thank you very much

 Josep Lluis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T08:36:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software,comp.lang.asm370
- **Message-ID:** `<3E37D8D1.63E6D18E@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net> <3E36D6CA.11E4A63C@ix.netcom.com> <b1875f$haa$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`

```


> joseluis wrote:
<snip>
> At end, I think (by now) that only way to make a correct
> migration is with steps :
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>          a normal maintenance proces.
<snip>

Your points where there needs to be clarification:

2) The ALC subroutines need to be looked at for more than
BAS. BAS/R and BAL/R both work the same in 31 bit mode
(PSW:32 = 1). However, you are correct in your idea of using
BASSM to force yourself into 31 bit mode. By doing this,
your code can be RMODE ANY and AMODE 31. Therefore, once you
have fixed these, placing an RMODE ANY statement close to
the START or CSECT statement (which should begin the
subroutine) will tell BINDER what it needs to know during
link edit, and/or LOADer should the subroutine be
dynamically invoked.

3) As long as your PL/1 code and COBOL code are not compiled
with a below-the-line residency requirement, then the code
generated should handle any type of addressing (24 or 31
bit).

4/5) Again the subroutines must be passed parameter lists
that do not contain addresses (call by reference) where the
high-order byte is corrupted by flags. They must be passed
pure addresses. If this is true, then the low level routines
can easily be made RMODE ANY.

Also, any subroutine that is doing I/O, other than VSAM,
must be modified to GETMAIN the area for the DCB below the
line (24 bit storage). OPEN/GET/PUT/CLOSE can all exist
above the line, but the DCB itself must exist below the
line. 

VSAM I/O can reside anywhere. So subroutines that are only
doing VSAM can easily be fixed for RMODE ANY, AMODE 31.

For your ALC questions in this area, you should probably put
them on comp.lnag.asm370. There you will get very serious
answers and sometimes code examples. Accordingly, this post
has had comp.lang.asm370 added to it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-28T17:18:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software
- **Message-ID:** `<3E372BE2.D83A1EBC@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`

```
Jose Luis,
My suggestions are as follows:
1.  Locate all of your subroutines.  If you have Assembler Language subroutines,
either upgrade them 31-bit addressability, or convert them to COBOL, so that you
can use 31-bit addressing as a compile / program bind (link edit) option.  Many
things we used to need to do in Assembler can now be done in COBOL using POINTER
data items, etc.  If you have subroutines in other languages besides COBOL, then
compiling them with an LE-compatible compiler should simplify interlanguage
communication.  (My shop didn't have this issue.)  The COBOL subroutines should
be migrated from VS COBOL II to COBOL for OS/390 & VM or Enterprise COBOL, so
that you'll be able to make them 31-bit addressable when you want to or need to.

2.  Locate the subroutines which do not do any CALLs to other subroutines.
These are "lowest level" subroutines, and they can all unconditionally (I think)
be made 31-bit addressable, because anyone can call a 31-bit routine (but only
24-bit code can call 24-bit routines).  Once all of these subroutines have been
upgraded, go through the rest of the subroutines to locate the next lowest level
(the ones which call only the subs you've alreay upgraded).  Bring those up to
31-bit addressability.  When you are finished, you can compile and program bind
any main program as 31-bit, without fear that it will fail because of mixed
address modes.

3.  Start on your main programs, using the same strategy, and soon (maybe not so
soon) you'll be all 31-bit.  This will let you take advantage of some LE
performance enhancements.

You must go to the IBM COBOL Web Site and get the Migration Guide for COBOL and
the one for PL/1.  These manuals will explain how you can get to Language
Environment.  The use of the RESIDENT / NORESIDENT compiler option for VS COBOL
II is an issue in accomplishing the migration.  My shop had been using RES for
both OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II for over a decade, so we had few load modules
that needed re-binding (relinkediting).

If you are using VS COBOL II as your current COBOL run time, go ahead and test
replacing that run time with Language Environment, to see what problems you
have.  They may be so small that you can do a "big bang" move to LE; they were
for my shop.  I do not know how easy or hard PL/1 is, because we have
practically no PL/1.

There is more to consider, but time is up for me today.  Your best chance of
success is to get busy as soon as possible!

Buena Suerte!

joseluis wrote:

>  Hi all,
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Josep Lluis
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe HLL Migration

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T13:20:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,es.comp.ingenieria.software
- **Message-ID:** `<b199hn$np2$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b16bk2$5nm$1@nsnmrro2-gest.nuria.telefonica-data.net>`

```
I have read a number of replies to this note in both comp.lang.cobol and
IBM-MAIN.  Let me summarize *my* personal suggestions on the steps that you
should follow:

1) Read all 3 Migration Guides at the following locations:
 For COBOL:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3MG00/CCONTENT
S

For PL/I:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IBM3MG00/CCONTENT
S

For LE:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA6130/CCONTENT
S

2) Review the ILC (InterLanguage Communications) Manual at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA4110/CCONTENT
S

3)  Look into the Edge Portfolio Analyzer (or other products if you can find
them).  See:
 http://edge-information.com/epa-00.htm

4) Make certain that you verify that all your "development tools"
(debuggers, dump analyzers, etc) are at the appropriate levels to support
current levels of LE and an compilers.  Then make certain that the same is
true for any "outside" provided applications run in your shop.

 ... After this preparatory work - I have a few suggestions:

A) *Never* override the link-edit attributes for COBOL programs.   If you
set the compiler options correctly (for what you want), then "take" the
linkage editor (or binder) options that are produced.

B) Move to an environment where LE is the *only* run-time library in use
(Linklist, Steplib, etc)

C) *After* you have killed off all older run-time libraries, then make
certain that you move to LE as the only link-edit library in use.

D) *AFTER* you have moved to LE as the only run-time and link-edit library,
then use RACF or similar facility to insure that only the "newer" compilers
are used for maintenance and development.
```

## Thread: Micro Focus CALL (or CHAIN) strange limitation — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus CALL (or CHAIN) strange limitation

- **From:** z.atlas@verizon.net (Z. Atlas)
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T02:15:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e3737bf.51250714@news.bellatlantic.net>`

```
Hi
Is there any known limitation in MicroFocus COBOL so that I may not
CALL a program when this program name exceed 6 characters. Under what
circumstaces do we have this limitation and how could I circumvent it?
I use NetExpress 3.0, but I do not think this is the problem. In past
projects I used 8 characters program-name on regular basis. I believe
I have special circumstances in the current project but I do not know
what are they.
In the current project I use either PRO*COBOL Oracle precompiler or
regular COBOL with ODBC to access the Oracle database. In BOTH cases
COBOL enters the CALLed program somewhere in the middle unless I
truncate the name to 6 characters. I've noticed that the PRO*COBOL
precompiler generated CALLs almost alllways use 6 character
program-names such ad SQLADR or SQLBXE. However, somehow I do not
think that it has anything to do with the Oracle environment (and
again, if it does, how do I circumvent the problem?)
Thanks
ZA
```

softwaresimple.com.

#### ↳ Re: accessing comports

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T14:23:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ntRZ9.218$dL4.199961@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<dae38c37.0301290354.241daceb@posting.google.com>`

```
"Mike Allison" <fbs-ltd@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message
news:dae38c37.0301290354.241daceb@posting.google.com...
> Where can I get hold of cob32api.dll and a manual for it. I am using
> Fujitsu cobol v6 (windows) and I want to read and write to com 1/2/3.
>
> I need to talk to a customer display / mag reader and epos printer.

Serial communications is not my specialty, but a quick trip through the
MS-Windows Platform SDK indicates that the functions necessary to
open/read/write communications resources are included in the standard API.

This includes such mutlti-purpose functions as CreateFile, ReadFile and
WriteFile; and a series of specialty functons including SetupComm,
WaitCommEvent and TransmitCommChar.

A complete article on serial communications is in the "Techincal Articles"
section of the PSDK; the article is titled
"Serial Communications in Win32" by Allen Denver, Microsoft Windows
Developer Support, December 11, 1995.

I do not have an on-line URL direct to this (my PSDK is on CD), but you can
go to the Microsoft Developer's start page at
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/default.asp  and do a search for "serial
communications" to find all kinds of info.

I also never heard of cobapi32.dll. Neither, it seems, has Microsoft's
'search' function.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: accessing comports

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T21:49:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0301292149.75e01f9e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dae38c37.0301290354.241daceb@posting.google.com> <ntRZ9.218$dL4.199961@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
My implementation was based on the article Michael mentions below. 
The hardest part was figuring out the DCB structure which is described
in fractional bytes (bits).  Once I got that it was pretty easy, and
Fujitsu offers the new (in the 2002 standard) Boolean data types PIC 1
- which made it easy (well easier) to implement.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"Mike Allison" <fbs-ltd@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message
news:dae38c37.0301290354.241daceb@posting.google.com...
> Where can I get hold of cob32api.dll and a manual for it. I am using
> Fujitsu cobol v6 (windows) and I want to read and write to com 1/2/3.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Many thanks in advance.

cob32api.dll is a Micro Focus DLL, not Fujitsu.  It simply maps windows api
calls to cobol.  There's a demo on the Micro Focus support site on how to
deal with com ports.  Try www.microfocus.com and choose support then
samples.

Calin, TORONTO
```

<a id="t-6b63e1bf2f"></a>
## Thread: Trying to recreate problem of multi-user lockout — 6 msgs

### Trying to recreate problem of multi-user lockout

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T05:00:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6e1fab7.0301290500.28d19529@posting.google.com>`

```
With sincerest thanks to JerryMouse, I am now convinced that 
Google is the holy grail of information mining on Usenet. 
If anyone knows of an even better way to research these 
archives, please break the news to me slowly, as I am not sure 
that my heart would stand the shock.  

Our system has been plagued with multi-user lockouts and index 
file corruptions for years.  Recently as a result of 
researching these problems I was quite delighted to discover 
what should be a solution to our problem, namely, to disable 
new file sharing and locking semantics in each workstation.  
We are currently using MF COBOL 3.4 (16-bit) and we will soon 
be moving up to Net Express (32-bit).  It is my understanding 
from what I have read in the archives that the same problem & 
solution apply to both products.

What I am trying to find out now is how to specifically 
recreate the problem.  The incidence of this problem for 
us has always been sporadic, making it too difficult for 
us to pinpoint an actual chain of events to recreate it.  
Does anyone have any suggestions on how I could set up a 
scenario between two users sharing one isam file to 
dependably cause a lockout and/or index corruption?  If 
we could do this then we could prove (and subsequently 
convince our system support folks) how turning off 
opportunistic locking will resolve our problem.  

I deeply appreciate any suggestions.  

Thanks,
DaveM
```

```
Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3E37FCFA.1030803@Sympatico.ca>...
> Renfrew wrote:
> > Our system has been plagued with multi-user lockouts and index 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

> I'm sorry that I did not notice this earlier, as we had
> that problem and that solution for years now.  We use Fujitsu
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Could I ask you to describe more specifically what your lock test 
program does?  Am not asking you to write the program for me,
of course, but may I presume that you are doing something like
read-with-lock followed by an unanswered accept?  If so, then 
this is considerably simpler than what I had mentally pictured, 
which was something like iteratively
read-with-lock/pause-a-moment/chg-key-value/rewrite,   or,  
iteratively read-with-lock/pause-a-moment/delete/write.  I will go
ahead with the simple read-with-lock presumption for now.

could you maybe send me a code snippet from your procedure div?

DaveM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trying to recreate problem of multi-user lockout

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T15:43:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E386713.90505@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0301290500.28d19529@posting.google.com> <3E37FCFA.1030803@Sympatico.ca> <d6e1fab7.0301291203.1d49180c@posting.google.com>`

```
I simply opened a file, wrote out a hundred records, then set it up in a 
loop ... open,lock, add one to a counter in the record and re-write 
without releasing, delay 2 seconds, add one to second counter in the 
record, rewrite close, unlock. Ran that on four terminals, 
simultaneously, overnight. If the two counters (in each record) are 
equal at the end, then you are ok.  Of course, that method requires that 
you manually lock the record, rather than use locking is automatic. If 
you insert a re-read and re-lock after the first re-write and before the 
delay though, it should work with automatic locking as well.

If I remember correctly (it was a couple of years back), I tested it 
BEFORE applying the fix, to ensure that it would fail if record locking 
was NOT working, then ran it again with the fix on the second night. I 
was not really sure that record locking was the problem. That was back 
in win95, and I got enough failures on the first run (none on the 
second) to make me confident I had nailed it.  You might want to play 
with the delay a bit, but I just sat in a loop looking at current time. 
The granularity of the two second delay plus the small file should give 
you enough conflicts for it to work.  The keys I used were just the loop 
counter.

What I would do is use the select statements and read/write statements 
out of the original code, and use the first test to ensure that file 
locking is indeed the problem.  You can test it with file locking turned 
off, to make sure the code works. I simply looked at the file with my 
editor to see the problems ... wrote the code quick and dirty.

Donald

Renfrew wrote:
> Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3E37FCFA.1030803@Sympatico.ca>...
> 
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> DaveM
```

<a id="t-d015892f96"></a>
## Thread: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390 — 11 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** "max Boettner" <max.boettner@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T02:48:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
content?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T22:00:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E389557.F0DBE1BD@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
max Boettner wrote:
> 
> How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
> content?

The simple answer is, you can't. 

JCL is designed to pass information to the JOB steps. Unlike
VSE where you could use the "Communications Region" to pass
info to the next or subsequent executing program(s), this is
not available natively in "MVS". 

But let us suppose that you really need to do this, and
information that is currently part of the now executing JOB
is not needed. What you can do now is, write to a DD coded
as follows:

//SUBMIT  DD  SYSOUT=(A,INTRDR)

You will need a SELECT/ASSIGN for the DDNAME of SUBMIT and
an FD that will be writing RECFM=F/FB LRECL=80 records.

What will happen here is (assuming you aren't barred from
this), you will now submit a whole new JOB that will inherit
the current JOB's security profile (assuming you don't have
a SUBMIT exit in your shop that does other things). This way
you can pass all the parameters in JCL that you want by
having your COBOL program "generate" the whole JOB stream
(or however much of it you want while "hard-coding" the
rest).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** B Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T14:33:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E393804.AB68ED25@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com> <3E389557.F0DBE1BD@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:

> ...is not needed. What you can do now is, write to a DD coded
> as follows:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Steve - I've found that most shops no longer allow this, as the submitted job
is not under the control of CA-7 or UCC-1 or whatever job scheduling system
they use.  I did a lot of this in the 70's, but haven't been allowed to do
this in umpteen years.

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **Date:** 2003-01-29T22:54:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1a7mf$8cp0$1@news3.infoave.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Could you be more explicit in what you are trying to accomplish?

max Boettner wrote:
> How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
> content?
> 
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-29T21:57:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1a7qh$pnr$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
> How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
> content?

Are you sure you don't mean the other way around - pass a variable in JCL
to a program? Nevertheless, if you mean it the way you have written, this
is not possible. This is not a cobol restriction, it is not possible from
any language. You can set a return code and execute (or not) JCL based on
it. A program can create new JCL to be executed as a separate job. You can
pass data through a file. That's about it. What is it you are wanting to
do?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T07:21:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ao4_9.22451$zF6.1613957@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

max Boettner <max.boettner@t-online.de> wrote in message news:b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com...
> How can a cobol program pass a variable to JCL, so that JCL can use the
> content?

You want COBOL program A in Step1 of Job JCL1
to pass a variable to Step2 of Job JCL1?

Method #1
Have COBOL program A in Step1 of Job JCL1
create a sequential dataset DATASET.STEP1OUT.

Have Step2 of Job JCL1 read in DATASET.STEP1OUT.

Method #2
Have COBOL program A in Step1 of Job JCL1
create a Parmlib member PARMLIB(STEP1OUT).

Have Step2 of Job JCL1 read in PARMLIB(STEP1OUT).

Method #3
Have COBOL program A in Step1 of Job JCL1
update a Proclib member PROCLIB(STEP2IN).

Have Step2 of Job JCL1 execute PROCLIB(STEP2IN).

Normally, I would try these theories before posting,
to resolve any technical issues, but I cannot do that right now,
so you'll have to work out the bugs yourself.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T07:51:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0301300751.7d403fc2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b1a0d1$gg6$03$1@news.t-online.com> <ao4_9.22451$zF6.1613957@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:
> Method #3
> Have COBOL program A in Step1 of Job JCL1
> update a Proclib member PROCLIB(STEP2IN).
> 
> Have Step2 of Job JCL1 execute PROCLIB(STEP2IN).

All the procs of Job JCL1 will be expanded before the first step
executes.

Other than the return code there really is no way for JCL to be aware
of any prior condition.  Sad fact of life in the MVS|z/OS|OS/390
world.  As shells go, JCL is badly lacking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL, JCL AND OS/390

<a id="t-3afd613fee"></a>
## Thread: COMP conversion to decimal using MicroFocus Netexpress COBOL — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COMP conversion to decimal using MicroFocus Netexpress COBOL

- **From:** "David Thomas" <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T17:25:25
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1bn11$glb$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
Got a peculiar one here.

I'm extracting some salary data from a payroll system running on VMS with
MicroFocus COBOL.

We are migrating the system to a Windows/Intel platform and have to extract
the data from the system on the VMS system to the new server.

Running a simple COBOL program to extract basic ID detail and salary from
our VMS version of the payroll system, I can extract the data perfectly.

In the FD section, the salary in the master table is defined as 'S9(7)V99
COMP', and when output it's defined as 'ZZZZZZZZ9.99'. On VMS it works
fine - all amounts returned normally.

If I run the same COBOL code against the new Windows version, the salary is
not converted properly - all amounts are returned as '538976288'.

Not just the salary but any amount using 'S9(7)V99 COMP'.

'S9(4) COMP' amounts are fine - would this indicate a problem with 'S9(7)V99
COMP' which (I think) is 4 bytes as opposed to 2 bytes for 'S9(4) COMP'?



Any information for a humble COBOL dummy, would be gratefully received.



Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP conversion to decimal using MicroFocus Netexpress COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T13:25:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3iqrsruipc3a6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b1bn11$glb$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
The value '538976288' is equal to x"20202020".
The field contains ASCII spaces!


David Thomas <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk> wrote in message
news:b1bn11$glb$1@news.swman.net.uk...
> Got a peculiar one here.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> We are migrating the system to a Windows/Intel platform and have to
extract
> the data from the system on the VMS system to the new server.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> If I run the same COBOL code against the new Windows version, the salary
is
> not converted properly - all amounts are returned as '538976288'.
>
> Not just the salary but any amount using 'S9(7)V99 COMP'.
>
> 'S9(4) COMP' amounts are fine - would this indicate a problem with
'S9(7)V99
> COMP' which (I think) is 4 bytes as opposed to 2 bytes for 'S9(4) COMP'?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP conversion to decimal using MicroFocus Netexpress COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T13:24:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1bu5k$fjq$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b1bn11$glb$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
Check your setting of the Micro Focus "byte-mode" directive.  It may be that
VMS supports only half-word/whole-word binary fields - but you have set your
Micro Focus environment to support 1, 2, 3, & 4 byte binaries.  This may
cause your "entire" FD to map incorrectly.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP conversion to decimal using MicroFocus Netexpress COBOL

- **From:** James Johnson <saildot.maryland@verizon.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T01:26:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4bnj3vcf7vvolgr81sde3ojfabpd482jhr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b1bn11$glb$1@news.swman.net.uk>`

```
We have had a problem where if it is defined as S9(5)V2 and the input file has
special characters in that field!@#$%^&*(), instead of error with a type
missmatch it takes the last digit of the octal ascii value for that character
and keeps on happily processing. This is just the latest bug involving numeric
handling we have found on this conversion project.  This is on a Sun Solaris
system.  My impression is MF needs to do some more QC on its compilers.  Chasing
compiler bugs is getting very old.

JJ

On Thu, 30 Jan 2003 17:25:25 -0000, "David Thomas" <d.m.thomas@swan.ac.uk>
wrote:

>Got a peculiar one here.
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

James Johnson
remove the dot from after sail in email address to reply
```
