<a id="t-ca9964f89f"></a>
## Thread: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure? — 9 this month / 13 total

### Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T19:30:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca>`

```
Last month itbusiness.ca had an article about top management losing
confidence in their IT department.  This month there is a survey that
shows IT having little faith in top management.  And somewhere I read
that IT should be focusing on the bottom line and adding business
value.  In short many organizations remain dysfunctional.

My belief is that many companies get the IT that they manage for.  In
most organizations, if the IT department isn't functioning well, I
suspect that the relations among the rest of the department are not
good as well.  If an organization, doesn't believe in documentation and
providing help to its employees to do a job, the applications will
probably reflect that.  While I am basically interested in the technical
side and have little interest in setting the overall goals of an
organization, I want to use my technical skills to meet the needs of
people who are tasked with meeting those goals.  The following is what I
believe is needed for an organization to be successful.

The job of top management is to set the goals, define the culture, and
both live and communicate those things.  There must be the willingness
to hear and deal with bad news.  The story was told a couple of years
ago in the Wall Street Journal that Sam Walton of Walmart used to start
the day asking what went wrong so that the top management could work on
improving while the then head of K-mart didn't want to hear any bad news
believing the lower levels should correct the problem without his help. 
Note today which organization is in bankruptcy.  Note which organization
is noted for having effective IT.  Equally important is making certain
that common terminology is used.  There has to be agreement on the
definition of terms such as a sale, a customer, or a product.  I know of
one organization that is spending a good deal of money to accomplish
this.  There must be teamwork in both setting and achieving the goals. 
As an applications programmer/analyst, I did not know the intimate
details of the business.  I had to depend on those who were actually in
the area of concern such as accounts receivable to make sure that what I
did met the business need.  At higher levels, it is not up to IT to
determine whether it is more important to get inventory turnaround
higher than it is to improve the order entry process.  It is up to IT to
provide insight as to the tools and costs of the tools to achieve the
goals.  IT should also provide the vision as to how multiple goals can
be combined and the roles that other people will play in them.

A second thing that must be a top management decision is that
documentation is important and requires people who have writing skills.
Documentation is needed so that an organization knows what it is doing.
This includes help systems and anything else needed so that an
organization isn't dependent on the memory of old Joe.  It will be
expensive to do it if an organization hasn't been doing so.  The
benefits are that processes that are documented probably help qualify an
organization for ISO 9000 certification and there will be fewer
surprises in any process upgrade be it a computer system, a 
manufacturing process or the set of hiring guidelines.

The IT organization should truly believe in teamwork and review.  Like
many people I can spot everyone's mistakes but mine.  I have been
fortunate enough to work in places where people willingly share their
knowledge.  What seems to be rare is formal review and post-mortems on
projects.  If we don't keep track of both what went right and what went
wrong as well as the time taken, our estimates will continue to be bad
and we will both repeat mistakes and forget the good practices.  This is
not an exercise in shooting the wounded, it is a way to reduce the
wounds in future battles.

IT also has to realize that it is basically in the communication and
publishing business.  An order entry system exists to let people in the
organization know that a customer is buying goods or services to be sent
to a given place and paid for by some method.  It probably has a
component that keeps track of what goods an organization has and where
they are.  It will only function well when terms such as customer are
understood in the same way by all concerned.  Changes to that or any
other system may have side effects so testing and specification is a
team effort involving both those who understand the computer side and
those who know how the business should work.  

While computer systems, even Windows, aren't as bad as some detractors
portray them, reliability isn't what it could be.  Much of this is at
the level of the quirks that we put up with in our cars or our dwelling
places but some of the problems are more serious.  Greater emphasis must
be placed on testing and verification.  This means that an ongoing
cooperative effort must be made to have current validation suites.  It
is extremely easy to make a change that in itself works but randomly
breaks something else.  Here again, it is not just IT but the various
groups within an organization that must cooperate to achieve the goal of
systems that function well.
  
In short, if IT is to be successful, the implementation and modification
of systems must be a team effort where varying skills are recognized and
used.  Programmers should not be expected to be writers.  People with
political skills can help those who are not good at negotiation.  The
various units must be willing to do their part in specification, review,
and validation.  Mistakes must be acknowledged and recorded so that they
won't be repeated.  Both successes and failures must be acknowledged and
analyzed so that the knowledge from both can be used to implement future
projects better.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-30T20:36:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E39D317.3627AA49@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
<snip>
> While computer systems, even Windows, aren't as bad as some detractors
> portray them, reliability isn't what it could be.  Much of this is at
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> projects better.
<snip>

Show me one "legacy" O/S that is as bad as Windows and I'll
let you slide.

Mainframe O/S systems have to be regression tested, then
certification tested, before they are allowed out the door
(at least on the mainframes I've worked on). As a result,
the SECURITY and RAS go in FIRST, not as an after thought.

Now more to the point. What I just said is what most
managers miss. They want results now, and they don't want to
have to pay for the results. This is why so many are moving
anything they can off-shore to cheaper labor. Ultimately,
what will be the cost?

The argument that programmers just weren't productive enough
and that we needed 4GL and then 5GL to solve the problem
seems to be erroneous. We take things that are developed
cheaply and rapidly and then wonder why we have "memory
leaks" (when I was a pup, we called those poor programming
techniques). But, hey, its no big deal, just reboot the
computer and it will automatically fix itself (3000 people
down simultaneously for 45 minutes is a BIG DEAL vs 3000
people down asynchronously multiple times for 10 minutes...
Why is it the large machines with lots of users are always
held to a higher standard?).

In the shops that I consult to, I'm finding the older,
seasoned workers being displaced. What happens then is what
you described -- people in charge of this or that system and
not understanding its place relative to the rest of the
organization because that knowledge was not passed on.
Therefore, people wonder why certain things get missed --
performance things that could have been done but aren't,
etc.

Then I see managers (read that CEOs, CFOs, CIOs, CTOs, etc.)
who seem to know everything (they all read the same
magazines and go for the same degrees at the same type
schools), but understand nothing. Result, they force
migrations to new and "better" systems (which haven't been
proven). When the response times are worse, when there are
problems with systems that must talk to each other not being
in sync, etc., the problem must be with the IT people, or
those contractors they brought in (now let's throw more
money at the problem in the form of hardware).

Meanwhile, everyone can see that the emperor has no clothes
and so they start looking to work elsewhere before they get
laid off.

And you wonder why IT workers seem to have low moral? When
they do know something, they are told to keep their opinions
to themselves and make the new system work.

Perhaps if we were to deal with the Greed and Ego quotients
first, the whole problem would get solved?

(Don't ask me how many of these fiascoes I've seen. I'm
under too many non-disclosures to tell.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T02:21:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca>`

```

Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message news:3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca...
> Last month itbusiness.ca had an article about top management losing
> confidence in their IT department.  This month there is a survey that
> shows IT having little faith in top management.  And somewhere I read
> that IT should be focusing on the bottom line and adding business
> value.  In short many organizations remain dysfunctional.

I haven't read either article, so my responses are directed solely
to what was stated in your post.
>
> My belief is that many companies get the IT that they manage for.

I disagree.  They get the IT department that is the sum of the ability
and passion of the department's personnel.  If the majority are in IT
solely for the money, and lack the passion to do a good job and to
make sure that the systems anticipate all (or most) of the potential problems,
then more management or better management or closer management
isn't going to change the inbred characteristics of the individuals.

A company gets a great IT department when the people care.
You cannot make me care.  You cannot manage me into caring.
I care because I want to, and you cannot stop me.  Even if you manage me
unfairly, I will still care about the quality of my work.

> In most organizations, if the IT department isn't functioning well, I
> suspect that the relations among the rest of the department are not
> good as well.

I don't know what you mean/meant to say here.

> If an organization, doesn't believe in documentation and
> providing help to its employees to do a job, the applications will
> probably reflect that.

I document to satisfy my own standards, which are as high or higher
than anything that you can come up with.  I get hired because I am capable.
My output is of a consistent high standard, regardless of the conditions you stated.

>While I am basically interested in the technical
> side and have little interest in setting the overall goals of an
> organization, I want to use my technical skills to meet the needs of
> people who are tasked with meeting those goals.

You think that corporate policy makers care much about what the lower echelons think?

> The following is what I
> believe is needed for an organization to be successful.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> is noted for having effective IT.

In other words, common sense.

> Equally important is making certain
> that common terminology is used.  There has to be agreement on the
> definition of terms such as a sale, a customer, or a product.  I know of
> one organization that is spending a good deal of money to accomplish
> this.

In other words, communication, and more common sense.

> There must be teamwork in both setting and achieving the goals.
> As an applications programmer/analyst, I did not know the intimate
> details of the business.

I asked a manager once
"If you were to offer me one piece of invaluable advice, what would it be?"

His forceful reply, without hesitation, was "LEARN THE BUSINESS!!".
Nothing more needed to be said, by either of us.  I have always been grateful
to that gentleman, and I have never forgotten his advice.

> I had to depend on those who were actually in
> the area of concern such as accounts receivable to make sure that what I
> did met the business need.

I march to a different drummer here.  I do not place such trust
until it has been earned.  I have no hesitation in telling operating department
personnel to revisit their interpretation of a business need if I think it necessary.

> At higher levels, it is not up to IT to
> determine whether it is more important to get inventory turnaround
> higher than it is to improve the order entry process.

Yes, it is, if IT has insight not available to the external entities.

>It is up to IT to
> provide insight as to the tools and costs of the tools to achieve the
> goals.  IT should also provide the vision as to how multiple goals can
> be combined and the roles that other people will play in them.

Basically true.  IT can produce tentative schedules of what IT can accomplish
in a given timeframe and at a given cost, and let the business dictate priority,
unless there is an existing critical condition that basically takes a decision
out of hand and forces an immediate reaction.

> A second thing that must be a top management decision is that
> documentation is important and requires people who have writing skills.

Breathing requires lungs, and air.

> Documentation is needed so that an organization knows what it is doing.

Documentation does not ensure that.

> This includes help systems and anything else needed so that an
> organization isn't dependent on the memory of old Joe.

You can document until you are blue, but an organization will run better
if the current personnel have a valuable accumulation of IP.

The current downsizing trend is nothing more than a cost shifting exercise.
Companies will eventually pay dearly for shoving so much IP out the door.

>It will be expensive to do it if an organization hasn't been doing so.  The
> benefits are that processes that are documented probably help qualify an
> organization for ISO 9000 certification and there will be fewer
> surprises in any process upgrade be it a computer system, a
> manufacturing process or the set of hiring guidelines.

Again, the one doesn't follow the other.  There is no guarantee that the
documentation will reflect all of the nuances and IP inherent in the system.
>
> The IT organization should truly believe in teamwork and review.  Like
> many people I can spot everyone's mistakes but mine.

Maybe you don't look for your own, or don't look hard enough.

> I have been
> fortunate enough to work in places where people willingly share their
> knowledge.  What seems to be rare is formal review and post-mortems on
> projects.

I've found them to be second nature.  Our paths must not have crossed.

>If we don't keep track of both what went right and what went
> wrong as well as the time taken, our estimates will continue to be bad
> and we will both repeat mistakes and forget the good practices.  This is
> not an exercise in shooting the wounded, it is a way to reduce the
> wounds in future battles.

People need to be informed that honest mistakes will be tolerated within reason,
but that coverups and bad judgement will not be.

> IT also has to realize that it is basically in the communication and
> publishing business.

Slavery comes to mind, also.  Or didn't you spend the turn of the century
bleary-eyed in front of a monitor waiting for the grim Y2K reaper to show.
Makes you almost glad you won't be around to work on the Y21C problem.

>An order entry system exists to let people in the
> organization know that a customer is buying goods or services to be sent
> to a given place and paid for by some method.  It probably has a
> component that keeps track of what goods an organization has and where
> they are.

I call that the Inventory Management System.

>It will only function well when terms such as customer are
> understood in the same way by all concerned.  Changes to that or any
> other system may have side effects so testing and specification is a
> team effort involving both those who understand the computer side and
> those who know how the business should work.

I could tell you a horror story about an otherwise great IT department
that set up multiple testing environments, and used them for years
until a rather smart individual pointed out a glaring deficiency,
which was of course then immediately apparent to all,
most of whom proceeded to take the credit for discovering the problem.

> While computer systems, even Windows, aren't as bad as some detractors
> portray them, reliability isn't what it could be.  Much of this is at
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> used.  Programmers should not be expected to be writers.

Say what?

> People with
> political skills can help those who are not good at negotiation.  The
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.

Your heart is in the right place.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca> <b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca...
> >
> > My belief is that many companies get the IT that they manage for.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T14:37:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hSv_9.574$HG6.204015@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca> <b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <NLu_9.570$HG6.195977@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:NLu_9.570$HG6.195977@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
> news:b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Personnel decisions are a primary function of management; every manager
gets
> the staff he hires.

And furthermore... the balance between "quality" and "timeliness" and
"expense" is not the employee's decision.. this is also a managerial
function.  It is perfectly legitimate for manager to accept a quality
compromise (reduced testing, having non-writers write documentation, etc)
for economic reasons. I'm not saying I'd necessarily agree with those
decisions, but I will defend forever the absolute right of the manager to
make them - and be held accountable for them. Been there, done that, got the
T-shirt to prove it.

( With more than twenty years in management, I also accept employees
thinking the boss an imbecile and griping about him/her as an immutable fact
of nature. As they say, "it comes with the territory.").

I suspect many of the negative attitudes about "management" come from
experience with bad managers. Please consider not all (so-called?) managers
are equal, and put away that broad brush with which you are painting.


MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T15:02:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3lku7l2s6s749@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca> <b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Hugh Candlin <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:b5l_9.23732$zF6.1696229@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
[...]
> I disagree.  They get the IT department that is the sum of the ability
> and passion of the department's personnel.  If the majority are in IT
> solely for the money, and lack the passion to do a good job and to
> make sure that the systems anticipate all (or most) of the potential
problems,
> then more management or better management or closer management
> isn't going to change the inbred characteristics of the individuals.

Forgiving the apparent personification of 'systems,' would you
clarify how "the department's personnel" may "anticipate all (or
most) of the potential problems." It appears that potential
problems of the unexpected variety are, by definition,
unanticipated; and, that potential problems of the unknown
variety cannot be anticipated!
```

#### ↳ Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T08:51:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1dv1n$8fp$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca>`

```
In article <3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca>,
Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>Last month itbusiness.ca had an article about top management losing
>confidence in their IT department.  This month there is a survey that
>shows IT having little faith in top management.  And somewhere I read
>that IT should be focusing on the bottom line and adding business
>value.  In short many organizations remain dysfunctional.

'Dysfunctional' is a curious term; given that a business exists in order 
to make money then it can be concluded that as long as the business is 
making money it is 'functional'.

[snip]

>The job of top management is to set the goals, define the culture, and
>both live and communicate those things.

Textbooks say that the job of Management is something like 'the 
responsibility for allocation, co-ordination and motivation of personnel 
and corporate resources in order to realise a stated Executive goal'... 
but what do those textbooks know, anyhow?

>There must be the willingness
>to hear and deal with bad news.  The story was told a couple of years
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>believing the lower levels should correct the problem without his help. 

The story is told to this day about a guy with a hook for a hand who 
stalks cars on Lover's Lane.

[snip]

>At higher levels, it is not up to IT to
>determine whether it is more important to get inventory turnaround
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>be combined and the roles that other people will play in them.

DP/IT/IS/MIS can and should do a variety of things... and as long as folks 
are permitted to refuse the results with 'I know that's what I told you 
but it's not what I want' then not much is going to change.

>
>A second thing that must be a top management decision is that
>documentation is important and requires people who have writing skills.

[snip]

>Programmers should not be expected to be writers.

Mr Morris, perhaps this bit of musing you've posted needs a bit more 
thinking-through.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ [OT] Nothing to do with (Re: Is IT failure a symptom of general failure?)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-01-31T12:00:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3la8qineevu3d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E39B5A6.837526A7@istar.ca> <b1dv1n$8fp$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b1dv1n$8fp$1@panix1.panix.com...
[...]
> The story is told to this day about a guy with a hook for a hand who
> stalks cars on Lover's Lane.

Late one moon-lit evening I found one of the cars the guy
caught. The body and interior were ripped to shreds by that
hook. The horror of it all!

I heard a noise and voices and slid into the shadows, hoping
not to be discovered.

Silently, I watched as a couple approached.

It was his cousin, the guy with a hooker on his arm, and
the whore of it all!
```
